幽明录 - Records of the Hidden and the Visible

    幽明录（南朝宋）刘义庆 撰

    　　●幽明录

    　　庙方四丈，不作墉壁。道广五尺，夹树兰香。斋者煮以沐浴，然后亲祭，所谓“浴兰汤”。
    　　海中有金台，出水百丈，结构巧丽，穷尽神工，横光岩渚，竦曜星汉。台内有金几，雕文备置，上有百味之食，四大力神常立守护。有一五通仙人来，欲甘膳，四神排击，延而退。
    　　邺城凤阳门五层楼，去地二十丈，长四十丈，广二十丈，安金凤皇二头于其上。石季龙将衰，一头飞入漳河，清朗见在水底；一头今犹存
    　　始兴县有皋天子国。因山崎岖，十有余里，坑堑数重，阡陌交通。城内堂基碎瓦，柱穿犹存。东有皋天子冢。皋天子，未之闻也。
    　　始兴县有睪天子城，城东有冢。昔有发之者，垂陷，而冢里有角声震于外，惧而塞之。 [1]

    Relevant Explanation

    Explanation

    [1]

    Idiomatic Expressions

    • 庙方四丈，不作墉壁 - Describes the temple's structure, emphasizing its grandeur and simplicity. '庙方四丈' means the temple is four zhang (a traditional Chinese unit of length) on each side, and '不作墉壁' means there are no walls or enclosures.
    • 道广五尺，夹树兰香 - Describes the path leading to the temple, which is five chi wide (another traditional Chinese unit of length) and lined with fragrant orchids.
    • 斋者煮以沐浴，然后亲祭，所谓‘浴兰汤’ - Explains the ritual of preparing for the ceremony, where the participants bathe in a mixture of water and orchids, known as 'Lan Tang' (Orchid Bath).
    • 海中有金台，出水百丈，结构巧丽，穷尽神工，横光岩渚，竦曜星汉 - Describes a golden platform in the sea that rises a hundred zhang above the water, with intricate and beautiful architecture, and radiates light across the rocks and stars.
    • 台内有金几，雕文备置，上有百味之食，四大力神常立守护 - Details the interior of the golden platform, which has a golden table with elaborate carvings, placed with a variety of foods, and guarded by four powerful gods.
    • 有一五通仙人来，欲甘膳，四神排击，延而退 - Tells of a five-way immortal who tried to take the food, but was repelled by the four gods.
    • 邺城凤阳门五层楼，去地二十丈，长四十丈，广二十丈，安金凤皇二头于其上 - Describes a five-story tower in the city of Yecheng, which is twenty zhang high, forty zhang long, and twenty zhang wide, with two golden phoenixes on top.
    • 石季龙将衰，一头飞入漳河，清朗见在水底；一头今犹存 - States that as Shi Jilong's power waned, one of the phoenixes flew into the Zhang River and can still be seen at the bottom of the water, while the other remains on the tower.
    • 始兴县有皋天子国。因山崎岖，十有余里，坑堑数重，阡陌交通 - Describes a kingdom of the Gao Emperor in Shixing County, which is rugged and mountainous, spanning over ten li (a traditional Chinese unit of distance), with multiple trenches and intersecting paths.
    • 城内堂基碎瓦，柱穿犹存 - Mentions the ruins of the city, with broken tiles and columns still standing.
    • 东有皋天子冢 - Refers to the tomb of the Gao Emperor located to the east.
    • 皋天子，未之闻也 - Indicates that the Gao Emperor is not well-known.
    • 始兴县有睪天子城，城东有冢 - Describes a city of the Ge Emperor in Shixing County, with a tomb to the east of the city.
    • 昔有发之者，垂陷，而冢里有角声震于外，惧而塞之 - Tells of someone who tried to open the tomb, but it nearly collapsed, and a sound of horns was heard from inside, causing fear and the tomb being sealed.

    Cultural and Historical Allusions

    • 庙 - Refers to a religious or ancestral temple.
    • 墉壁 - Refers to walls or enclosures.
    • 斋者 - Refers to those who are fasting or preparing for a ritual.
    • 浴兰汤 - A traditional Chinese ritual bath using orchids, symbolizing purification.
    • 金台 - A golden platform, often associated with divine or mythical places.
    • 五通仙人 - A five-way immortal, a type of deity in Chinese mythology.
    • 四大力神 - Four powerful gods, often associated with protection and guardianship.
    • 邺城 - The city of Yecheng, an important historical site in China.
    • 凤阳门 - The Phoenix Gate, a significant gate in Yecheng.
    • 石季龙 - Shi Jilong, a historical figure in the Jin Dynasty.
    • 漳河 - The Zhang River, a river in China.
    • 始兴县 - Shixing County, a location in China.
    • 皋天子 - The Gao Emperor, a historical or legendary figure.
    • 睪天子 - The Ge Emperor, another historical or legendary figure.

    Proper Nouns

    • 庙 - Temple.
    • 墉壁 - Walls or enclosures.
    • 斋者 - Those who are fasting or preparing for a ritual.
    • 浴兰汤 - Orchid Bath.
    • 金台 - Golden Platform.
    • 五通仙人 - Five-way Immortal.

    幽明录（南朝宋）刘义庆 撰

    幽明录

    The temple measures four zhang on each side and has no walls or enclosures. The path leading to it is five chi wide and lined with fragrant orchids. Those preparing for the ritual bathe in a mixture of water and orchids, then proceed to the ceremony, which is known as the "Orchid Bath."

    In the sea, there is a golden platform that rises a hundred zhang above the water, with intricate and beautiful architecture, showcasing the finest craftsmanship. It radiates light across the rocks and stars. Inside the platform, there is a golden table with elaborate carvings, placed with a variety of foods, and guarded by four powerful gods. One day, a five-way immortal came to take the food, but was repelled by the four gods and retreated.

    In Yecheng, the Phoenix Gate has a five-story tower that is twenty zhang high, forty zhang long, and twenty zhang wide, with two golden phoenixes on top. As Shi Jilong's power waned, one of the phoenixes flew into the Zhang River and can still be seen at the bottom of the water, while the other remains on the tower.

    In Shixing County, there is a kingdom of the Gao Emperor, which is rugged and mountainous, spanning over ten li, with multiple trenches and intersecting paths. The ruins of the city have broken tiles and columns still standing. To the east, there is the tomb of the Gao Emperor. The Gao Emperor is not well-known.

    In Shixing County, there is also a city of the Ge Emperor, with a tomb to the east of the city. Once, someone attempted to open the tomb, but it nearly collapsed, and a sound of horns was heard from inside, causing fear and the tomb being sealed. [1]

    始兴灵水，源有汤泉。每至霜雪，见其上烝气高数十丈，生物投之，须臾便熟。泉中常有细赤鱼出游，莫有获者。
    　　艾县辅山有温冷二泉，同出一山之足。两泉发源，相去数尺。热泉可煮鸡豚，冰泉常若冰生。双流数丈而合，俱会于一溪。
    　　襄邑县南濑乡，老子之旧乡也。有老子庙，庙中有九井，能洁斋入祠者，水温清随人意念。
    　　始安熙平县东南有山，山西其形长狭，水从下注塘，一日再减盈缩，因名为“朝夕塘”。
    　　耒阳县东北有芦塘，淹地八顷，其深不可测。中有大鱼，常至五日一跃奋出水，大可三围，其状异常。每跃出水，则小鱼奔迸，随水上岸，不可胜计。
    　　宜都建平二郡之界，有五六峰，参差互出。上有倚石，如二人像，攘袂相对。俗谓二郡督邮争界于此。
    　　武昌阳新县北山上有望夫石，状若人立。相传：昔有贞妇，其夫从役，远赴国难，妇携弱子，饯送此山，立望夫而化为立石，因以为名焉。 [2]

    Relevant Explanation

    Explanation

    [2]

    Idiomatic Expressions

    • 始興靈水，源有湯泉 - Descriptive phrase setting the scene.
    • 見其上烝氣高數十丈 - Steam rises tens of feet high; '烝氣' specifically refers to steam or vapor.
    • 生物投之，須臾便熟 - Idiom describing the heat of the spring; living things cook quickly.
    • 泉中常有細赤魚出游，莫有獲者 - Descriptive phrase with a hint of mystery; tiny red fish swim in the spring but are not caught.
    • 艾縣輔山有溫冷二泉，同出一山之足 - Descriptive phrase highlighting the unique feature of the springs.
    • 兩泉發源，相去數尺 - Precise measurement; two springs originate a few feet apart.
    • 熱泉可煮雞豚，冰泉常若冰生 - Descriptive phrase emphasizing temperature differences; hot spring can cook chickens and pigs, cold spring is always like ice.
    • 雙流數丈而合，俱會於一溪 - Descriptive phrase describing the flow of the springs.
    • 襄邑縣南瀨鄉，老子之舊鄉也 - Historical reference to the birthplace of Laozi.
    • 有老子廟，廟中有九井，能潔齋入祠者，水溫清隨人意念 - Descriptive phrase highlighting the spiritual significance of the temple.
    • 始安熙平縣東南有山，山西其形長狹，水從下注塘，一日再減盈縮，因名為‘朝夕塘’ - Descriptive phrase explaining the naming of the pond.
    • 耒陽縣東北有 - Incomplete phrase, context needed for full understanding.

    Here is the final translation of the source text:

    In Shixing, there is a spring called Ling Water, which has a hot spring at its source. Whenever frost or snow comes, the steam rises tens of feet high. If you throw living things into it, they will cook in a moment. There are often tiny red fish swimming in the spring, but no one catches them.

    In Aixian, Fushan has two hot and cold springs, both originating from the base of the same mountain. The two springs originate from sources a few feet apart. The hot spring can cook chickens and pigs, and the cold spring is always like ice. The two streams flow for several meters before merging into one stream.

    In Xiangyi County's Nanxie Township, this is the old hometown of Laozi. There is a Laozi temple, and within the temple are nine wells. Those who enter the temple after purifying themselves will find the water warm and clear according to their thoughts.

    In Shian Xiping County in the southeast, there is a mountain. The western side of the mountain is long and narrow, and water flows down into a pond, which rises and falls twice a day, hence it is called "Chaoxi Pond."

    In Leiyang County's northeast, there is a reed pond, covering eight hectares, and its depth is immeasurable. There are large fish in the pond that leap out of the water every five days, reaching a circumference of three zhang, and their appearance is extraordinary. Each time they leap out of the water, small fish scatter and follow the water onto the shore, too many to count.

    Between Yidu and Jianping counties, there are five or six peaks, alternating in height. On top of these peaks, there are leaning stones that resemble two people facing each other with their sleeves raised. Locals say that the magistrates of the two counties disputed the boundary here.

    On the northern mountain of Wuchang Yangxin County, there is a stone called the "Wife-Watching Stone," which looks like a person standing. It is said that once there was a chaste woman whose husband went off to serve in the military, far away to fight a national crisis. The woman accompanied her weak child to this mountain to see him off. She stood there watching for her husband, and later turned into a standing stone, which is why it is named as such. [2]

    巴丘县自金冈以上二十里，名黄金潭，莫测其深；上有濑，亦名黄金濑。古有钓于此潭，获一金锁，引之，遂满一船。有金牛出，声貌莽壮。钓人被骇，牛因奋勇跃而还潭，锁乃将尽，钓人以刀斫得数尺。潭、濑因此取名。
    　　淮南牛渚津水极深，无可算计，人见一金牛，形甚瑰壮，以金为锁绊。
    　　庐山自南行十余里，有鸡山，山有石鸡，冠距如生。道士李镇于此下住，常宝玩之。鸡一日忽摧毁，镇告人曰：“鸡忽如此，吾其终乎？”因与知故诀别，后月余遂卒。
    　　三峰最为竦桀，自非清霁素朝，不可望见。峰下有泉，飞流如舒一匹绢，分映青林，直注山下。虽纤罗不动，其上翛翛，恒凄清风也。
    　　宫亭湖边傍山间，有石数枚，形圆若镜，明可以鉴人，谓之石镜。后有行人过，以火燎一枚，至不复明，其人眼乃失明。 [3]

    Bachu County, located 20 li above Jin Gang Peak, is home to a lake named Golden Lake, whose depth cannot be measured. Above the lake lies a rapid, also named Golden Rapid. In ancient times, a fisherman who fished in this lake caught a golden lock, which, when pulled, filled an entire boat. Suddenly, a golden ox emerged, with a fierce and imposing appearance. The fisherman was terrified, and the ox, with great effort, leapt back into the lake. The lock was almost completely pulled out, and the fisherman cut off several feet with a knife. The lake and the rapid were thus named.

    The waters of the Niu Zhi ferry in Huainan are extremely deep, beyond calculation. A person once saw a golden ox there, whose form was very magnificent and imposing, with a golden chain as a tether.

    South of Mount Lu, after traveling more than ten li, there is a mountain called Chicken Mountain. On the mountain, there is a stone chicken with a comb and spurs like those of a living chicken. A Taoist priest named Li Zhen lived below this mountain and often treasured and played with the stone chicken. One day, the stone chicken suddenly collapsed. Li Zhen told his friends and acquaintances, "The stone chicken has suddenly collapsed; I am about to die." He then said his goodbyes and died a month later.

    The three peaks of Mount Lu are the most towering and majestic, and they cannot be seen unless it is a clear and bright morning. Below the peaks, there is a spring whose waterfall flows like a piece of silk, divided and reflected in the green forest, flowing directly down to the foot of the mountain. Even if the fine silk is not moving, it is very light and airy above, with a constant cool and refreshing breeze.

    Between the Gongting Lake and the mountains, there are several stones shaped round like mirrors, bright enough to reflect people, called stone mirrors. Later, a passerby burned one of these stones with fire, and it became no longer bright. The person's eyes then became blind. [3]

    山阴县九侯神山上有灵坛，坛前有古井，常无水，及请告神，水即涌出，供用足，乃复渐止。
    　　谯县城东，因城为台，方二十丈，高八尺，一曰：古之葬也，魏武帝即筑以为台，东面墙崩，金玉流出，取者多死，因复筑之。
    　　乐安县故市经荒乱，人民饿死，枯骸填地。每至天阴将雨，辄闻吟啸呻叹声聒于耳。
    　　平都县南陂上有冢，行人于陂取得鲤，道逢冢中人来云：“何敢取吾鱼？”夺着车上而去。
    　　广陵有冢，相传是汉江都王建之墓也。常有村人行过，见地有数十具磨，取一具持归。暮即叩门求磨甚急，明旦送着故处。
    　　广陵露白村人，每夜辄见鬼怪，咸有异形丑恶。怯弱者莫敢过。村人怪如此，疑必有故，相率得十人，一时发掘，入地尺许，得一朽烂方相头。访之故老，咸云：“尝有人冒雨送葬，至此遇劫，一时散走，方相头陷没泥中。” [4]

    Relevant Explanation

    Explanation

    [4]

    Idiomatic Expressions

    • 為之一空 - Set phrase meaning completely emptied; fields were left barren.
    • 請告神 - Formal way of saying 'praying to the god'.
    • 因城為台 - Descriptive phrase indicating the construction method.
    • 博學求之 - Seeking knowledge broadly.
    • 聒於耳 - Emphasizes the loud and persistent nature of the sounds.
    • 道逢 - Encountered on the road.
    • 相傳 - It is said.

    Cultural and Historical Allusions

    • 山陰縣 - Shanyin County, a historical place in present-day Shaoxing, Zhejiang Province.
    • 九侯神山 - A sacred mountain, possibly referring to a local deity or a legendary site.
    • 魏武帝 - Cao Cao, a powerful warlord and statesman during the late Eastern Han Dynasty.
    • 江都王建 - Liu Jian, a prince of the Han Dynasty, who was buried in Guangling.
    • 廣陵 - Guangling, an ancient name for Yangzhou, a city in Jiangsu Province.
    • 樂安縣 - Le'an County.
    • 平都縣 - Pingdu County.
    • 露白村 - Luobai Village.

    Proper Nouns

    • 山陰縣 - Shanyin County
    • 九侯神山 - Sacred Mountain of the Nine Lords
    • 魏武帝 - Cao Cao
    • 江都王建 - Liu Jian
    • 廣陵 - Guangling
    • 樂安縣 - Le'an County
    • 平都縣 - Pingdu County
    • 露白村 - Luobai Village

    In Shanyin County, on the sacred mountain of the Nine Lords, there is a divine altar. In front of the altar stands an ancient well, which usually has no water. When the people pray to the god, water immediately gushes out, sufficient for their needs, and then gradually recedes.

    In Qiao City, to the east of the city, a platform was built using the city wall as its base. The platform measures twenty zhang in length and eight chi in height. According to one account, during ancient burials, Emperor Wei Wu (Cao Cao) constructed this platform. One day, the eastern wall of the platform collapsed, and gold and jade flowed out. Those who took the treasure often died, so the platform was rebuilt.

    In Le'an County, the old market area had suffered from chaos and famine, resulting in many people starving to death, leaving their dried bones scattered across the ground. Whenever it was about to rain, one could hear the sounds of moaning and sighing, filling the ears.

    In Pingdu County, there is a tomb on the southern pond. A traveler caught a carp in the pond and encountered someone from the tomb who said, "How dare you take my fish?" The person took the fish and placed it on their cart, then left.

    In Guangling, there is a tomb said to be that of King Jiangdu of Han, Liu Jian. Villagers often saw dozens of stone millstones on the ground. One day, a villager took one of the millstones home. That night, someone urgently knocked on the door, demanding the return of the millstone. The next morning, the villager returned the millstone to its original place.

    In Luobai Village of Guangling, every night, people would see ghosts and apparitions with strange and ugly forms. The weak dared not pass through. The villagers were puzzled by this and suspected there must be a reason. They gathered ten people and dug together. After digging about a foot into the ground, they found a rotten and decayed mask. They consulted the elders, who said, "Once, someone was caught in the rain while escorting a funeral procession. They were attacked here, and everyone scattered, leaving the mask buried in the mud." [4]

    硕县下有眩潭，以视之眩人眼，因以为名。旁有田陂，昔有人船行过此陂，见一死蛟在陂上不得下。无何，见一人，长壮乌衣，立于岸侧，语行人云：“吾昨下陂，不过而死，可为报眩潭。”行人曰：“眩潭无人，云何可报？”乌衣人云：但至潭，便大言之。”行人如其旨，须臾，潭中有号泣声。
    　　东莱人性灵，作酒多醇，浊而更清，二人曰以是醇□。
    　　楚文王少时好猎，有一人献一鹰，文王见之，爪距神爽，殊绝常鹰。故为猎于云梦，置网云布，烟烧张天，毛群羽族，争噬竞搏。此鹰轩颈瞪目，无搏噬之志。王曰：“吾鹰所获以百数，汝鹰曾无奋意，将欺余耶？”献者曰：“若效于雉兔，臣岂敢献？”俄而，云际有一物凝翔，鲜白不辨其形，鹰便竦翮而升，矗若飞电。须臾，羽堕如雪，血下如雨，有大鸟堕地，度其两翅，广数十里，众莫能识。时有博物君子曰：“此大鹏雏也。”文王乃厚赏之。 [5]

    Relevant Explanation

    Explanation

    [5]

    Idiomatic Expressions

    • 眩潭 - A pool with a confusing name, possibly a local legend or myth.
    • 大鹏 - A mythical bird in Chinese mythology, often associated with the giant Peng bird from the *Zhuangzi*.

    Cultural and Historical Allusions

    • 硕县 - A place name, possibly a county in ancient China.
    • 东莱 - A region in ancient China, known for its spiritual sensitivity.
    • 楚文王 - King Wen of Chu, a historical figure from the Chu state during the Spring and Autumn period.
    • 云梦 - A vast area in the Huai River basin, known for its rich wildlife and hunting grounds.
    • 博物君子 - A knowledgeable person, often referring to scholars or experts.

    Proper Nouns

    • 眩潭 - A pool with a confusing name.
    • 东莱 - A region in ancient China.
    • 楚文王 - King Wen of Chu.
    • 云梦 - A vast area in the Huai River basin.
    • 大鹏 - A mythical bird in Chinese mythology.
    • 博物君子 - A knowledgeable person.

    Below the county of Shuo lies the Confusing Pool, which is named for its ability to bewilder the eyes. Nearby is a field or pond. Once, a traveler passed by this pond in a boat and saw a dead dragon on the surface that could not descend. Soon after, he saw a tall, strong man in black standing by the shore, who said to the traveler, "I died yesterday when I tried to descend the pond. You can report this to the Confusing Pool." The traveler replied, "The Confusing Pool has no one, how can I report to it?" The man in black said, "Just go to the pool and shout loudly." The traveler followed his instructions, and soon, the pool emitted a cry.

    The people of Donglai are spiritually sensitive, and they make wine that becomes clearer despite being initially turbid. Two people said it is thus pure.

    King Wen of Chu, when he was young, enjoyed hunting. One day, someone presented him with an eagle. The king saw it and was impressed by its sharp claws and extraordinary spirit, noting that it was unlike any other eagle. He decided to hunt in the Yundong area, setting up nets like clouds and smoke filling the sky. The animals fought fiercely. However, this eagle stood with its head held high and eyes wide, showing no intention to attack. The king said, "My eagles have captured hundreds of prey, but your eagle shows no enthusiasm. Are you deceiving me?" The presenter replied, "If it were suitable for hunting small game like pheasants and rabbits, I would not have dared to present it." Soon, a large, white object appeared in the sky, and the eagle suddenly ascended, rising like a streak of lightning. In a moment, feathers fell like snow, and blood rained down, and a large bird fell to the ground. Measuring its wings, they were several miles wide, and no one could recognize it. A knowledgeable person said, "This is a young giant Peng." The king then generously rewarded the presenter. [5]

    汉武帝常微行过人家，家有婢，国色，帝悦之，因留宿，夜与婢□。有书生亦家宿，善天文。忽见客星移掩帝座甚逼，书生大惊跃，连呼咄咄，不觉声高。乃见一男子，操刀将欲入户，闻书生声急，谓为己故，遂蹙缩走，客星应时即退。帝闻其声，异而召问之，书生具说所见，乃悟曰：“此人是婢婿，将欲肆其凶于朕。”乃召羽林，语主人曰：“朕，天子也。”于是擒奴伏诛，厚赐书生。
    　　汉武见物如牛肝，入地不动，问东方朔，朔曰：“此积愁之气，惟酒可以忘愁，今即以酒灌之，即消。”
    　　汉武帝在甘泉宫，有玉女降，常与帝围棋相娱。女风姿端正，帝密悦，乃欲逼之。女因唾帝面而去，遂病疮经年。故《汉书》云：“避暑甘泉宫，正其时也。” [6]

    Relevant Explanation

    Explanation

    [6]

    Idiomatic Expressions

    • 微行 - Emperor's incognito travels to avoid formalities.
    • 国色 - Euphemism for exceptional beauty, often applied to women.
    • 留宿 - To stay overnight.
    • 夜与婢□ - Refers to a sexual encounter; '夜' means 'at night,' and '与' means 'with.'
    • 书生亦家宿 - '书生' refers to a scholar or student, and '家宿' means to stay at home.
    • 善天文 - Knowledge of astronomy, highly valued in ancient China.
    • 客星移掩帝座甚逼 - '客星' refers to a temporary star, '移掩帝座' means it moved to cover the emperor's seat, and '甚逼' indicates it was very close.
    • 书生大惊跃 - To be greatly surprised and jump.
    • 连呼咄咄 - Interjection expressing surprise or warning.
    • 不觉声高 - Unconsciously, the voice became loud.
    • 有书生亦家宿 - '书生' refers to a scholar or student, and '家宿' means to stay at home.
    • 操刀将欲入户 - '操刀' means 'holding a knife,' and '将欲入户' means 'about to enter the house.'
    • 闻书生声急 - '闻' means 'to hear,' and '声急' means 'the voice was urgent.'
    • 谓为己故 - '谓为' means 'thought,' and '己故' means 'for my own sake.'
    • 蹙缩走 - To shrink back and run away.
    • 客星应时即退 - '应时' means 'at the right time,' and '即退' means 'immediately retreated.'
    • 帝闻其声，异而召问之 - '异' means 'strange,' and '召问之' means 'called him to ask.'
    • 书生具说所见 - '具说' means 'to fully explain,' and '所见' means 'what he saw.'
    • 乃悟曰 - '乃悟' means 'then realized,' and '曰' means 'said.'
    • 此人是婢婿 - '此人' means 'this person,' and '婢婿' means 'the husband of the maid.'
    • 将欲肆其凶于朕 - '将欲' means 'about to,' '肆其凶' means 'unleash his evil,' and '于朕' means 'on me.'
    • 召羽林 - '召' means 'to summon,' and '羽林' refers to the imperial guards.
    • 语主人曰 - '语' means 'to tell,' and '主人' means 'the host.'
    • 朕，天子也 - '朕' is the first-person pronoun used by the emperor, and '天子' means 'Son of Heaven' or 'emperor.'
    • 擒奴伏诛 - '擒奴' means 'capture the slave,' and '伏诛' means 'to be executed.'
    • 厚赐书生 - '厚赐' means 'to give a generous reward,' and '书生' means 'the scholar.'
    • 汉武见物如牛肝 - '见物' means 'saw something,' and '如牛肝' means 'like a cow's liver.'
    • 入地不动 - '入地' means 'sink into the ground,' and '不动' means 'not moving.'
    • 问东方朔 - '问' means 'to ask,' and '东方朔' is a famous scholar and court jester during the Han dynasty.
    • 惟酒可以忘愁 - '惟' means 'only,' '酒' means 'wine,' and '忘愁' means 'forget sorrow.'
    • 以酒灌之，即消 - '以酒灌之' means 'to pour wine over it,' and '即消' means 'immediately disappeared.'
    • 汉武帝在甘泉宫 - '甘泉宫' is a palace in the Han dynasty.
    • 有玉女降 - '玉女' means 'a beautiful woman,' and '降' means 'to descend' or 'to appear.'
    • 常与帝围棋相娱 - '常' means 'often,' '围棋' means 'Go' (a board game), and '相娱' means 'to entertain each other.'
    • 女风姿端正 - '风姿' means 'graceful appearance,' and '端正' means 'upright and proper.'
    • 帝密悦 - '密' means 'secretly,' and '悦' means 'pleased.'
    • 乃欲逼之 - '乃' means 'then,' '欲' means 'want,' and '逼之' means 'to force her.'
    • 女因唾帝面而去 - '因' means 'because,' '唾' means 'spit,' '面' means 'face,' and '而去' means 'and left.'
    • 遂病疮经年 - '遂' means 'then,' '病疮' means 'developed sores,' and '经年' means 'for a year.'
    • 避暑甘泉宫，正其时也 - '避暑' means 'to escape the heat,' '甘泉宫' is the palace, and '正其时也' means 'it was the right time.'

    Cultural and Historical Allusions

    • 汉武帝 - Emperor Wu of Han, one of the most powerful and influential emperors of the Han dynasty.
    • 微行 - Incognito travels, often used by emperors to avoid the formalities of official visits.
    • 国色 - Term used to describe exceptional beauty, often applied to women.
    • 书生 - Scholar or student, often used to describe someone with knowledge and education.
    • 善天文 - Knowledge of astronomy, which was highly valued in ancient China.
    • 客星 - Temporary star, often associated with significant events or omens.
    • 帝座 - Emperor's seat, symbolizing the emperor's position and power.
    • 羽林 - Imperial guards, responsible for protecting the emperor.
    • 东方朔 - Famous scholar and court jester during the Han dynasty, known for his wit and wisdom.
    • 甘泉宫 - Palace in the Han dynasty, often used for official and ceremonial purposes.
    • 玉女 - Beautiful woman, often used in legends and stories to describe a goddess or a celestial being.
    • 围棋 - Board game Go, highly regarded in ancient China and often played by scholars and nobles.

    Emperor Wu of Han often traveled incognito and passed by a household. There was a maid in the house who was exceptionally beautiful, and the emperor was pleased with her. He stayed overnight and had a tryst with the maid. A scholar was also staying at the same household and was knowledgeable about astronomy. Suddenly, the scholar saw a temporary star moving to cover the emperor's seat, which was very close. The scholar was greatly surprised and jumped, calling out in surprise. Then he saw a man holding a knife about to enter the house. When the man heard the scholar's urgent voice, he thought it was for his own sake and ran away, causing the temporary star to immediately retreat. The emperor heard the scholar's voice, found it strange, and called him to ask. The scholar fully explained what he saw, and the emperor realized, "This person is the maid's husband, who was about to unleash his evil on me." The emperor then summoned the imperial guards and told the host, "I am the Son of Heaven." The slave was captured and executed, and the scholar was generously rewarded.

    Emperor Wu saw something like a cow's liver that sank into the ground and did not move. He asked Dongfang Shuo, who said, "This is the accumulated sorrow, only wine can forget sorrow. Now pour wine over it, and it will disappear."

    Emperor Wu of Han was at the Palace of Ganquan when a beautiful woman descended. She often played Go with the emperor for entertainment. The woman had a graceful and upright appearance, and the emperor secretly admired her. He wanted to force her, but the woman spat in his face and left. As a result, the emperor developed sores and suffered for a year. Therefore, the Book of Han states, "To escape the heat at the Palace of Ganquan, it was the right time." [6]

    甘泉王母降。
    　　汉武帝与群臣宴于未央，方啖黍臛，忽闻人语云：“老臣冒死自诉。”不见其形，寻觅良久，梁上见一老翁长八九寸，面目赪皱，须发皓白，拄杖偻步，笃老之极。帝问曰：“叟姓字何？居在何处？何所病苦，而来诉朕？”翁缘柱而下，放杖稽首，默而不言。因仰头视屋，俯指帝脚，忽然不见。帝骇愕不知何等，乃曰：“东方朔必识之。”于是召方朔以告，朔曰：“其名为‘藻兼’，水木之精也。夏巢幽林，冬潜深河。陛下顷日频兴造宫室，斩伐其居，故来诉耳。仰头看屋，而复俯指陛下脚者，足也。愿陛下宫室足于此也。”帝感之。既而息役。幸瓠子河，闻水底有弦歌之声，前梁上翁及年少数人，绛衣素带，缨佩甚鲜，皆长八九寸，有一人，长尺余，凌波而出，衣不沾濡，或有挟乐器者。帝方食，为之辍膳，命列坐于食案前。 [7]

    Relevant Explanation

    Explanation

    [7]

    Idiomatic Expressions

    • 甘泉王母降 - Classical expression meaning 'the Queen Mother of the West descended at Ganquan.'
    • 未央 - Refers to Weiyang Palace, a significant imperial palace in Chang'an during the Han dynasty.
    • 黍臛 - A dish made of millet and meat, often served as a delicacy.
    • 老臣冒死自诉 - A formal expression meaning 'an old subject risks his life to appeal.'
    • 面目赪皱 - Describes a face that is red and wrinkled, indicating old age and possibly illness.
    • 须发皓白 - Describes white beard and hair, further emphasizing the old age of the character.
    • 拄杖偻步 - Describes the act of walking with a cane, bent over, indicating extreme oldness and frailty.
    • 笃老之极 - Means 'extremely old and frail,' reinforcing the description of the old man.
    • 叟姓字何？居在何处？何所病苦，而来诉朕？ - A series of questions asking for the name, residence, and reason for the old man's plea.
    • 缘柱而下 - Means 'climbing down the pillar,' describing the old man's movement.
    • 放杖稽首 - Means 'putting down the cane and bowing deeply,' a gesture of deep respect or submission.
    • 默而不言 - Means 'silent and not speaking,' indicating the old man's initial silence.
    • 仰头视屋，俯指帝脚 - Describes the old man looking up at the ceiling and then pointing down at the emperor's feet.
    • 忽然不见 - Means 'suddenly disappeared,' indicating the sudden vanishing of the old man.
    • 骇愕不知何等 - Means 'shocked and unable to understand what it was,' describing the emperor's reaction.
    • 东方朔必识之 - Means 'Dongfang Shuo must know it,' indicating the emperor's belief that Dongfang Shuo would recognize the old man.
    • 其名为‘藻兼’，水木之精也 - Means 'his name is 'Zaojian,' a spirit of water and wood,' identifying the old man as a spirit.
    • 夏巢幽林，冬潜深河 - Describes the spirit's habitat, living in deep forests in summer and deep rivers in winter.
    • 陛下顷日频兴造宫室，斩伐其居 - Means 'Your Majesty recently frequently built palaces, cutting down their homes,' explaining the reason for the old man's plea.
    • 愿陛下宫室足于此也 - Means 'I hope Your Majesty's palaces are sufficient here,' expressing the spirit's wish for the emperor to stop building.
    • 瓠子河 - Refers to the Huizi River, a river in ancient China.
    • 水底有弦歌之声 - Means 'there was a sound of stringed music and singing under the water,' describing the mysterious music heard.
    • 前梁上翁及年少数人 - Means 'the old man on the beam and several young people,' describing the appearance of the spirits.
    • 绛衣素带，缨佩甚鲜 - Describes the attire of the young people, with red clothes, white belts, and bright decorations.
    • 皆长八九寸，有一人，长尺余 - Describes the size of the spirits, with most being about 8-9 inches tall and one being over a foot tall.
    • 凌波而出，衣不沾濡 - Means 'emerging from the water, their clothes not wet,' describing the spirits' appearance.
    • 或有挟乐器者 - Means 'some were holding musical instruments,' indicating the presence of instruments.
    • 为之辍膳 - Means 'caused him to stop eating,' describing the emperor's reaction to the music.
    • 命列坐于食案前 - Means 'ordered them to sit in a row in front of the dining table,' describing the emperor's command.

    Cultural and Historical Allusions

    • 甘泉 - Refers to a palace or temple in Chang'an, likely a significant location during the Han dynasty.
    • 未央 - Refers to Weiyang Palace, a major imperial palace in Chang'an.
    • 黍臛 - A traditional dish made of millet and meat, often served as a delicacy.
    • 王母 - The Queen Mother of the West, a figure in Chinese mythology known for her immortality and supernatural powers.
    • 东方朔 - A famous scholar and court jester during the Han dynasty, known for his wit and wisdom.
    • 瓠子河 - The Huizi River, a river in ancient China, often associated with historical and mythological events.
    • 弦歌之声 - Refers to the sound of stringed instruments and singing, often associated with musical performances or spiritual activities.

    Proper Nouns

    • 甘泉 - A palace or temple in Chang'an.
    • 未央 - Weiyang Palace, a major imperial palace in Chang'an.
    • 黍臛 - A traditional dish made of millet and meat.
    • 王母 - The Queen Mother of the West, a figure in Chinese mythology.
    • 东方朔 - A famous scholar and court jester during the Han dynasty.
    • 瓠子河 - The Huizi River, a river in ancient China.
    • 弦歌之声 - The sound of stringed instruments and singing.

    The Queen Mother of the West descended at Ganquan.

    During the reign of Emperor Wu of Han, he was feasting with his ministers at Weiyang Palace, enjoying a dish of millet and meat, when suddenly he heard a voice say, "An old subject risks his life to appeal." He could not see the form of the speaker. After searching for a long time, he saw an old man on the beam, about eight to nine inches tall, with a red, wrinkled face, white beard and hair, leaning on a cane and walking hunched over, extremely old and frail. The emperor asked, "What is your name and surname? Where do you live? What suffering have you endured, and why are you appealing to me?" The old man climbed down the pillar, put down his cane, bowed deeply, and remained silent. He then looked up at the ceiling and pointed down at the emperor's feet, and suddenly disappeared. The emperor was shocked and did not know what it was, and said, "Dongfang Shuo must know it." He then summoned Dongfang Shuo and told him about it. Dongfang Shuo said, "His name is 'Zaojian,' a spirit of water and wood. In summer, he nests in deep forests, and in winter, he hides in deep rivers. Your Majesty has recently frequently built palaces, cutting down their homes, so he has come to appeal. He looked up at the ceiling and then pointed down at Your Majesty's feet, indicating that he hopes Your Majesty's palaces are sufficient here." The emperor was moved by this. Soon after, he stopped the construction. Later, when he visited the Huizi River, he heard the sound of stringed music and singing from under the water. He saw the old man on the beam and several young people, all dressed in red clothes, white belts, and bright decorations, all about eight to nine inches tall, with one person over a foot tall, emerging from the water, their clothes not wet, some holding musical instruments. The emperor was eating, and it caused him to stop eating. He ordered them to sit in a row in front of the dining table. [7]

    帝问曰：“闻水底奏乐，为是君耶？”老翁对曰：“老臣前昧死归诉，幸蒙陛下天地之施，即息斧斤，得全其居，不胜欢喜，故私相庆乐耳！”帝曰：“可得奏乐否？”曰：“故赍乐来，安敢不奏？”其最长人便治弦而歌，歌曰：“天地德兮垂至仁，愍幽魄兮停斧斤。保窟宅兮庇微身，愿天子兮寿万春！”歌声小大无异于人，清彻绕越梁栋。又二人鸣管抚节调契声谐。帝欢悦，举觞并劝曰：“不德不足当雅贶。”老翁等并起拜爵，各饮数升不醉。献帝一紫螺壳，中有物状如牛脂。 [8]

    Relevant Explanation

    Explanation

    [8]

    Idiomatic Expressions

    • 聞水底奏樂，為是君耶 - Rhetorical question meaning 'Did you hear music playing underwater? Is it you, Your Majesty?'
    • 老臣前昧死歸訴 - Formal expression meaning 'Your humble servant previously risked his life to return and plead.'
    • 幸蒙陛下天地之施 - Means 'fortunate to receive Your Majesty's boundless grace.'
    • 得全其居 - Means 'to preserve our homes.'
    • 故私相慶樂耳 - Means 'so we privately celebrate in joy.'
    • 可得奏樂否 - Question meaning 'May we play music?'
    • 故赍乐来 - Means 'We have brought the music with us.'
    • 治弦而歌 - Means 'to tune the strings and sing.'
    • 天地德兮垂至仁 - Poetic phrase meaning 'The virtue of heaven and earth, bestowing utmost benevolence.'
    • 愍幽魄兮停斧斤 - Means 'Pitying the souls of the deep, stopping the axes.'
    • 保窟宅兮庇微身 - Means 'Protecting our homes, sheltering our humble selves.'
    • 愿天子兮寿万春 - Means 'May Your Majesty live for ten thousand springs.'
    • 歌声小大无异于人 - Means 'The voice is neither small nor large, just like a person's.'
    • 清彻绕越梁栋 - Means 'The sound is clear and resonates through the beams and pillars.'
    • 又二人鸣管抚节调契声谐 - Means 'And two others play pipes and strike the rhythm, their sounds harmonizing.'
    • 不德不足当雅贶 - Means 'Your Majesty's virtue is too great for me to accept such a gracious gift.'
    • 老翁等并起拜爵 - Means 'The old men all rise to toast.'
    • 各饮数升不醉 - Means 'Each drank several sheng without getting drunk.'
    • 献帝一紫螺壳 - Means 'Presented to the Emperor a purple conch shell.'
    • 中有物状如牛脂 - Means 'Inside is something shaped like cow fat.'

    Cultural and Historical Allusions

    • 帝 - Refers to the Emperor, likely the Han Emperor in this context.
    • 老翁 - Refers to an elderly man, likely a representative of the underwater inhabitants.
    • 紫螺壳 - A purple conch shell, often associated with mystical or divine gifts in Chinese literature.
    • 天地德 - Refers to the virtue of heaven and earth, a common theme in Chinese philosophy and literature.
    • 斧斤 - Refers to axes, symbolizing destruction or harm.
    • 窟宅 - Refers to homes or dwellings, often used metaphorically for one's place in life.
    • 微身 - Refers to oneself in a humble or modest way.
    • 万春 - A poetic way of saying 'forever' or 'for a very long time.'
    • 清彻绕越梁栋 - Describes the clarity and resonance of the music, often used to praise the beauty of music in Chinese literature.
    • 鸣管抚节 - Refers to playing pipes and striking the rhythm, common musical instruments in ancient China.
    • 不醉 - Refers to not getting drunk, often used to describe the virtue of moderation.
    • 紫螺壳 - A purple conch shell, often associated with mystical or divine gifts in Chinese literature.
    • 牛脂 - Refers to cow fat, often used metaphorically for something valuable or precious.

    Proper Nouns

    • 帝 - The Emperor, likely the Han Emperor in this context.
    • 老翁 - An elderly man, likely a representative of the underwater inhabitants.
    • 紫螺壳 - A purple conch shell.
    • 天地德 - The virtue of heaven and earth.
    • 斧斤 - Axes.
    • 窟宅 - Homes or dwellings.
    • 微身 - Oneself in a humble or modest way.
    • 万春 - Forever or for a very long time.
    • 清彻绕越梁栋 - The clarity and resonance of the music.
    • 鸣管抚节 - Playing pipes and striking the rhythm.
    • 不醉 - Not getting drunk.
    • 紫螺壳 - A purple conch shell.
    • 牛脂 - Cow fat.

    The Emperor asked, "Have you heard music playing underwater? Is it you, Your Majesty?" The old man replied, "Your humble servant previously risked his life to return and plead. Fortunate to receive Your Majesty's boundless grace, the axes were stopped, and we were able to preserve our homes. We are overjoyed, so we privately celebrate in joy!" The Emperor said, "May we play music?" The old man replied, "We have brought the music with us. How dare we not perform?" The tallest of them tuned the strings and began to sing, singing, "The virtue of heaven and earth, bestowing utmost benevolence. Pitying the souls of the deep, stopping the axes. Protecting our homes, sheltering our humble selves. May Your Majesty live for ten thousand springs!" The voice was neither small nor large, just like a person's, and the sound was clear and resonated through the beams and pillars. Two others played pipes and struck the rhythm, their sounds harmonizing. The Emperor was delighted and raised his cup, saying, "Your Majesty's virtue is too great for me to accept such a gracious gift." The old men all rose to toast, each drinking several sheng without getting drunk. They presented the Emperor with a purple conch shell, inside of which was something shaped like cow fat. [8]

    帝问曰：“朕暗，无以识此物。”曰：“东方生知之耳！”帝曰：“可更以珍异见贻。”老翁顾命，取洞穴之宝。一人受命，下没渊底，倏忽还到，得一大珠，径数寸，明耀绝世，帝甚爱玩。翁等忽然而隐。帝问朔：“紫螺壳中何物？”朔曰：“是蛟龙髓，以傅面，令人好颜色；又女子在孕，产之必易。”会后宫难产者，试之，殊有神效。帝以脂涂面，便悦泽。又曰：“何以此珠名洞穴珠？”朔曰：“河底有一穴，深数百丈，中有赤蚌，蚌生珠，故以名焉。”帝既深叹此事，又服朔之奇识。
    　　汉武帝以玄豹白凤膏磨青锡屑，以酥油和之为灯，虽雨中灯不灭。
    　　董仲舒尝下帷独咏，忽有客来，风姿音气，殊为不凡，与论《五经》，究其微奥。仲舒素不闻有此人而疑其非常。客又曰：“欲雨。”因此戏之曰：“巢居知风，穴居知雨。卿非狐狸，即是鼷鼠！”客闻此言，色动形坏，化成老狸，蹶然而走。 [9]

    Relevant Explanation

    Explanation

    [9]

    Idiomatic Expressions

    • 朕暗，无以识此物 - A formal expression of ignorance, used by the emperor to indicate his lack of knowledge about an object.
    • 是蛟龙髓，以傅面，令人好颜色 - A description of a mythical substance believed to improve complexion when applied to the face.
    • 巢居知风，穴居知雨 - A proverbial saying indicating that those who live in nests can sense the wind, and those who live in caves can sense the rain.

    Cultural and Historical Allusions

    • 帝 - Refers to the emperor, likely Emperor Wu of Han.
    • 东方生 - A historical figure known for his knowledge and wisdom.
    • 董仲舒 - A prominent Confucian scholar during the Han dynasty.
    • 《五经》 - The Five Classics of Confucianism: the Book of Songs, Book of Documents, Book of Changes, Book of Rites, and Spring and Autumn Annals.

    Proper Nouns

    • 东方生 - A historical figure known for his knowledge and wisdom.
    • 董仲舒 - A prominent Confucian scholar during the Han dynasty.
    • 《五经》 - The Five Classics of Confucianism: the Book of Songs, Book of Documents, Book of Changes, Book of Rites, and Spring and Autumn Annals.

    The emperor asked, "I am ignorant; I cannot recognize this object." Someone replied, "Only Dongfang Sheng knows about it!" The emperor said, "You may bring more rare and precious items." The old man ordered someone to retrieve treasures from a cave. One person took the order, dived into the depths of the cave, returned quickly, and brought a large pearl, several inches in diameter, which was exceptionally bright and dazzling. The emperor greatly valued it. The old men suddenly disappeared. The emperor asked Dongfang Sheng, "What is inside the purple snail shell?" Dongfang Sheng said, "It is dragon marrow. When applied to the face, it improves one's complexion; it also makes childbirth easier for pregnant women." When a difficult childbirth occurred in the imperial harem, it was tested, and it proved to be very effective. The emperor applied the substance to his face and found it rejuvenating. He then asked, "Why is this pearl called the cave pearl?" Dongfang Sheng said, "At the bottom of the river, there is a cave, hundreds of feet deep, containing red clams. The clams produce pearls, hence the name." The emperor was deeply impressed by the event and Dongfang Sheng's extraordinary knowledge. Emperor Wu of Han used a mixture of black panther and white phoenix oil, ground with green tin powder and mixed with clarified butter to create a lamp that remained lit even in the rain. Dong Zhongshu was studying alone when a distinguished guest arrived, with an extraordinary presence and voice. They discussed the Five Classics, delving into their subtle meanings. Dong Zhongshu had never heard of this person before and was suspicious of his unusual nature. The guest then said, "It will rain." Dong Zhongshu playfully challenged him, saying, "Those who live in nests know the wind, those who live in caves know the rain. You are not a fox, then you must be a mouse!" The guest, upon hearing these words, his face changed and he transformed into an old fox, fleeing suddenly. [9]

    文翁常欲断大树，砍断处去地一丈八尺，翁先祝曰：“吾若得二千石，斧当着此处。”因掷之，中所砍一丈八尺处。后果为郡。
    　　长安有张氏者，昼独处室，有鸠自入，止于对床。张恶之，披怀祝曰：“鸠，尔来为我祸耶止承尘，为我福耶入我怀。”鸠翻飞入怀，以手探之，不知所在，而得一金带钩焉。遂宝之。自是之后，子孙昌盛。
    　　汉何比干梦有贵客，车骑满门，觉，以语妻子。未已，门首有老姥，年可八十余，求避雨，雨甚盛而衣不沾濡。比干延入，礼待之，乃曰：“君先出自后稷，佐尧，至晋有阴功，今天赐君策。”如简，长九寸，凡九百九十枚以授之，曰：“子孙能佩者富贵。”言讫出门，不复见。 [10]

    Relevant Explanation

    Explanation

    [10]

    Idiomatic Expressions

    • 文翁常欲断大树 - Wen翁often wanted to cut down a large tree.
    • 砍断处去地一丈八尺 - The place where it was cut was one zhang and eight chi from the ground.
    • 翁先祝曰 - The old man first made a wish.
    • 吾若得二千石，斧当着此处 - If I become a high-ranking official (二千石), the axe will land here.
    • 因掷之，中所砍一丈八尺处 - Then he threw it. It hit the place where it was cut one zhang and eight chi from the ground.
    • 后果为郡 - Later, he indeed became a governor.
    • 长安有张氏者，昼独处室，有鸠自入，止于对床 - In Chang'an, there was a family named Zhang. During the day, he was alone in his room. A magpie flew in on its own. It stopped on the opposite bed.
    • 张恶之，披怀祝曰 - Zhang disliked it. He opened his chest and made a wish.
    • 鸠，尔来为我祸耶 - Magpie, did you come to bring me misfortune?
    • 止承尘，为我福耶 - Stay on the ceiling, to bring me good fortune?
    • 鸠翻飞入怀，以手探之，不知所在，而得一金带钩焉 - The magpie flew back and forth and entered his chest. He reached out with his hand. He did not know where it was, but found a golden belt hook.
    • 遂宝之，自是之后，子孙昌盛 - He then treasured it. From then on, his descendants flourished.
    • 汉何比干梦有贵客，车骑满门 - Han He Bǐgān dreamed of a noble guest. With chariots and horses filling the door.
    • 觉，以语妻子 - When he woke up, he told his wife and children.
    • 未已，门首有老姥 - Before long, an old woman appeared at the door.
    • 年可八十余，求避雨 - Who was around eighty years old. Asked to take shelter from the rain.
    • 雨甚盛而衣不沾濡 - The rain was very heavy, but her clothes were not wet.
    • 比干延入，礼待之 - Bǐgān invited her in and treated her with courtesy.
    • 乃曰，君先出自后稷 - She then said. You are descended from Houji.
    • 佐尧，至晋有阴功 - Assisted Yao and had hidden merits up to the Jin dynasty.
    • 今天赐君策，如简，长九寸 - Today, heaven grants you a strategy. Like a bamboo slip, nine cun long.
    • 凡九百九十枚以授之 - A total of nine hundred and ninety slips were given to him.
    • 子孙能佩者富贵 - Those who can wear them will become wealthy and noble.
    • 言讫出门，不复见 - After finishing her words, she left, and was never seen again.

    Cultural and Historical Allusions

    • 文翁 - A historical figure known for his contributions to education and governance in ancient China.
    • 二千石 - An ancient Chinese title for a high-ranking official, often used to denote a governor or a senior administrative position.
    • 长安 - The ancient capital of China, now known as Xi'an.
    • 汉何比干 - A historical figure from the Han dynasty, known for his wisdom and integrity.
    • 后稷 - An ancient Chinese agricultural deity and ancestor of the Zhou dynasty.
    • 尧 - A legendary sage king of ancient China, known for his virtue and wisdom.
    • 晋 - A historical dynasty in China, known for its cultural and political achievements.

    Wen翁 often wanted to cut down a large tree, and the place where it was cut was one zhang and eight chi from the ground. The old man first made a wish, saying, "If I become a high-ranking official, the axe will land here." Then he threw it, and it hit the place where it was cut one zhang and eight chi from the ground. Later, he indeed became a governor.

    In Chang'an, there was a family named Zhang. During the day, he was alone in his room when a magpie flew in on its own and stopped on the opposite bed. Zhang disliked it and opened his chest, making a wish: "Magpie, did you come to bring me misfortune? Stay on the ceiling, to bring me good fortune?" The magpie flew back and forth and entered his chest. He reached out with his hand, not knowing where it was, but found a golden belt hook. He then treasured it. From then on, his descendants flourished.

    Han He Bǐgān dreamed of a noble guest, with chariots and horses filling the door. When he woke up, he told his wife and children. Before long, an old woman appeared at the door, who was around eighty years old, asking to take shelter from the rain. The rain was very heavy, but her clothes were not wet. Bǐgān invited her in and treated her with courtesy. She then said, "You are descended from Houji, who assisted Yao and had hidden merits up to the Jin dynasty. Today, heaven grants you a strategy." She gave him a bamboo slip, nine cun long, a total of nine hundred and ninety slips, and said, "Those who can wear them will become wealthy and noble." After finishing her words, she left, and was never seen again. [10]

    汉建武元年，东莱人姓乜，家尝作酒卢，入内政见三奇客，共持曲饭至抒其酒饮，异以饭曲代处，而三鬼相与醉于林中。
    　　汉明帝永平五年，剡县刘晨、阮肇共入天台山取谷皮，迷不得返，经十三日，粮食乏尽，饥馁殆死。遥望山上有一桃树，大有子实，而绝岩邃涧，永无登路。攀援藤葛，乃得至上。各啖数枚，而饥止体充。复下山，持杯取水，欲盥漱，见芜菁叶从山腹流出，甚鲜新，复一杯流出，有胡麻饭糁，相谓曰：“此知去人径不远。”便共没水，逆流二三里，得度山出一大溪，溪边有二女子，姿质妙绝，见二人持杯出，便笑曰：“刘、阮二郎，捉向所失流杯来。”晨、肇既不识之，缘二女便呼其姓，如似有旧，乃相见忻喜。问：“来何晚邪？”因邀还家。其家筒瓦屋，南壁及东壁下各有一大床，皆施绛罗帐，帐角悬铃，金银交错。 [11]

    Relevant Explanation

    Explanation

    [11]

    Idiomatic Expressions

    • 漢建武元年 - Refers to the first year of the Jianwu era, the reign title of Emperor Guangwu of Han.
    • 東萊人姓乜 - An individual from Donglai with the surname '乜,' a rare or obscure surname.
    • 家嘗作酒盧 - Means 'his family often made a wine vat,' where '酒盧' likely refers to a wine vat or brewing vessel.
    • 入內政見三奇客 - Means 'he entered the house and saw three strange guests,' where '奇客' could mean 'strange guests' or 'unusual visitors.'
    • 共持曲飯至抒其酒飲 - Means 'together they brought the wine and drink it,' where '曲飯' refers to the ingredients for brewing, and '抒其酒飲' means 'to pour out the wine to drink.'
    • 異以飯曲代處 - Means 'he replaced the wine with the brewing ingredients,' where '異以' means 'to replace.'
    • 而三鬼相與醉於林中 - Means 'and the three ghosts drank together in the forest,' where '相與' means 'together' or 'mutually.'
    • 漢明帝永平五年 - Refers to the fifth year of the Yongping era, the reign title of Emperor Ming of Han.
    • 剡縣劉晨、阮肇共入天台山取谷皮 - Means 'Liu Chen and Yuan Zao from Yandian entered Tian Tai Mountain to collect grain husks,' where '谷皮' refers to the husks of grain.
    • 迷不得返 - Means 'they became lost and could not return.'
    • 經十三日 - Means 'after thirteen days.'
    • 糧食乏盡 - Means 'their food supplies ran out.'
    • 饑餒殆死 - Means 'they were starving and on the verge of death.'
    • 遙望山上有一桃樹，大有子實 - Means 'they saw a peach tree on the mountain, with many fruits.'
    • 而絕岩邃涧 - Means 'but it was a sheer cliff and deep ravine.'
    • 永無登路 - Means 'there was no way to climb up.'
    • 攀援藤葛 - Means 'they climbed up using vines and creepers.'
    • 乃得至上 - Means 'and finally reached the top.'
    • 各啖數枚 - Means 'each ate several peaches.'
    • 而饑止體充 - Means 'and their hunger was satisfied and their bodies were full.'
    • 復下山，持杯取水 - Means 'they then went down the mountain and carried cups to fetch water.'
    • 欲盥漱 - Means 'to wash their hands and rinse their mouths.'
    • 見芜菁葉從山腹流出，甚鮮新 - Means 'they saw fresh radish leaves flowing out from the mountain's belly, very fresh and new.'
    • 復一杯流出，有胡麻飯糁 - Means 'another cup flowed out, containing sesame seed porridge.'
    • 相謂曰 - Means 'they said to each other.'
    • 此知去人徑不遠 - Means 'This indicates that the path back to civilization is not far away.'
    • 便共沒水，逆流二三里 - Means 'they then plunged into the water and swam upstream for two or three miles.'
    • 得度山出一大溪 - Means 'they crossed the mountain and emerged at a large stream.'
    • 溪邊有二女子，姿質妙絕 - Means 'there were two women by the stream, with extraordinary beauty.'
    • 見二人持杯出 - Means 'they saw the two men carrying cups emerge.'
    • 便笑曰 - Means 'they laughed and said.'
    • 劉、阮二郎，捉向所失流杯來 - Means 'Liu and Yuan, you two young men, brought back the cups we lost.'
    • 晨、肇既不識之 - Means 'Liu and Yuan did not recognize them.'
    • 緣二女便呼其姓 - Means 'the two women called out their names.'
    • 如似有舊 - Means 'as if they were old acquaintances.'
    • 乃相见忻喜 - Means 'they were then delighted to meet.'
    • 問：來何晚邪 - Means 'They asked, 'Why did you come so late?''
    • 因邀還家 - Means 'they invited them to return home.'
    • 其家筒瓦屋 - Means 'their house was made of bamboo and tiles.'
    • 南壁及東壁下各有一大床 - Means 'there was a large bed under the southern and eastern walls.'
    • 皆施绛罗帐，帐角悬铃，金银交错 - Means 'all were covered with crimson silk canopies, with bells hanging from the corners, and golden and silver decorations interwoven.'

    Cultural and Historical Allusions

    • 漢建武元年 - The first year of the Jianwu era, marking the beginning of Emperor Guangwu's reign.
    • 漢明帝永平五年 - The fifth year of the Yongping era, marking a period in Emperor Ming's reign.
    • 東萊 - A historical region in present-day Shandong Province.
    • 剡縣 - A county in present-day Zhejiang Province.
    • 天台山 - A famous mountain in Zhejiang Province, known for its scenic beauty and historical significance.
    • 谷皮 - The husks of grain, often used as animal feed or for brewing.
    • 胡麻飯糁 - Sesame seed porridge, a traditional Chinese dish.
    • 筒瓦屋 - A type of house made with bamboo and tiles, reflecting traditional Chinese architecture.

    Proper Nouns

    • 漢建武元年 - The first year of the Jianwu era.
    • 漢明帝永平五年 - The fifth year of the Yongping era.
    • 東萊 - A historical region in present-day Shandong Province.
    • 剡縣 - A county in present-day Zhejiang Province.
    • 天台山 - A famous mountain in Zhejiang Province, known for its scenic beauty and historical significance.
    • 谷皮 - The husks of grain, often used as animal feed or for brewing.
    • 胡麻飯糁 - Sesame seed porridge, a traditional Chinese dish.
    • 筒瓦屋 - A type of house made with bamboo and tiles, reflecting traditional Chinese architecture.

    In the first year of Jianwu (25 AD), a man from Donglai with the surname "乜" had a family that often made a wine vat. One day, he entered his house and saw three strange guests. Together, they brought the brewing ingredients and poured out the wine to drink. He replaced the wine with the brewing ingredients, and the three ghosts drank together in the forest.

    In the fifth year of Yongping (62 AD) during the reign of Emperor Ming, Liu Chen and Yuan Zao from Yandian entered Tian Tai Mountain to collect grain husks. They became lost and could not return, spending thirteen days without food. Their supplies ran out, and they were starving and on the verge of death. They saw a peach tree on the mountain, with many fruits, but it was a sheer cliff and deep ravine, with no way to climb up. They climbed up using vines and finally reached the top. Each ate several peaches, and their hunger was satisfied and their bodies were full. They then went down the mountain and carried cups to fetch water, to wash their hands and rinse their mouths. They saw fresh radish leaves flowing out from the mountain's belly, very fresh and new. Another cup flowed out, containing sesame seed porridge. They said to each other, "This indicates that the path back to civilization is not far away." They then plunged into the water and swam upstream for two or three miles, crossing the mountain and emerging at a large stream. There were two women by the stream, with extraordinary beauty. They saw the two men carrying cups emerge and laughed, saying, "Liu and Yuan, you two young men, brought back the cups we lost." Liu and Yuan did not recognize them, but the two women called out their names, as if they were old acquaintances. They were then delighted to meet and asked, "Why did you come so late?" They invited them to return home. Their house was made of bamboo and tiles, with a large bed under the southern and eastern walls, all covered with crimson silk canopies, with bells hanging from the corners, and golden and silver decorations interwoven. [11]

    床头各有十侍婢，敕云：“刘、阮二郎，经涉山岨，向虽得琼实，犹尚虚弊，可速作食。”食胡麻饭、山羊脯、牛肉甚甘美。食毕行酒，有一群女来，各持五三桃子，笑而言：“贺汝婿来。”酒酣作乐，刘、阮忻怖交并。至暮，令各就一帐宿，女往就之，言声清婉，令人忘忧。十日后，欲求还去，女云：“君已来是，宿福所牵，何复欲还邪？”遂停半年。气候草木是春时，百鸟啼鸣，更怀悲思，求归甚苦。女曰：“罪牵君，当可如何？”遂呼前来女子有三四十人，集会奏乐，共送刘、阮，指示还路。既出，亲旧零落，邑屋改异，无复相识。问讯得七世孙，传闻上世入山，迷不得归。至晋太元八年，忽复去，不知何所。 [12]

    Relevant Explanation

    Explanation

    [12]

    Idiomatic Expressions

    • 床頭各有十侍婢 - Literally 'ten maidservants at the head of the bed,' indicating a luxurious or supernatural setting.
    • 敕云 - A formal expression meaning 'the decree says' or 'the command was given,' often used in classical texts to introduce a narrative voice.
    • 雖得瓊實 - Refers to a mythical or magical fruit, often associated with immortality or divine sustenance in Chinese mythology.
    • 猶尚虛弊 - Still weak and debilitated, describing their physical state after the journey.
    • 可速作食 - Quickly prepare food, a command to provide sustenance.
    • 食畢行酒 - After eating, the wine was served, indicating a transition from food to drink.
    • 一羣女來 - A group of women came, likely supernatural beings.
    • 各持五三桃子 - Each holding five or three peaches, indicating a gift or offering.
    • 笑而言 - Laughing and saying, describing the manner in which they spoke.
    • 賀汝婿來 - Congratulating your husband, indicating a joyful greeting.
    • 酒酣作樂 - When the wine was flowing freely, music was played, describing a festive atmosphere.
    • 忻怖交並 - Joy and fear mingled, describing the emotional state of Liu and Ruan.
    • 至暮，令各就一帳宿 - At dusk, they were ordered to each sleep in one tent, indicating a separation or arrangement.
    • 言聲清婉 - Their voices were clear and gentle, describing the quality of their speech.
    • 令人忘憂 - Causing one to forget all worries, indicating a soothing or enchanting effect.
    • 十日后，欲求還去 - Ten days later, they wished to return, indicating a desire to leave.
    • 宿福所牽 - Bound by past merit, indicating that their stay is due to a karmic or fated connection.
    • 何復欲還邪 - Why do you wish to return? a rhetorical question expressing surprise or disappointment.
    • 遂停半年 - They stayed for half a year, indicating a prolonged stay.
    • 氣候草木是春時 - The weather and plants were in spring, describing the environment.
    • 百鳥啼鳴 - A hundred birds were singing, indicating a lively or harmonious atmosphere.
    • 更懷悲思 - They felt even more sorrow, indicating a shift in emotion.
    • 求歸甚苦 - They sought to return with great distress, indicating a strong desire to leave.
    • 罪牽君，當可如何 - Bound by sin, what can be done? a rhetorical question expressing helplessness.
    • 遂呼前來女子有三四十人 - They then called forward thirty or forty women, indicating a gathering.
    • 集會奏樂，共送劉、阮 - Assembled to play music and send off Liu and Ruan, indicating a farewell ceremony.
    • 指示還路 - Showed them the way back, indicating guidance.
    • 既出，親舊零落，邑屋改異 - Upon leaving, their relatives and friends were scattered, and the houses and towns had changed, indicating a sense of time passing and loss.
    • 無復相識 - No one was recognizable, indicating a complete transformation of the world.
    • 問訊得七世孫 - They inquired and found their seventh-generation descendants, indicating a long passage of time.
    • 傳聞上世入山，迷不得歸 - It was said that their ancestors had entered the mountains and become lost, never to return, indicating a legend or myth.
    • 至晉太元八年，忽復去，不知何所 - By the eighth year of Taiyuan in the Jin dynasty, they suddenly left again, and no one knew where they went, indicating a final departure and mystery.

    Cultural and Historical Allusions

    • 劉、阮二郎 - Likely refers to Liu and Ruan, the protagonists of the story, possibly based on historical or legendary figures.
    • 瓊實 - Refers to a mythical or magical fruit, often associated with immortality or divine sustenance in Chinese mythology.
    • 胡麻飯 - Sesame rice, a traditional Chinese dish.
    • 山羊脯 - Dried lamb, a preserved meat product.
    • 牛肉 - Beef, a common meat in Chinese cuisine.
    • 晉太元八年 - Refers to the year 383 AD in the Eastern Jin dynasty, providing a historical context for the story.

    Proper Nouns

    • 劉、阮二郎 - The protagonists of the story.
    • 晉太元八年 - The year 383 AD in the Eastern Jin dynasty.

    At the head of each bed, there were ten maidservants, and the decree was given: "Liu and Ruan, the two young men, have traversed mountains and ravines; though they have obtained the jade fruit, they are still weak and debilitated. Quickly prepare food for them." They were served sesame rice, dried lamb, and beef, which were very delicious. After eating, wine was served, and a group of women came, each holding five or three peaches, and laughing, they said, "We congratulate your husband." When the wine was flowing freely, music was played, and Liu and Ruan felt joy and fear mingled. At dusk, they were ordered to each sleep in one tent, and the women came to them, their voices clear and gentle, causing them to forget all worries. Ten days later, they wished to return, and the women said, "You have come here, bound by past merit; why do you wish to return?" They stayed for half a year. The weather and plants were in spring, and a hundred birds were singing, but they felt even more sorrow and sought to return with great distress. The women said, "Bound by sin, what can be done?" They then called forward thirty or forty women, who assembled to play music and send off Liu and Ruan, showing them the way back. Upon leaving, their relatives and friends were scattered, and the houses and towns had changed; no one was recognizable. They inquired and found their seventh-generation descendants, and it was said that their ancestors had entered the mountains and become lost, never to return. By the eighth year of Taiyuan in the Jin dynasty, they suddenly left again, and no one knew where they went. [12]

    曹娥父溺死，娥见瓜浮，得尸。
    　　汉袁安父亡，母使安以鸡酒诣卜工，问葬地。道逢三书生，问安何之？具以告。书生曰：“吾知好葬地。”安以鸡酒礼之，毕，告安地处云：“当葬此地，世世为贵公。”便与别，数步顾视，皆不见。安疑是神人，因葬其地，遂登司徒，子孙昌盛，曰世五公焉。
    　　陈仲举微时，常行宿主人黄申家。申妇夜产，仲举不知。夜三更，有扣门者，久许闻里有人应云：“门里有贵人，不可前，宜从后门往。”俄闻往者还，门内者问之：“见何儿？名何？当几岁？”还者云：“是男儿，名阿奴，当十五岁。”又问曰：“后当若为死？”答曰：“为人作屋，落地死。”仲举闻此，默志之。后十五年，为豫章太守，遣吏往问昔儿阿奴所在家，云：“助东家作屋，落地而死矣。”仲举后果大贵。 [13]

    Relevant Explanation

    Explanation

    [13]

    Idiomatic Expressions

    • 溺死 - Drowned.
    • 好葬地 - A good burial site.
    • 世世为贵公 - For generations to come, will be noble lords.
    • 默志之 - Memorized it silently.

    Cultural and Historical Allusions

    • 曹娥 - A historical figure known for her filial piety, particularly for finding her father's body.
    • 袁安 - A historical figure who became a high-ranking official and whose descendants were influential for many generations.
    • 司徒 - A high-ranking government position in ancient China, equivalent to a Grand Tutor.
    • 陈仲举 - Another historical figure known for his wisdom and foresight.
    • 豫章太守 - The governor of Yuzhang, an important administrative region in ancient China.

    Proper Nouns

    • 曹娥 - A historical figure.
    • 袁安 - A historical figure.
    • 司徒 - A high-ranking government position.
    • 陈仲举 - A historical figure.
    • 豫章太守 - The governor of Yuzhang.

    Cao E's father drowned, and Cao E saw a melon floating and found his body.
    During the Han dynasty, Yuan An's father died, and his mother sent Yuan An with chicken and wine to a diviner to ask about the burial site. On the way, he met three scholars who asked him where he was going. Yuan An told them in detail. The scholars said, "We know a good burial site." Yuan An offered them chicken and wine as a gift, and after the ceremony, they told him the location, saying, "This is the place where you should bury your father. For generations to come, he will be a noble lord." They then parted ways, and after a few steps, Yuan An looked back and saw no one. Yuan An suspected they were divine beings and buried his father there. He eventually became the Grand Tutor, and his descendants prospered, holding high offices for five generations.
    Tchen Zhongju was in humble times and often traveled and stayed at the home of his host, Huang Shen. Huang Shen's wife gave birth at night, but Tchen Zhongju did not know. At midnight, someone knocked on the door. After a long time, someone inside responded, "There is a noble person inside the door; do not go forward. Go through the back door instead." Soon, the person who went back returned, and the person inside asked, "What child did you see? What is his name? How old is he?" The person replied, "He is a boy named Anu, and he will be fifteen years old." The person inside then asked, "How will he die later?" The person replied, "He will die upon touching the ground while building a house for someone." Tchen Zhongju heard this and memorized it silently. Fifteen years later, he became the governor of Yuzhang and sent officials to inquire about the former child named Anu. The officials reported, "He helped build a house for the neighbor and died upon touching the ground." Tchen Zhongju later became very powerful. [13]

    陇西秦嘉，字士会，俊秀之士。妇曰徐淑，亦以才美流誉。桓帝时，嘉为曹掾赴洛。淑归宁于家，昼卧，流涕覆面，嫂怪问之，云：“适见嘉自说往津乡亭病亡，二客俱留，一客守丧，一客赍书还，日中当至。”举家大惊。书至，事事如梦。
    　　常山张颢为梁相。天新雨后，有鸟如山鹊，飞翔稍下坠地。民争取，即化为一圆石。颢椎破之，得金印，文曰：“忠孝侯印。”颢表上闻，藏之秘府。颢汉灵帝时至太尉。
    　　冯贵，前汉汉桓帝贵人也，美艳绝双。死后卅余年，群贼发其冢，见贵人颜色如故。贼遂竞奸之，斗争相煞而死。
    　　句章人至东野还，暮不至门，见路旁有小屋灯火，因投寄宿。有一小女，不欲与丈夫共处，呼邻家止宿。女自伴夜，共弹琴箜篌。至晓，此人谢去，问其姓字，女不答，弹弦而歌曰：“连绵葛上藤，一援复一絚；欲知我姓名，姓陈名阿登。” [14]

    Relevant Explanation

    Explanation

    [14]

    Idiomatic Expressions

    • 陇西秦嘉，字士会，俊秀之士 - Introduction of a person from Longxi, named Qin Jia, with the courtesy name Shihui, described as an outstanding scholar.
    • 昼卧，流涕覆面 - Describes a daytime nap where the woman weeps, covering her face with tears.
    • 嫂怪问之 - The sister-in-law finds this behavior strange and asks about it.
    • 适见嘉自说往津乡亭病亡 - The woman claims she just saw Qin Jia say he died at a place called Jinxiang Pavilion.
    • 二客俱留，一客守丧，一客赍书还 - Two guests were left behind, one to mourn and the other to bring a message back.
    • 日中当至 - The messenger should arrive by noon.
    • 书至，事事如梦 - When the message arrived, everything matched what she had dreamed.
    • 常山张颢为梁相 - Zhang Hao from Changshan served as the governor of Liang.
    • 天新雨后 - After a recent rain.
    • 有鸟如山鹊，飞翔稍下坠地 - A bird resembling a magpie flew and gradually fell to the ground.
    • 举家大惊 - The entire family was greatly surprised.
    • 颢椎破之，得金印 - Zhang Hao smashed it open and found a golden seal.
    • 文曰：‘忠孝侯印’ - The inscription on the seal read 'Seal of the Marquis of Loyalty and Filial Piety'.
    • 颢表上闻，藏之秘府 - Zhang Hao reported this to the emperor and had the seal stored in a secret treasury.
    • 冯贵，前汉汉桓帝贵人也，美艳绝双 - Feng Gui was a favored concubine of Emperor Huan of Han, known for her extraordinary beauty.
    • 死后卅余年，群贼发其冢 - Thirty years after her death, bandits plundered her tomb.
    • 见贵人颜色如故 - They found her still looking as beautiful as when she was alive.
    • 贼遂竞奸之，斗争相煞而死 - The bandits fought over her, and in the struggle, they all died.
    • 句章人至东野还 - A person from Juzhang returned from the eastern wilds.
    • 见路旁有小屋灯火 - He saw a small house with lights along the way.
    • 呼邻家止宿 - He asked a neighbor to stay the night.
    • 女自伴夜，共弹琴箜篌 - The woman stayed the night and played the qin and konghou.
    • 弹弦而歌曰 - She played the strings and sang:
    • 连绵葛上藤，一援复一絚 - 'The vines on the gourd grow intertwined, one after another.'
    • 欲知我姓名，姓陈名阿登 - 'If you want to know my name, my surname is Chen and my name is A Deng.'

    Cultural and Historical Allusions

    • 陇西 - A region in ancient China.
    • 秦嘉 - A historical figure from Longxi.
    • 士会 - The courtesy name of Qin Jia.
    • 常山 - A region in ancient China.
    • 张颢 - A historical figure from Changshan.
    • 梁相 - Governor of Liang.
    • 汉桓帝 - Emperor Huan of Han.
    • 句章 - A place name in ancient China.
    • 东野 - Eastern wilds, a vague geographical term.

    Proper Nouns

    • 陇西 - A region in ancient China.
    • 秦嘉 - A historical figure from Longxi.
    • 士会 - The courtesy name of Qin Jia.
    • 常山 - A region in ancient China.
    • 张颢 - A historical figure from Changshan.
    • 梁相 - Governor of Liang.
    • 汉桓帝 - Emperor Huan of Han.
    • 句章 - A place name in ancient China.
    • 东野 - Eastern wilds, a vague geographical term.
    • 忠孝侯印 - The name of the seal.
    • 冯贵 - A historical figure and concubine of Emperor Huan of Han.
    • 陈阿登 - The name of the woman the traveler met.

    Qin Jia, a distinguished scholar from Longxi with the courtesy name Shihui, had a wife named Xu Shu, who was also renowned for her talent and beauty. During the reign of Emperor Huan, Qin Jia was appointed as a clerk in the Cao office and traveled to Luoyang. Upon returning home, Xu Shu fell asleep during the day, weeping and covering her face with tears. Her sister-in-law found this strange and asked her about it. Xu Shu replied, "I just saw Qin Jia say he died at Jinxiang Pavilion. Two guests were left behind, one to mourn and the other to bring a message back. He should arrive by noon." The entire family was greatly surprised. When the message arrived, everything matched what she had dreamed.

    Zhang Hao, a governor of Liang from Changshan, encountered a situation after a recent rain. A bird resembling a magpie flew and gradually fell to the ground. The locals gathered to take it, and it transformed into a circular stone. Zhang Hao smashed it open and found a golden seal. The inscription on the seal read "Seal of the Marquis of Loyalty and Filial Piety." Zhang Hao reported this to the emperor and had the seal stored in a secret treasury. During the reign of Emperor Ling, Zhang Hao rose to the position of Grand Commandant.

    Feng Gui, a favored concubine of Emperor Huan of Han, was known for her extraordinary beauty. Thirty years after her death, bandits plundered her tomb and found her still looking as beautiful as when she was alive. The bandits fought over her, and in the struggle, they all died.

    A person from Juzhang returned from the eastern wilds at dusk and did not reach his home. He saw a small house with lights along the way and asked a neighbor to stay the night. There was a young woman who did not want to share the night with him and called another neighbor to stay instead. She stayed the night and played the qin and konghou. In the morning, the traveler thanked her and asked her name. She did not answer but played the strings and sang: "The vines on the gourd grow intertwined, one after another. If you want to know my name, my surname is Chen and my name is A Deng." [14]

    汉时太山黄原，平旦开门，忽有一青犬在门外伏守，备如家养。原绁犬，随邻里猎，日垂夕，见一鹿，便放犬，犬行甚迟，原绝力逐终不及。行数里，至一穴，入百余步，忽有平衢，槐柳列植，行墙回匝。原随犬入门，列房栊户可有数十间，皆女子，姿容妍媚，衣裳鲜丽。或抚琴瑟，或执博棋。至北阁，有三间屋，二人侍直，若有所伺。见原，相视而笑：“此青犬所致妙音婿也！”一人留，一人入阁。须臾，有四婢出，称太真夫人，白黄郎：“有一女年已弱笄，冥数应为君妇。”既暮，引原入内。内有南向堂，堂前有池，池中有台，台四角有径尺穴，穴中有光映帷席。妙音容色婉妙，侍婢亦美。交礼既毕，宴寝如旧。经数日，原欲暂还报家，妙音曰：“人神异道，本非久势。”至明日，解佩分袂，临阶涕泗，后会无期，深加爱敬：“若能相思，至三月旦，可修斋洁。”四婢送出门，半日至家。情念恍惚，每至其期，常见空中有軿车彷佛若飞。 [15]

    Relevant Explanation

    Explanation

    [15]

    Idiomatic Expressions

    • 平旦 - Refers to dawn or early morning.
    • 伏守 - Means 'to crouch and guard' or 'to lie in wait.'
    • 備如家養 - Means 'as if it were a domesticated pet.'
    • 絕力逐 - Means 'to chase with all one's strength.'
    • 弱笄 - Refers to a woman who is nearly of marriageable age (around 15 years old).
    • 冥數 - Refers to destiny or fate.
    • 交禮 - Refers to the formal rites of marriage.
    • 解佩分袂 - Means 'to part ways, often symbolized by untying a belt or removing a garment.'
    • 修齋潔 - Means 'to perform a purification ritual.'
    • 軿車 - Refers to a carriage, often used in a metaphorical sense.

    Cultural and Historical Allusions

    • 漢時 - Refers to the Han Dynasty, a significant period in Chinese history.
    • 太山 - Refers to Mount Tai, one of the Five Great Mountains of China, known for its cultural and religious significance.
    • 黃原 - The name of the protagonist, a man from Mount Tai.
    • 太真夫人 - A title given to a divine or semi-divine female figure, often associated with immortality and supernatural powers.
    • 妙音 - The name of the divine woman, likely a reference to a goddess or a high-ranking deity.
    • 冥數 - The concept of fate or destiny, deeply rooted in Chinese philosophy and religion.
    • 交禮 - The formal rituals of marriage, which include various ceremonies and traditions.
    • 解佩分袂 - A poetic way of describing the act of parting, often used in literature to convey a sense of loss or farewell.
    • 修齋潔 - A purification ritual, often performed to cleanse oneself before important spiritual or religious events.
    • 軿車 - A type of carriage, often associated with nobility or deities.

    Proper Nouns

    • 黃原 - The protagonist, a man from Mount Tai.
    • 太真夫人 - A divine or semi-divine female figure.
    • 妙音 - The divine woman, likely a goddess or high-ranking deity.
    • 太山 - Mount Tai, one of the Five Great Mountains of China.
    • 軿車 - A type of carriage, often associated with nobility or deities.

    During the Han Dynasty, there was a man named Huang Yuan from Mount Tai. One dawn, he opened the door to find a green dog lying in wait outside, as if it were a domesticated pet. Huang Yuan tied up the dog and went hunting with his neighbors. As evening approached, they saw a deer. Huang Yuan released the dog, but the dog moved very slowly, and despite his best efforts, Huang Yuan could not catch up. After walking several miles, they came to a cave. They entered the cave, and after walking over a hundred steps, they suddenly found themselves on a flat road lined with rows of willows and elms, surrounded by a wall. Huang Yuan followed the dog inside, where he saw a row of rooms, perhaps dozens of them, all occupied by women. These women were beautiful and graceful, dressed in bright and lovely clothing. Some were playing the zither and lute, while others were playing board games. They reached a northern pavilion, where there were three rooms, and two attendants were standing guard, as if waiting for something. When they saw Huang Yuan, they looked at each other and smiled, saying, "This green dog has brought you to marry the divine maiden, Mei Yin!" One of them stayed behind, while the other entered the pavilion. Soon, four maids came out, announcing the arrival of the Lady Tai Zhen, who said to Huang Yuan, "There is a maiden who is almost of marriageable age, and her destiny is to be your wife." As night fell, she led Huang Yuan inside. Inside, there was a hall facing south, in front of which was a pond, and in the center of the pond was a platform. At each corner of the platform were holes about a foot in diameter, from which light shone onto the curtains and bedding. Mei Yin was charming and beautiful, and her attendants were also lovely. The wedding rites were completed, and they spent the night together as usual. After several days, Huang Yuan wanted to return home briefly to inform his family. Mei Yin said, "The ways of humans and gods are different, and this is not meant to last long." On the next day, they parted, untying their belts and separating, and they cried as they stood at the steps, knowing they would never meet again. She deeply loved and respected him, saying, "If you miss me, on the third day of the third month, you can perform a purification ritual." The four maids escorted him out, and he returned home in half a day. His thoughts were often in a daze, and whenever the time came, he would see a carriage floating in the air, as if flying. [15]

    汉末大乱，颍川有人将避地他郡。有女七八岁，不能涉远，势不两全。道边有古冢穿败，以绳系女下之。经年余还，于冢寻觅，欲更殡葬。忽见女尚存，父大惊，问女得活意，女云：“冢中有一物，于晨暮徐辄伸头翕气，为试效之，果觉不复饥渴。”家人于冢寻索此物，乃是大龟。
    　　孙钟，吴郡富春人，坚之父也。少时家贫，与母居，至孝笃信，种瓜为业。瓜熟，有三少年容服妍丽，诣钟乞瓜。钟引入庵中，设瓜及饭，礼敬殷勤。三人临去，谓钟曰：“蒙君厚惠，今示子葬地，欲得世世封侯乎？欲为数代天子乎？”钟跪曰：“数代天子，故当所乐。”便为定墓。又曰：“我司命也，君下山，百步勿反顾。”钟下山六十步，回看，并为白鹤飞去。钟遂于此葬母，冢上有气触天。钟后生坚，坚生权，权生亮，亮生休，休生和，和生皓，为晋所伐，降为归命侯。 [16]

    Relevant Explanation

    Explanation

    [16]

    Idiomatic Expressions

    • 汉末大乱 - Literal phrase meaning 'the end of the Han dynasty was in chaos,' indicating a period of social and political turmoil.
    • 颍川有人将避地他郡 - A phrase meaning 'someone from Yingchuan was about to seek refuge in another prefecture.'
    • 不能涉远 - A phrase meaning 'unable to travel far,' referring to the young girl's physical inability to undertake a long journey.
    • 势不两全 - A phrase meaning 'it is impossible to have both,' indicating a situation where one must choose between two options.
    • 古冢穿败 - A phrase meaning 'an ancient tomb had collapsed,' describing the state of the tomb.
    • 绳系女下之 - A phrase meaning 'tie the girl to a rope and lower her down,' describing the action taken to place the girl in the tomb.
    • 经年余还 - A phrase meaning 'after many years, he returned,' indicating a long passage of time.
    • 于冢寻觅 - A phrase meaning 'search the tomb,' describing the action of looking for the girl.
    • 欲更殡葬 - A phrase meaning 'intend to bury her again,' indicating the father's intention to re-bury the girl.
    • 忽见女尚存 - A phrase meaning 'suddenly saw that the girl was still alive,' describing the unexpected discovery.
    • 父大惊 - A phrase meaning 'the father was greatly surprised,' indicating his reaction to the girl's survival.
    • 问女得活意 - A phrase meaning 'ask the girl how she survived,' describing the father's inquiry.
    • 女云 - A phrase meaning 'the girl said,' introducing the girl's response.
    • 冢中有一物 - A phrase meaning 'there is something in the tomb,' describing what the girl found.
    • 晨暮徐辄伸头翕气 - A phrase meaning 'in the morning and evening, it slowly extends its head and breathes,' describing the creature's behavior.
    • 为试效之 - A phrase meaning 'to try it out,' indicating the girl's action.
    • 果觉不复饥渴 - A phrase meaning 'indeed, she no longer felt hungry or thirsty,' describing the result of the girl's action.
    • 家人于冢寻索此物 - A phrase meaning 'the family searched the tomb for this thing,' describing the family's action.
    • 乃是大龟 - A phrase meaning 'it turned out to be a large turtle,' identifying the creature.
    • 孙钟，吴郡富春人，坚之父也 - A phrase meaning 'Sun Zhong, a man from Fuchun in Wu Prefecture, was the father of Sun Jian,' providing biographical information.
    • 少时家贫 - A phrase meaning 'in his youth, he was poor,' describing Sun Zhong's early life.
    • 与母居 - A phrase meaning 'lived with his mother,' indicating his living situation.
    • 至孝笃信 - A phrase meaning 'extremely filial and devout,' describing Sun Zhong's character.
    • 种瓜为业 - A phrase meaning 'grew melons as his livelihood,' describing his occupation.
    • 瓜熟 - A phrase meaning 'when the melons were ripe,' indicating a specific time.
    • 诣钟乞瓜 - A phrase meaning 'visited Sun Zhong to ask for melons,' describing the visitors' action.
    • 钟引入庵中 - A phrase meaning 'Sun Zhong led them into his cottage,' describing the action taken.
    • 设瓜及饭，礼敬殷勤 - A phrase meaning 'set out melons and food, showing great respect and hospitality,' describing the hospitality extended.
    • 三人临去 - A phrase meaning 'as the three people were leaving,' indicating the timing of their departure.
    • 谓钟曰 - A phrase meaning 'told Sun Zhong,' introducing the visitors' words.
    • 蒙君厚惠 - A phrase meaning 'we are deeply grateful for your kindness,' expressing gratitude.
    • 今示子葬地 - A phrase meaning 'now we will show you a good burial site,' indicating the purpose of their visit.
    • 欲得世世封侯乎 - A phrase meaning 'do you want to have descendants who are always ennobled?' asking about a desire for noble lineage.
    • 欲为数代天子乎 - A phrase meaning 'do you want to have descendants who are emperors for several generations?' asking about a desire for imperial lineage.
    • 钟跪曰 - A phrase meaning 'Sun Zhong knelt and said,' introducing his response.
    • 数代天子，故当所乐 - A phrase meaning 'being emperors for several generations, that is what I would like,' expressing his preference.
    • 便为定墓 - A phrase meaning 'they then fixed the location of the tomb,' describing the action taken.
    • 又曰 - A phrase meaning 'they also said,' introducing additional information.
    • 我司命也 - A phrase meaning 'we are the officials of fate,' identifying the visitors.
    • 君下山，百步勿反顾 - A phrase meaning 'you should go down the mountain and do not look back for a hundred steps,' giving a specific instruction.
    • 钟下山六十步 - A phrase meaning 'Sun Zhong went down the mountain sixty steps,' describing his action.
    • 回看，并为白鹤飞去 - A phrase meaning 'when he looked back, they had all turned into white cranes and flown away,' describing the transformation.
    • 钟遂于此葬母 - A phrase meaning 'Sun Zhong then buried his mother at this site,' describing the final action.
    • 冢上有气触天 - A phrase meaning 'the tomb had a vapor reaching the sky,' describing the supernatural phenomenon.
    • 钟后生坚，坚生权，权生亮，亮生休，休生和，和生皓，为晋所伐，降为归命侯 - A phrase meaning 'Sun Zhong later gave birth to Sun Jian, who gave birth to Sun Quan, who gave birth to Sun Liang, who gave birth to Sun Xiu, who gave birth to Sun He, who gave birth to Sun Hao, who was defeated by the Jin dynasty and became the Duke of Surrender,' tracing the lineage and historical events.

    Cultural and Historical Allusions

    • 汉末大乱 - Refers to the end of the Han dynasty, a period marked by political instability and social unrest.
    • 颍川 - Refers to Yingchuan, a historical region in Henan Province, known for its cultural and historical significance.
    • 吴郡富春 - Refers to Fuchun in Wu Prefecture, a region in present-day Zhejiang Province, known for its historical and cultural importance.
    • 孙钟 - Refers to Sun Zhong, a historical figure and the father of Sun Jian, a prominent warlord during the late Eastern Han period.
    • 孙坚 - Refers to Sun Jian, a military leader and warlord during the late Eastern Han period.
    • 孙权 - Refers to Sun Quan, a warlord and founder of the state of Eastern Wu during the Three Kingdoms period.
    • 孙亮 - Refers to Sun Liang, the second emperor of Eastern Wu.
    • 孙休 - Refers to Sun Xiu, the third emperor of Eastern Wu.
    • 孙和 - Refers to Sun He, the fourth emperor of Eastern Wu.
    • 孙皓 - Refers to Sun Hao, the last emperor of Eastern Wu, who was defeated by the Jin dynasty.
    • 晋 - Refers to the Jin dynasty, which succeeded the Three Kingdoms period and unified China.
    • 归命侯 - Refers to a title given to Sun Hao after his defeat by the Jin dynasty, meaning 'Duke of Surrender.'

    At the end of the Han dynasty, when chaos prevailed, there was a man from Yingchuan who was planning to seek refuge in another prefecture. He had a daughter, about seven or eight years old, who could not travel far, and it was impossible to save both of them. By the roadside, there was an ancient tomb that had collapsed, and the man tied the girl to a rope and lowered her down into the tomb. After many years, he returned to the tomb to search for her, intending to bury her again. Suddenly, he saw that the girl was still alive, and he was greatly surprised. He asked the girl how she had survived, and she said, "There was something in the tomb that slowly extended its head and breathed in the morning and evening. I tried it, and indeed, I no longer felt hungry or thirsty." The family searched the tomb for this thing and found it to be a large turtle.

    Sun Zhong was a man from Fuchun in Wu Prefecture, the father of Sun Jian. In his youth, he was poor and lived with his mother. He was extremely filial and devout, and he grew melons as his livelihood. When the melons were ripe, three young men with beautiful attire visited Sun Zhong to ask for melons. Sun Zhong led them into his cottage and set out melons and food, showing great respect and hospitality.
