As the three people were leaving, they told Sun Zhong, "We are deeply grateful for your kindness. Now we will show you a good burial site. Do you want to have descendants who are always ennobled? Or do you want to have descendants who are emperors for several generations?" Sun Zhong knelt and said, "Being emperors for several generations is what I would like." They then fixed the location of the tomb. They also said, "We are the officials of fate. You should go down the mountain and do not look back for a hundred steps." Sun Zhong went down the mountain sixty steps and looked back, and they had all turned into white cranes and flown away. Sun Zhong then buried his mother at this site, and the tomb had a vapor reaching the sky. Sun Zhong later gave birth to Sun Jian, who gave birth to Sun Quan, who gave birth to Sun Liang, who gave birth to Sun Xiu, who gave birth to Sun He, who gave birth to Sun Hao, who was defeated by the Jin dynasty and became the Duke of Surrender. [16]

    董卓信巫，军中常有言祷祀求福。一日，从卓求布，仓卒与新布手巾。又求取笔，便捉以书手巾上。如作两口，一口大，一口小，相累于巾上。授卓曰：“慎此也！”后卓为吕布所杀，后人乃知况吕布也。
    　　魏武帝猜忌晋宣帝子非曹氏纯臣。又尝梦三匹马，在一槽中共食，意尤憎之。因召文、明二帝，告以所见，并云：“防理自多，无为横虑。”帝然之。后果害族移器，悉如梦焉。
    　　钟繇忽不复朝会，意性有异于常。寮友问其故，云：“常有妇人来，美丽非凡。”问者曰：“必是鬼物，可杀之。”后来，止户外曰：“何以有相杀意？”元常曰：“无此。”殷勤呼入，意亦有不忍，乃微伤之。便出去，以新绵拭血，竟路。明日，使人寻迹，至一大冢，棺中一妇人形体如生，白练衫，丹绣裲裆，伤一髀，以裲裆中绵拭血。自此便绝。 [17]

    Relevant Explanation

    Explanation

    [17]

    Idiomatic Expressions

    • 如作两口，一口大，一口小，相累于巾上 - Metaphorical description; requires careful translation to convey the intended meaning.
    • 防理自多，无为横虑 - Advice given by the emperor; requires careful translation to convey the intended meaning.

    Cultural and Historical Allusions

    • 董卓 - Dong Zhuo, a powerful warlord during the late Eastern Han dynasty.
    • 魏武帝 - Emperor Wei Wu, a title often used to refer to Cao Cao, a prominent warlord and statesman.
    • 晋宣帝 - Emperor Jin Xuan, a title often used to refer to Sima Yi, a prominent statesman and general.
    • 文、明二帝 - Emperors Wen and Ming, likely referring to Cao Pi and Cao Rui, sons of Cao Cao.
    • 钟繇 - Zhong Yao, a famous calligrapher and official during the Three Kingdoms period.
    • 鬼物 - Ghosts or supernatural beings, a common theme in Chinese folklore and literature.

    Syntactic Ambiguities

    • 董卓信巫，军中常有言祷祀求福 - Introduces Dong Zhuo's belief in witchcraft and the common practice of prayers and rituals in the army.
    • 魏武帝猜忌晋宣帝子非曹氏纯臣 - Complex sentence requiring careful parsing to convey the suspicion and the historical context.
    • 又尝梦三匹马，在一槽中共食，意尤憎之 - Dream interpretation requiring contextualization to understand its significance.
    • 因召文、明二帝，告以所见，并云：“防理自多，无为横虑。” - Requires careful translation to convey the advice given by the emperor.
    • 帝然之 - Simple statement requiring a straightforward translation.
    • 后果害族移器，悉如梦焉 - Reference to the fulfillment of the dream requiring careful translation to convey the historical context.
    • 钟繇忽不复朝会，意性有异于常 - Description of a change in behavior requiring careful translation to convey the significance.
    • 寮友问其故 - Simple statement requiring a straightforward translation.
    • 云：“常有妇人来，美丽非凡。” - Requires contextualization to understand the significance.
    • 问者曰：“必是鬼物，可杀之。” - Statement requiring a straightforward translation.
    • 后来，止户外曰：“何以有相杀意？” - Dialogue requiring careful translation to convey the interaction.
    • 元常曰：“无此。” - Simple statement requiring a straightforward translation.
    • 殷勤呼入，意亦有不忍，乃微伤之 - Complex sentence requiring careful translation to convey the nuanced interaction.
    • 便出去，以新绵拭血，竟路 - Description of the aftermath requiring careful translation.
    • 明日，使人寻迹，至一大冢，棺中一妇人形体如生，白练衫，丹绣裲裆，伤一髀，以裲裆中绵拭血 - Detailed description requiring careful translation to convey the historical and cultural context.
    • 自此便绝 - Simple statement requiring a straightforward translation.

    Proper Nouns

    • 董卓 - Dong Zhuo
    • 魏武帝 - Emperor Wei Wu (Cao Cao)
    • 晋宣帝 - Emperor Jin Xuan (Sima Yi)
    • 文、明二帝 - Emperors Wen and Ming (Cao Pi and Cao Rui)
    • 钟繇 - Zhong Yao

    Dong Zhuo believed in witchcraft, and in the army, there were often prayers and rituals seeking blessings. One day, he hastily gave a new handkerchief to someone who asked for it. When asked for a pen, he grabbed one and wrote on the handkerchief, as if making two mouths, one large and one small, overlapping on the handkerchief. He handed it to Dong Zhuo and said, "Be cautious about this!" Later, Dong Zhuo was killed by Lü Bu, and it was then known that the handkerchief had been a warning about Lü Bu.

    Emperor Wei Wu suspected that Emperor Jin Xuan's son was not a loyal minister of the Cao family. He also once dreamed of three horses eating together in one trough, which he particularly disliked. He then summoned Emperors Wen and Ming, told them what he had seen, and said, "There are many ways to prevent it, do not worry unnecessarily." The emperor agreed. Later, the consequences were the destruction of the clan and the removal of power, just as in the dream.

    Zhong Yao suddenly stopped attending court meetings, his demeanor was different from usual. His colleagues asked him why, and he said, "A beautiful woman often comes." The questioner said, "It must be a ghost, you can kill it." Later, she stopped at the door and said, "Why do you have a killing intent?" Yuan Chang said, "There is no such thing." He called her in earnestly, but also had不忍, so he slightly injured her. She then left, wiping the blood with new cotton, all the way. The next day, he sent someone to follow the trail, reaching a large tomb, where a woman's body was found, still alive, wearing a white silk robe, a red embroidered vest, with a wound on her thigh, and wiping the blood with the vest. From then on, she disappeared. [17]

    魏齐王芳时，中山有王周南者，为襄邑长。忽有鼠从穴出，语曰：“周南，尔以某日死。”周南不应。至期，更冠帻皂衣而出，曰：“周南，尔以日中死。”亦不应，鼠复入穴。日适中，鼠又冠帻而出，曰：“周南，汝不应，我何道？”言绝，颠蹶而死，即失衣冠所在。就视之，与常鼠无异。
    　　孙权时，南方遣吏献犀簪。吏过宫亭湖庐山君庙请福，神下教求簪，而盛簪器便在神前。吏叩曰：“簪献天子，必乞哀念。”神云：“临入石头，当相还。”吏遂去，达石头，有三尺鲤鱼跳入船，吏破腹得之。 [18]

    Relevant Explanation

    Explanation

    [18]

    Idiomatic Expressions

    • 忽有鼠从穴出 - An idiomatic expression meaning 'suddenly a rat emerged from its hole.'
    • 语曰 - Means 'spoke, saying,' introducing the rat's speech.
    • 周南不应 - Means 'Zhou Nan did not respond.'
    • 更冠帻皂衣而出 - Means 'changed into a hat and black clothes and came out,' indicating a change in attire.
    • 言绝，颠蹶而死 - Means 'when the words ended, it fell over and died,' describing the rat's demise.
    • 就视之 - Means 'to look at it,' indicating an examination.
    • 与常鼠无异 - Means 'there was no difference from an ordinary rat,' emphasizing the rat's mundane nature.
    • 南方遣吏献犀簪 - Means 'officials from the south sent a rhinoceros horn hairpin as tribute,' indicating a diplomatic act.
    • 吏过宫亭湖庐山君庙请福 - Means 'the official passed by the Palace Pavilion Lake and Lushan Lord Temple to seek blessings,' indicating a ritual act.
    • 神下教求簪 - Means 'the deity descended and commanded the seeking of the hairpin,' indicating divine intervention.
    • 盛簪器便在神前 - Means 'the container holding the hairpin was already before the deity,' indicating the hairpin's presence.
    • 吏叩曰 - Means 'the official pleaded, saying,' indicating a request.
    • 簪献天子，必乞哀念 - Means 'the hairpin is offered to the Son of Heaven, so please show mercy,' indicating a plea for compassion.
    • 临入石头，当相还 - Means 'when you reach the city of Shitou, I will return it to you,' indicating a promise.
    • 吏遂去，达石头，有三尺鲤鱼跳入船，吏破腹得之 - Means 'the official then left and reached the city of Shitou, a three-foot-long carp jumped into the boat, the official opened the fish's belly and found it,' describing the official's journey and the miraculous recovery of the hairpin.

    Cultural and Historical Allusions

    • 魏齐王芳时 - Refers to the reign of Cao Fang, the second emperor of the state of Wei during the Three Kingdoms period.
    • 中山有王周南者 - Refers to Wang Zhou Nan, a person from Zhongshan.
    • 襄邑长 - Refers to the position of magistrate in Xiangyi, indicating a local official.
    • 孙权时 - Refers to the reign of Sun Quan, the founder of the state of Eastern Wu during the Three Kingdoms period.
    • 南方遣吏献犀簪 - Indicates a diplomatic act of tribute from the southern region to the central authority.
    • 宫亭湖庐山君庙 - Refers to the Palace Pavilion Lake and Lushan Lord Temple, sacred sites in the region.
    • 孙权 - A historical figure, the founder of the state of Eastern Wu.
    • 石头 - Refers to the city of Shitou, a significant location in the region.

    Proper Nouns

    • 魏齐王芳 - The second emperor of the state of Wei during the Three Kingdoms period.
    • 中山 - A region in ancient China.
    • 王周南 - A person from Zhongshan.
    • 襄邑 - A place name, likely a county or city.
    • 孙权 - The founder of the state of Eastern Wu during the Three Kingdoms period.
    • 南方 - Refers to the southern region.
    • 犀簪 - A rhinoceros horn hairpin, a valuable item.
    • 宫亭湖 - A lake in the region.
    • 庐山君庙 - A temple dedicated to the Lushan Lord.
    • 石头 - A city in the region.
    • 三尺鲤鱼 - A three-foot-long carp, a significant size.

    During the reign of Cao Fang, the second emperor of the state of Wei, there was a man named Wang Zhou Nan from Zhongshan who served as the magistrate of Xiangyi. Suddenly, a rat emerged from its hole and spoke, saying, "Zhou Nan, you will die on a certain day." Zhou Nan did not respond. When the day arrived, he changed into a hat and black clothes and came out, saying, "Zhou Nan, you will die at noon." Again, he did not respond, and the rat returned to its hole. When noon came, the rat emerged again wearing a hat and said, "Zhou Nan, you do not respond, what should I say?" When the words ended, it fell over and died, and the hat and clothes it had worn disappeared. Upon examination, there was no difference from an ordinary rat.

    During the reign of Sun Quan, officials from the south sent a rhinoceros horn hairpin as tribute. The official passed by the Palace Pavilion Lake and Lushan Lord Temple to seek blessings. The deity descended and commanded the seeking of the hairpin, and the container holding the hairpin was already before the deity. The official pleaded, saying, "The hairpin is offered to the Son of Heaven, so please show mercy." The deity said, "When you reach the city of Shitou, I will return it to you." The official then left and reached the city of Shitou, where a three-foot-long carp jumped into the boat. The official opened the fish's belly and found the hairpin inside. [18]

    孙权病，巫启云：“有鬼着绢巾，似是故将相，呵叱初不顾，径进入宫。”其夜，权见鲁肃来，衣巾悉如其言。
    　　吴兴钱乘，孙权时，曾昼卧久，不觉两吻沫出数升。其母怖而呼之，曰：“适见一老公，食以熇筋，恨未尽而呼之。”乘本尪瘠，既尔之后，遂以力闻。官至无难监。
    　　葛祚，吴时衡阳太守，郡境有大槎横水，能为妖怪。百姓为立庙，行旅祷祀，槎乃沉没；不者，槎浮，则船为之破坏。祚将去官，乃大具斤斧，将去民累。明日当至，其夜，闻江中哅哅有人声。往视，槎移去，沿流下数里，驻湾中，自此行者无复沉覆之患。衡阳人为祚立碑曰：正德祈禳，神木为移也。
    　　吴时，有王姥，年九岁病死，自朝至暮复苏。云：见一老妪，挟将飞见北斗君，有狗如狮子大，深目，伏井栏中，云此天公狗也。 [19]

    Relevant Explanation

    Explanation

    [19]

    Idiomatic Expressions

    • 巫启云 - Classical expression meaning 'the witch said' or 'the witch reported,' introducing a supernatural element.
    • 有鬼着绢巾 - Literally 'a ghost wearing a silk scarf,' describing a ghostly figure.
    • 似是故将相 - Means 'seems to be an old general or prime minister,' indicating the ghost's identity.
    • 呵叱初不顾 - Means 'initially did not respond to shouts or commands,' showing the ghost's initial disregard.
    • 径进入宫 - Means 'directly entered the palace,' describing the ghost's action.
    • 衣巾悉如其言 - Means 'the clothes and scarf were exactly as he said,' confirming the description.
    • 吴兴钱乘 - Refers to a person named Qian Cheng from Wu Province.
    • 曾昼卧久 - Means 'once lay awake for a long time during the day,' describing Qian Cheng's state.
    • 不觉两吻沫出数升 - Means 'unconsciously, saliva dripped out of his mouth in several liters,' describing an unusual physical occurrence.
    • 适见一老公 - Means 'just saw an old man,' introducing another supernatural figure.
    • 食以熇筋 - Means 'fed him with roasted tendons,' describing the old man's action.
    • 恨未尽而呼之 - Means 'regretted not finishing and called out to him,' indicating the old man's reaction.
    • 本尪瘠 - Means 'originally weak and emaciated,' describing Qian Cheng's initial condition.
    • 遂以力闻 - Means 'then became known for his strength,' indicating a change in his condition.
    • 无难监 - Refers to a high-ranking official position.
    • 葛祚 - Refers to a person named Ge Zuo.
    • 吴时 - Refers to the time of the Wu Kingdom.
    • 衡阳太守 - Refers to the governor of Hengyang.
    • 郡境有大槎横水 - Means 'in the jurisdiction, a large log was blocking the water,' describing a physical obstacle.
    • 能为妖怪 - Means 'could cause supernatural disturbances,' indicating the log's malevolent nature.
    • 百姓为立庙 - Means 'the people built a temple for it,' describing a religious response.
    • 行旅祷祀 - Means 'travelers prayed and worshipped,' indicating a ritual practice.
    • 槎乃沉没 - Means 'the log then sank,' describing a positive outcome.
    • 不者，槎浮，则船为之破坏 - Means 'if not, the log would float and destroy the boats,' describing a negative outcome.
    • 将去官 - Means 'was about to leave office,' indicating Ge Zuo's departure.
    • 大具斤斧 - Means 'prepared a large number of axes and hammers,' describing his preparation.
    • 将去民累 - Means 'to remove the burden on the people,' indicating his intention.
    • 明曰当至 - Means 'the next day it would arrive,' describing a future event.
    • 闻江中哅哅有人声 - Means 'heard a loud noise of human voices in the river,' describing an auditory phenomenon.
    • 往视，槎移去 - Means 'went to see, and the log had moved away,' describing a change in the situation.
    • 沿流下数里 - Means 'flowed downstream for several miles,' describing the log's movement.
    • 驻湾中 - Means 'stopped in a bay,' describing the log's final resting place.
    • 自此行者无复沉覆之患 - Means 'from then on, travelers no longer suffered from capsizing,' indicating a positive outcome.
    • 衡阳人为祚立碑曰 - Means 'the people of Hengyang erected a stele for him saying,' describing a commemorative action.
    • 正德祈禳，神木为移也 - Means 'upright virtue, prayers, and sacrifices; the divine log was moved,' describing the inscription on the stele.
    • 王姥 - Refers to a woman named Wang Mu.
    • 年九岁病死 - Means 'died at the age of nine from illness,' describing her condition.
    • 自朝至暮复苏 - Means 'revived from morning to evening,' describing her revival.
    • 见一老妪，挟将飞见北斗君 - Means 'saw an old woman, who took her to fly to meet the Lord of the North Star,' describing a supernatural encounter.
    • 有狗如狮子大，深目，伏井栏中 - Means 'there was a dog as big as a lion, with deep-set eyes, crouching in the well enclosure,' describing a supernatural dog.
    • 云此天公狗也 - Means 'said it was the dog of the Heavenly Emperor,' indicating the dog's identity.

    Cultural and Historical Allusions

    • 孙权 - Refers to Sun Quan, the founder of the Wu Kingdom during the Three Kingdoms period.
    • 巫 - Refers to a shaman or witch, a figure in traditional Chinese culture.
    • 鬼 - Refers to a ghost or spirit, a common theme in Chinese folklore.
    • 绢巾 - Refers to a silk scarf, a common accessory in ancient China.
    • 故将相 - Refers to former generals and prime ministers, indicating high-ranking officials.
    • 呵叱 - Refers to shouting or commanding, a common method of communication in ancient times.
    • 宫 - Refers to the palace, the residence of the emperor or a ruler.
    • 吴兴 - Refers to Wu Province, a region in China.
    • 钱乘 - Refers to a person named Qian Cheng.
    • 尪瘠 - Refers to a weak and emaciated condition, a medical term in ancient times.
    • 无难监 - Refers to a high-ranking official position, indicating a significant role.
    • 葛祚 - Refers to a person named Ge Zuo.
    • 衡阳 - Refers to Hengyang, a city in Hunan Province.
    • 槎 - Refers to a log or piece of wood, a common object in ancient times.
    • 妖怪 - Refers to supernatural disturbances, a common theme in Chinese folklore.
    • 庙 - Refers to a temple, a place of worship.
    • 祷祀 - Refers to prayers and sacrifices, common religious practices.
    • 神木 - Refers to a divine log, a supernatural object.
    • 王姥 - Refers to a woman named Wang Mu, a common name in ancient times.
    • 北斗君 - Refers to the Lord of the North Star, a celestial deity in Chinese mythology.
    • 天公狗 - Refers to the dog of the Heavenly Emperor, a supernatural creature.

    Proper Nouns

    • 孙权 - Founder of the Wu Kingdom during the Three Kingdoms period.
    • 巫 - Shaman or witch, a figure in traditional Chinese culture.
    • 鬼 - Ghost or spirit, a common theme in Chinese folklore.
    • 绢巾 - Silk scarf, a common accessory in ancient China.
    • 故将相 - Former generals and prime ministers, indicating high-ranking officials.
    • 呵叱 - Shouting or commanding, a common method of communication in ancient times.
    • 宫 - Palace, the residence of the emperor or a ruler.
    • 吴兴 - Wu Province, a region in China.
    • 钱乘 - Person named Qian Cheng.
    • 尪瘠 - Weak and emaciated condition, a medical term in ancient times.
    • 无难监 - High-ranking official position, indicating a significant role.
    • 葛祚 - Person named Ge Zuo.
    • 衡阳 - Hengyang, a city in Hunan Province.
    • 槎 - Log or piece of wood, a common object in ancient times.
    • 妖怪 - Supernatural disturbances, a common theme in Chinese folklore.
    • 庙 - Temple, a place of worship.
    • 祷祀 - Prayers and sacrifices, common religious practices.
    • 神木 - Divine log, a supernatural object.
    • 王姥 - Woman named Wang Mu, a common name in ancient times.
    • 北斗君 - Lord of the North Star, a celestial deity in Chinese mythology.
    • 天公狗 - Dog of the Heavenly Emperor, a supernatural creature.

    Sun Quan fell ill, and the witch reported: "There is a ghost wearing a silk scarf, who seems to be an old general or prime minister. Initially, he did not respond to shouts or commands, but directly entered the palace." That night, Sun Quan saw Lu Su come, dressed exactly as described.
    During the time of Sun Quan, in Wu Province, there was a man named Qian Cheng. Once, he lay awake for a long time during the day, and unconsciously, saliva dripped out of his mouth in several liters. His mother, terrified, called out to him, saying, "I just saw an old man who fed you with roasted tendons, regretting not finishing and calling out to you." Qian Cheng was originally weak and emaciated, but after this incident, he became known for his strength and reached the position of Wu Nanjian.
    Ge Zuo, during the time of Wu, was the governor of Hengyang. In his jurisdiction, a large log was blocking the water, causing supernatural disturbances. The people built a temple for it, and travelers prayed and worshipped. The log then sank; if not, it would float and destroy the boats. As Ge Zuo was about to leave office, he prepared a large number of axes and hammers to remove the burden on the people. The next day it would arrive, and that night, he heard a loud noise of human voices in the river. He went to see, and the log had moved away, flowing downstream for several miles and stopping in a bay. From then on, travelers no longer suffered from capsizing. The people of Hengyang erected a stele for him saying: "Upright virtue, prayers, and sacrifices; the divine log was moved."
    During the time of Wu, there was a woman named Wang Mu, who died at the age of nine from illness and revived from morning to evening. She said: "I saw an old woman who took me to fly to meet the Lord of the North Star. There was a dog as big as a lion, with deep-set eyes, crouching in the well enclosure, and she said it was the dog of the Heavenly Emperor." [19]

    吴时，陈仙以商贾为事，驱驴行。忽过一空宅，广夏朱门，都不见人，仙牵驴入宿。至夜，闻有语声：“小人无畏，敢见行灾？“便有一径到仙前，叱之曰：“汝敢辄入官舍！”时笼月暧昧，见其面上黡深，目无瞳子，唇褰齿露，手执黄丝。仙即奔走后村，具说事状。父老云：“旧有恶鬼。”明日，看所见屋宅处，并高坟深■〈土遂〉。
    　　吴末，中书郎失其姓名，夜读书。家有重门，忽闻外面门皆开，恐有急诏。户复开，一人有八尺许，乌衣帽，持杖坐床下，与之熟相视，吐舌至膝。于是大怖，裂书为火，至晓鸡鸣，便去。门户闭如故，其人平安。
    　　邓艾庙在京口，上有一草屋。晋安北将军司马恬于病中，梦见一老翁曰：“我邓公，屋舍倾坏，君为治之。”后访之，乃知艾庙，为立瓦屋。隆安中，有人与女子会于神座上，有一蛇来绕之数四匣。女家追寻见之，以酒脯祷祠，然后得解。 [20]

    Relevant Explanation

    Explanation

    [20]

    Idiomatic Expressions

    • 吳時 - Refers to the Wu period, a historical era in China (222–280 CE), specifically during the Three Kingdoms period.
    • 商賈 - Refers to merchants or traders.
    • 驅驢行 - Means 'to drive a donkey on a journey.'
    • 廣夏朱門 - Describes a large house with red doors, symbolizing wealth or grandeur.
    • 行災 - Literally 'to bring disaster,' often used in the context of supernatural or malevolent actions.
    • 黃絲 - Likely refers to a yellow thread or cord, possibly a symbol of authority or punishment.
    • 裂書為火 - Means 'to tear the book and burn it,' indicating an act of desperation or destruction.
    • 高墳深■〈土遂〉 - Describes a high mound and deep pit, likely a grave or burial site.
    • 中書郎 - A title for an official in charge of imperial records and documents.
    • 烏衣帽 - Refers to a black hat, often associated with scholars or officials.
    • 持杖 - Means 'to carry a staff,' often symbolizing authority or a ritual object.
    • 吐舌至膝 - Describes the demon spitting out its tongue to the knee, a terrifying gesture.
    • 隆安中 - Refers to the Yong'an period (397–402 CE) during the Eastern Jin Dynasty.
    • 飯牛牧羊 - Refers to feeding cattle and herding sheep, common rural occupations.
    • 可為師表 - Means 'can serve as a teacher and model,' emphasizing exemplary status.

    Cultural and Historical Allusions

    • 吳 - The Wu Kingdom, one of the Three Kingdoms.
    • 鄧艾 - A general of the Wei Kingdom during the Three Kingdoms period.
    • 晉安北將軍 - A title for a high-ranking military officer during the Jin Dynasty.
    • 隆安 - The Yong'an period (397–402 CE) during the Eastern Jin Dynasty.
    • 京口 - A city in modern-day Zhenjiang, Jiangsu Province.

    Proper Nouns

    • 吳 - The Wu Kingdom, one of the Three Kingdoms.
    • 鄧艾 - A general of the Wei Kingdom during the Three Kingdoms period.
    • 晉安北將軍 - A title for a high-ranking military officer during the Jin Dynasty.
    • 隆安 - The Yong'an period (397–402 CE) during the Eastern Jin Dynasty.
    • 京口 - A city in modern-day Zhenjiang, Jiangsu Province.

    During the Wu period, Chen Xian was a merchant who drove his donkey on a journey. Suddenly, he passed by an empty house with a grand hall and red doors, but there was no one inside. Chen Xian led his donkey into the house to stay for the night. That night, he heard a voice saying, "You have no fear, dare to bring disaster?" Then, a path appeared before Chen Xian, and a demon stepped forward, shouting, "How dare you enter the official residence!" The moonlight was dim, and Chen Xian saw that the demon had deep marks on its face, no pupils in its eyes, its lips pulled back to reveal its teeth, and it held a yellow thread in its hand. Chen Xian immediately ran to the village behind him and recounted what had happened. The elders said, "There used to be an evil spirit here." The next day, when they looked at the place where the house had been, they found a high mound and a deep pit.

    At the end of the Wu period, a central secretariat official, whose name is unknown, was reading at night. His house had multiple gates, and suddenly he heard the outer doors open. He feared there might be an urgent imperial decree. Then the inner door opened, and a man about eight feet tall, wearing a black hat and holding a staff, sat on the bed. He stared at the official intently and spat his tongue to his knees. The official was terrified and tore the book into pieces, burning them. By dawn, when the roosters crowed, the man disappeared. The doors and gates were closed as before, and the official was unharmed.

    The temple of Deng Ai was in Jingkou, with a thatched hut on top. During his illness, General Sima Tian of the Northern Army of Jin had a dream in which an old man said, "I am General Deng, and my house is in disrepair. You should fix it." Later, when he inquired, he learned that it was Deng Ai's temple, and he had a tile-roofed house built. During the Yong'an period, someone met a woman at the altar of a deity. A snake wrapped itself around them several times. The woman's family searched for the snake and found it. They offered wine and meat to pray and sacrifice, and then the situation was resolved. [20]

    有人相羊叔子父墓，有帝王之气，叔子于是乃自掘断墓。后相者又云：“此墓尚当出折臂三公。”祜工骑乘，有一儿五六岁，端明可喜。掘墓之后，儿即亡，羊时为襄阳都督，因盘马落地，遂折臂。于时士林咸叹其忠诚。
    　　汉时，洛下有一洞穴，其深不测。有一妇人欲杀夫，谓夫曰：“未尝见此穴。”夫自逆视之，至穴，妇遂推下，经多时至底。妇于后掷饭物，如欲祭之。此人当时颠坠恍惚，良久乃苏，得饭食之，气力小强。周皇觅路，仍得一穴，便匍匐从就。崎岖反侧，行数十里，穴宽，亦有微明，遂得宽平广远之地。步行百余里，觉所践如尘，而闻糠米香，啖之，芬美过于充饥。即裹以为粮，缘穴行而食此物。既尽，复过如泥者，味似向尘，复赍以去。所历幽远，里数难详，□就明广。食所赍尽，便入一都。 [21]

    Relevant Explanation

    Explanation

    [21]

    Idiomatic Expressions

    • 有人相羊叔子父墓 - 有人 means 'there was someone,' 相 here refers to divination or fortune-telling, 羊叔子 is the name of Yang Shu, a historical figure, and 父墓 means 'his father's tomb.'
    • 帝王之氣 - A term referring to the aura or destiny associated with a future emperor or ruler.
    • 叔子於是乃自掘斷墓 - 乃 means 'then' or 'thereupon,' and 掘斷 means 'to dig up and destroy.'
    • 折臂三公 - 折臂 means 'broken arm,' and 三公 refers to high-ranking officials in the imperial court.
    • 工騎乘 - 工 means skilled, and 騎乘 means riding horses.
    • 端明可喜 - 端明 means upright and bright, and 可喜 means lovable or endearing.
    • 盤馬落地 - 盤馬 means circling the horse, and 落地 means to fall to the ground.
    • 士林咸嘆其忠诚 - 士林 refers to the scholarly community, and 咸嘆 means to universally praise.
    • 洛下有一洞穴 - 洛下 refers to the area around Luoyang, and 洞穴 means cave.
    • 其深不測 - Means 'its depth is immeasurable.'
    • 自逆視之 - 自逆 means to turn around, and 視之 means to look at it.
    • 婦遂推下 - 遂 means 'then' or 'thereupon,' and 推下 means to push down.
    • 周皇觅路 - 周皇 is a term that seems out of place and may be a typo or misinterpretation; it should likely be 周皇觅路 meaning 'searched for a path.'
    • 匍匐从就 - 匍匐 means to crawl, and 从就 means to follow or proceed.
    • 崎岖反侧 - 崎岖 means rugged or uneven, and 反侧 means to turn over or move about.
    • 食所赍尽 - 食 means to eat, 所赍 means what was carried or brought, and 尽 means finished or exhausted.
    • 便入一都 - 便 means then, and 入一都 means entered a city or capital.

    Cultural and Historical Allusions

    • 羊叔子 - Yang Shu, a historical figure known for his loyalty and integrity during the Jin Dynasty.
    • 三公 - High-ranking officials in the imperial court, often including the Chancellor, Grand Tutor, and Grand Guardian.
    • 盤馬落地 - A specific action that led to Yang Shu's son's injury, reflecting the historical narrative.
    • 士林 - The scholarly community, which often praised Yang Shu's actions.
    • 洛下 - The area around Luoyang, an important city in Chinese history.
    • 洞穴 - Caves have often been associated with mystical or supernatural elements in Chinese folklore.

    Proper Nouns

    • 羊叔子 - Yang Shu, a historical figure.
    • 三公 - High-ranking officials in the imperial court.
    • 洛下 - The area around Luoyang.
    • 洞穴 - Cave.

    A diviner once observed Yang Shu's father's tomb and predicted that it had the aura of a future emperor. In response, Yang Shu destroyed the tomb himself. Later, another diviner said, "This tomb will still produce a high-ranking official with a broken arm." Yang Shu was skilled in riding horses and had a son who was five or six years old, bright and lovable. After the tomb was destroyed, the boy died. At the time, Yang Shu was the governor of Xiangyang, and while practicing his horse riding, he fell and broke his arm. The scholarly community at the time universally praised his loyalty.

    During the Han Dynasty, there was a cave in the area around Luoyang, whose depth was immeasurable. A woman wanted to kill her husband and told him, "I have never seen this cave." Her husband turned around to look at it, reached the cave, and was pushed down by the woman. After falling for a long time, he reached the bottom. The woman then threw food and offerings, as if to sacrifice to him. At that moment, he was disoriented and confused, but after a long time, he regained consciousness. He ate the food, which gave him a little strength. He searched for a path and found another cave, then crawled forward. After wandering for dozens of miles through rugged terrain, the cave widened and became slightly illuminated, leading to a spacious and distant land. Walking for more than a hundred miles, he felt as if he was stepping on dust and smelled the fragrance of bran and rice. He ate it, and it was more fragrant and satisfying than just filling his hunger. He wrapped some to take as food and continued walking along the cave, eating this substance. After finishing it, he encountered something like mud, which tasted similar to the dust, and he carried it with him. The place he passed through was remote and far, and the distance was difficult to measure. Eventually, he entered a city. [21]

    郛郭修整，宫馆壮丽，台榭房宇，悉以金魄为饰，虽无日月，而明逾三光。人皆长三丈，被羽衣，奏奇乐，非世间所闻。便告求哀，长人语令前去，从命前进。凡过如此者九处。最后所至，苦饥馁，长人指中庭一大柏树，近百围，下有一羊，令跪捋羊须。初得一珠，长人取之，次捋亦取，后捋令啖，即得疗饥。请问九处之名，求停不去。答曰：“君命不得停，还问张华，当悉此间。”人便随穴而行，遂得出交郡。往还六七年间，即归洛。问华，以所得二物视之。华云：“如尘者是黄河下龙涎，泥是昆山下泥。九处地，仙名九馆大夫。羊为痴龙，其初一珠，食之与天地等寿，次者延年。后者充饥而已。” [22]

    Relevant Explanation

    Explanation

    [22]

    Idiomatic Expressions

    • 郛郭修整，宮館壯麗 - Describes the outer walls and palaces as well-maintained and grand.
    • 悉以金魄為飾 - Indicates that everything is decorated with a substance called 'gold essence' or 'golden aura.'
    • 雖無日月，而明逾三光 - Suggests that despite the absence of sun and moon, the light surpasses that of the three celestial bodies (sun, moon, and stars).
    • 人皆長三丈，被羽衣 - Describes the inhabitants as three zhang (about 9 meters) tall and wearing feathered clothing.
    • 奏奇樂，非世間所聞 - Indicates that they play extraordinary music that is not heard in the world.
    • 便告求哀，長人語令前去 - Describes a request for mercy and the instruction to proceed.
    • 凡過如此者九處 - Indicates that there are nine such places.
    • 苦饑餒，長人指中庭一大柏樹 - Describes the state of extreme hunger and the pointing to a large cypress tree in the courtyard.
    • 近百圍，下有一羊 - Describes the tree as having a circumference of nearly one hundred zhang and a sheep beneath it.
    • 令跪捋羊須 - Instructs to kneel and stroke the sheep's beard.
    • 初得一珠，長人取之 - Describes getting a pearl initially, which is taken by the tall person.
    • 次捋亦取 - Describes getting another pearl, which is also taken.
    • 後捋令啖，即得療饑 - Describes the final pearl being eaten to alleviate hunger.
    • 九處地，仙名九館大夫 - Indicates that these nine places are known as the Nine Pavilion Officials.
    • 羊為痴龍 - Describes the sheep as a foolish dragon.
    • 其初一珠，食之與天地等壽 - Describes the first pearl as granting immortality equivalent to the age of the universe.
    • 次者延年 - Describes the second pearl as prolonging life.
    • 後者充饑而已 - Describes the third pearl as merely satisfying hunger.
    • 問九處之名，求停不去 - Describes the request to know the names of the nine places and to stay.
    • 答曰：“君命不得停，還問張華，當悉此間。” - Describes the response that the traveler cannot stay and must return to ask Zhang Hua.
    • 人便隨穴而行，遂得出交郡 - Describes the traveler following a tunnel and eventually emerging in Jiaozhou.
    • 往還六七間，即歸洛 - Describes the round trip taking six to seven years and returning to Luoyang.
    • 問華，以所得二物視之 - Describes showing the two items to Zhang Hua.
    • 華云：“如塵者是黃河下龍涎，泥是昆山下泥。” - Describes Zhang Hua identifying the dust-like substance as dragon saliva from the Yellow River and the mud as from Mount Kunlun.

    Cultural and Historical Allusions

    • 郛郭 - Refers to the outer walls of a city or palace.
    • 宮館 - Refers to palaces and halls.
    • 金魄 - A term for a precious substance, possibly a type of gold or a golden aura.
    • 三光 - Refers to the sun, moon, and stars.
    • 羽衣 - Refers to feathered clothing, often associated with immortals or deities.
    • 奇樂 - Refers to extraordinary or magical music.
    • 九館大夫 - Refers to a rank or title in a celestial or mythical hierarchy.
    • 痴龍 - Refers to a foolish or mischievous dragon.
    • 龍涎 - Refers to dragon saliva, often associated with precious substances.
    • 昆山 - Refers to Mount Kunlun, a mythical mountain in Chinese mythology known for its precious resources.

    Proper Nouns

    • 張華 - Refers to Zhang Hua, a historical figure known for his knowledge and wisdom.
    • 交郡 - Refers to Jiaozhou, a region in southern China.
    • 洛 - Refers to Luoyang, a city in Henan Province, China.
    • 黃河 - Refers to the Yellow River, one of the major rivers in China.
    • 昆山 - Refers to Mount Kunlun, a mythical mountain in Chinese mythology.

    In the outer walls, palaces, pavilions, and buildings were well-maintained and grand, all decorated with "gold essence." Despite the absence of sun and moon, the light surpassed that of the three celestial bodies. The inhabitants were three zhang (about 9 meters) tall, wearing feathered clothing, and playing extraordinary music that was not heard in the world. The traveler requested mercy, and the tall person instructed him to proceed. He obeyed and moved forward. There were nine such places in total. Upon arriving at the last place, he was suffering from extreme hunger. The tall person pointed to a large cypress tree in the courtyard, nearly one hundred zhang in circumference, with a sheep beneath it. He was instructed to kneel and stroke the sheep's beard. Initially, he obtained a pearl, which was taken by the tall person. The second pearl was also taken. The final pearl was eaten, and it alleviated his hunger. He asked for the names of the nine places and requested to stay. The response was, "Your command cannot be stopped; return and ask Zhang Hua, who will know about this place." The traveler followed a tunnel and eventually emerged in Jiaozhou. The round trip took six to seven years, and he returned to Luoyang. He showed the two items to Zhang Hua. Zhang Hua identified the dust-like substance as dragon saliva from the Yellow River and the mud as from Mount Kunlun. The nine places were known as the Nine Pavilion Officials. The sheep was described as a foolish dragon. The first pearl granted immortality equivalent to the age of the universe, the second prolonged life, and the third merely satisfied hunger. [22]

    嵩高山北有大穴，晋时有人误堕穴中，见二人围棋。下有一杯白饮，与堕者饮，气力十倍。棋者曰：“汝欲停此否？”堕者曰：“不愿停。”棋者曰：“从此西行有大井，其中有蛟龙，但投身入井，自当出。若饿，取井中物食之。”堕者如言，可半年，乃出蜀中。归洛下，问张华。华曰：“此仙馆。夫所饮者玉浆，所食者龙穴石髓。” [23]

    Relevant Explanation

    Explanation

    [23]

    Idiomatic Expressions

    • 大穴 - A large cave or hole.
    • 誤堕 - Accidentally fall into.
    • 围棋 - Playing chess.
    • 白饮 - White drink, likely referring to a clear liquid.
    • 气力十倍 - Ten times the strength or energy.
    • 汝欲停此否 - Do you wish to stay here?
    • 西行 - Travel west.
    • 大井 - A large well.
    • 蛟龙 - A mythical dragon-like creature often associated with water.
    • 投身 - Throw oneself.
    • 石髓 - Stone marrow, a term for a type of mineral or rock formation.
    • 洛下 - Referring to the area around Luoyang.
    • 玉浆 - Jade juice, a metaphor for a precious or magical drink.
    • 龙穴石髓 - Stone marrow from a dragon's lair, a metaphor for a rare and valuable substance.

    Cultural and Historical Allusions

    • 晋时 - Refers to the Jin Dynasty (266–420 AD).
    • 张华 - Zhang Hua (232–300 AD) was a prominent scholar, statesman, and writer during the Western Jin Dynasty.
    • 嵩高山 - Mount Song, one of the Five Great Mountains of China.
    • 仙馆 - A place associated with immortals or supernatural beings.
    • 玉浆 - Jade juice, often used in classical literature to describe a precious or magical drink.
    • 龙穴石髓 - Stone marrow from a dragon's lair, a metaphor for a rare and valuable substance.

    Proper Nouns

    • 晋时 - The Jin Dynasty.
    • 张华 - Zhang Hua.
    • 嵩高山 - Mount Song.
    • 蜀中 - Sichuan.
    • 洛下 - The area around Luoyang.

    During the Jin Dynasty, there was a large cave on the northern side of Mount Song. A person accidentally fell into the cave and saw two people playing chess. Below them was a cup of white drink, which the chess players gave to the fallen person to drink. After drinking it, the fallen person gained ten times the strength. The chess players asked, "Do you wish to stay here?" The fallen person replied, "I do not want to stay." The chess players said, "Traveling west from here, there is a large well. Inside the well is a mythical dragon. Just throw yourself into the well, and you will naturally come out. If hungry, take something from the well to eat." The fallen person followed their advice and, after about half a year, came out in Sichuan. He returned to the area around Luoyang and asked Zhang Hua. Zhang Hua replied, "This is a place of immortals. The drink they consumed was jade juice, and the food they ate was stone marrow from a dragon's lair." [23]

    张华将败，有飘风吹衣轴，六七倚壁。
    　　陈郡谢鲲，尝在一亭中宿。此亭从来杀人，夜四更末，有一人黄衣呼：“幼舆可开户。”鲲令申臂于窗中，于是授腕，鲲即极力而牵之，臂便说，乃还去。明日，看，乃鹿臂，寻血，遂取获焉。
    　　阮德如尝于厕见一鬼，长丈余，色黑而眼大，着皂单衣，平上帻，去之咫尺。德如心安气定，徐笑语之曰：“人言鬼可憎，果然！”鬼即赧愧而退。
    　　阮瞻素秉无鬼论，世莫能难，每自谓理足可以辨正幽明。忽有一鬼，通姓名作客诣阮，寒温毕，即谈名理。客甚有才情，末及鬼神事，反复甚苦，遂屈。乃作色曰：“鬼神，古今圣贤所共传，君何独言无耶？仆便是鬼！”于是忽変为异形，须臾消灭。阮默然，意色大恶。后年余病死。 [24]

    Relevant Explanation

    Explanation

    [24]

    Idiomatic Expressions

    • 張華將敗 - Indicates that Zhang Hua is about to fail or meet a disastrous end.
    • 有飘风吹衣轴，六七倚壁 - Describes an unusual occurrence where a gust of wind blows against Zhang Hua's clothes and presses them against the wall several times.
    • 此亭从来杀人 - Suggests that this pavilion has a history of causing deaths.
    • 夜四更末，有一人黄衣呼：“幼舆可开户。” - Describes a yellow-clad figure calling out to Xie Kun at the end of the fourth watch of the night.
    • 鲲令申臂于窗中，于是授腕，鲲即极力而牵之，臂便说，乃还去 - Xie Kun extends his arm through the window, and the figure grasps his wrist; Xie Kun pulls with all his might, and the figure speaks before leaving.
    • 明日，看，乃鹿臂，寻血，遂取获焉 - The next day, Xie Kun finds a deer's arm, follows the blood trail, and eventually captures the deer.
    • 阮德如尝于厕见一鬼 - Refers to Ruan Deru, who once saw a ghost in the toilet.
    • 长丈余，色黑而眼大，着皂单衣，平上帻，去之咫尺 - Describes the ghost as being over ten feet tall, dark-skinned with large eyes, wearing a black single-layered robe and a flat cap, standing just a few steps away.
    • 德如心安气定，徐笑语之曰：“人言鬼可憎，果然！” - Ruan Deru remains calm and says to the ghost, 'People say ghosts are hateful; indeed, they are!'
    • 鬼即赧愧而退 - The ghost blushes with shame and retreats.
    • 阮瞻素秉无鬼论，世莫能难，每自谓理足可以辨正幽明 - Ruan Zhan, who always held the belief that there are no ghosts, believed that no one could refute him, and he often claimed that reason was sufficient to distinguish between the visible and the invisible.
    • 忽有一鬼，通姓名作客诣阮 - Suddenly, a ghost, introducing himself by name, visited Ruan Zhan as a guest.
    • 寒温毕，即谈名理 - After exchanging pleasantries, they began discussing philosophical principles.
    • 客甚有才情，末及鬼神事，反复甚苦，遂屈 - The guest was very talented, but when the conversation turned to matters of ghosts and spirits, he found it difficult to argue and eventually conceded.
    • 乃作色曰：“鬼神，古今圣贤所共传，君何独言无耶？仆便是鬼！” - He then became angry and said, 'Ghosts and spirits are something that ancient and modern sages have all passed down; why do you alone say there are none? I am a ghost!'
    • 于是忽变作异形，须臾消灭 - At that moment, he suddenly transformed into a strange form and vanished shortly after.
    • 阮默然，意色大恶 - Ruan Zhan remained silent, and his expression turned very grim.
    • 后年余病死 - A year or so later, he fell ill and died.

    Cultural and Historical Allusions

    • 张华 - Zhang Hua, a famous scholar and statesman of the Western Jin Dynasty.
    • 陈郡谢鲲 - Xie Kun, a historical figure from Chen Prefecture, known for his bravery and skill.
    • 阮德如 - Ruan Deru, a historical figure known for his calm demeanor and wit.
    • 阮瞻 - Ruan Zhan, a philosopher and literary figure of the Eastern Jin Dynasty, known for his skepticism towards the existence of ghosts and spirits.

    Proper Nouns

    • 张华 - Zhang Hua
    • 陈郡谢鲲 - Xie Kun
    • 阮德如 - Ruan Deru
    • 阮瞻 - Ruan Zhan

    Zhang Hua was about to meet his downfall, and there was a gust of wind that blew against his clothes and pressed them against the wall several times.
    Once, Xie Kun from Chen Prefecture stayed overnight in a pavilion. This pavilion had a history of causing deaths. At the end of the fourth watch of the night, a yellow-clad figure called out to him, "Younyu, open the door!" Xie Kun extended his arm through the window, and the figure grasped his wrist. He then pulled with all his might, and the figure spoke before leaving. The next day, he found a deer's arm, followed the blood trail, and eventually captured the deer.
    Ruan Deru once saw a ghost in the toilet. The ghost was over ten feet tall, dark-skinned with large eyes, wearing a black single-layered robe and a flat cap, standing just a few steps away. Ruan Deru remained calm and said to the ghost, "People say ghosts are hateful; indeed, they are!" The ghost blushed with shame and retreated.
    Ruan Zhan, who always held the belief that there are no ghosts, believed that no one could refute him, and he often claimed that reason was sufficient to distinguish between the visible and the invisible. Suddenly, a ghost, introducing himself by name, visited Ruan Zhan as a guest. After exchanging pleasantries, they began discussing philosophical principles. The guest was very talented, but when the conversation turned to matters of ghosts and spirits, he found it difficult to argue and eventually conceded. He then became angry and said, "Ghosts and spirits are something that ancient and modern sages have all passed down; why do you alone say there are none? I am a ghost!" At that moment, he suddenly transformed into a strange form and vanished shortly after. Ruan Zhan remained silent, and his expression turned very grim. A year or so later, he fell ill and died. [24]

    永嘉中，泰山巢氏先为相县令，居在晋陵。家婢采薪，忽有一人追之，如相问讯，遂共通情，随婢还家，仍住不复去。巢恐为祸，夜辄出婢。闻与婢讴歌言语，大小悉闻，不使人见，见形者唯婢而已。每与婢宴饮，辄吹笛而歌，歌云：“闲夜寂已清，长笛亮且鸣。若欲知我者，姓郭字长生。”
    　　晋永嘉之乱，郡县无定主，强弱相暴。宜阳县有女子，姓彭名娥，父母昆弟十余口，为长沙贼所攻。时娥负器出汲于溪，闻贼至，走还。正见坞壁已破，不胜其哀，与贼相格，贼缚娥驱出溪边，将杀之。溪际有大山，石壁高数十丈，娥仰天呼曰：“皇天宁有神不？我为何罪，而当如此！”因奔走向山，山立开，广数丈，平路如砥。群贼亦逐娥入山，山遂隐合，泯然如初，贼皆压死山里，头出山外，娥遂隐不复出。娥所舍汲器化为石，形似鸡。土人因号曰石鸡山，其水为娥潭。 [25]

    Relevant Explanation

    Explanation

    [25]

    Idiomatic Expressions

    • 永嘉中 - Refers to the Yongjia period (307–313 CE) during the Western Jin dynasty, a time of significant political turmoil and migration.
    • 泰山巢氏 - Indicates the family name 'Chao' from Mount Tai (Taishan).
    • 相縣令 - Means 'magistrate of Xiang County.'
    • 晉陵 - Refers to Jinfeng, a place in Jiangsu province.
    • 家婢采薪 - Means 'a household servant went to gather firewood.'
    • 相問訊 - Means 'to inquire about each other.'
    • 通情 - Means 'to establish a relationship' or 'to become acquainted.'
    • 隨婢還家，仍住不復去 - Means 'followed the servant back home and stayed there without leaving.'
    • 恐為禍 - Means 'fear of causing trouble.'
    • 夜辄出婢 - Means 'at night, he would take the servant out.'
    • 聞與婢讴歌言语 - Means 'heard singing and talking with the servant.'
    • 大小悉闻 - Means 'all could hear.'
    • 不使人见 - Means 'no one else could see.'
    • 见形者唯婢而已 - Means 'only the servant could see his form.'
    • 每与婢宴饮，辄吹笛而歌 - Means 'every time he drank with the servant, he would play the flute and sing.'
    • 歌云 - Means 'the song went.'
    • 闲夜寂已清，长笛亮且鸣 - A poetic description of a quiet night and the sound of a long flute.
    • 若欲知我者，姓郭字长生 - Means 'If you want to know me, my surname is Guo and my given name is Changsheng.'
    • 郡县无定主，强弱相暴 - Means 'the counties had no fixed rulers, and the strong oppressed the weak.'
    • 宜阳县 - Refers to Yiyang County.
    • 姓彭名娥 - Means 'her surname is Peng and her given name is E.'
    • 父母昆弟十余口 - Means 'her parents, brothers, and sisters, more than ten in total.'
    • 为长沙贼所攻 - Means 'were attacked by bandits from Changsha.'
    • 负器出汲于溪 - Means 'carrying a container, she went to draw water from the stream.'
    • 见坞壁已破 - Means 'saw that the fortifications had been breached.'
    • 不胜其哀，与贼相格 - Means 'overwhelmed by grief, she fought against the bandits.'
    • 贼缚娥驱出溪边，将杀之 - Means 'the bandits bound her and drove her to the edge of the stream, intending to kill her.'
    • 溪际有大山，石壁高数十丈 - Means 'there was a large mountain at the edge of the stream, with a stone cliff hundreds of feet high.'
    • 奔走向山，山立开，广数丈 - Means 'ran towards the mountain, and the mountain split open, revealing a wide path.'
    • 群贼亦逐娥入山，山遂隐合，泯然如初 - Means 'the bandits also chased her into the mountain, and the mountain closed back up, as if nothing had happened.'
    • 头出山外，娥遂隐不复出 - Means 'their heads emerged outside the mountain, and E disappeared, never to be seen again.'
    • 所舍汲器化为石，形似鸡 - Means 'the container she left behind turned into a stone, resembling a chicken.'
    • 土人因号曰石鸡山，其水为娥潭 - Means 'the locals named the mountain Stone Chicken Mountain and the stream E's Pond.'

    Cultural and Historical Allusions

    • The Yongjia period was marked by the invasion of nomadic tribes, leading to the fall of the Western Jin dynasty.
    • Mount Tai is a sacred mountain in Chinese culture, often associated with longevity and immortality.
    • Indicates a local official, reflecting the administrative structure of the time.
    • A historical region in Jiangsu province, known for its strategic importance.
    • A county in Henan province, known for its historical significance.
    • Refers to bandits from Changsha, a city in Hunan province, indicating the widespread unrest during the period.

    Proper Nouns

    • 永嘉中 - The Yongjia period (307–313 CE).
    • 泰山巢氏 - The Chao family from Mount Tai.
    • 相县令 - Magistrate of Xiang County.
    • 晋陵 - Jinfeng, a place in Jiangsu province.
    • 宜阳县 - Yiyang County.
    • 长沙贼 - Bandits from Changsha.
    • 彭娥 - The main character, a woman from Yiyang County.
    • 石鸡山 - Stone Chicken Mountain.
    • 娥潭 - E's Pond.

    During the Yongjia period, the Chao family from Mount Tai, who were previously magistrates of Xiang County, resided in Jinfeng. One of their household servants went to gather firewood and was suddenly pursued by a man who inquired about her, and they became acquainted. The man followed the servant back home and stayed there without leaving. Concerned that this might bring trouble, the man would take the servant out at night. They were heard singing and talking with each other, and all could hear them, but no one else could see him; only the servant could see his form. Every time he drank with the servant, he would play the flute and sing, and the song went: "On a quiet night, the long flute sounds clear. If you wish to know me, my surname is Guo and my given name is Changsheng."

    During the Yongjia turmoil, the counties had no fixed rulers, and the strong oppressed the weak. In Yiyang County, there was a woman named Peng E, whose parents, brothers, and sisters numbered more than ten. They were attacked by bandits from Changsha. At the time, E was carrying a container and went to draw water from the stream. Hearing the bandits approach, she ran back to find that the fortifications had been breached. Overwhelmed by grief, she fought against the bandits. The bandits bound her and drove her to the edge of the stream, intending to kill her. There was a large mountain at the edge of the stream, with a stone cliff hundreds of feet high. E cried out to the heavens, "Is there a god above? What crime have I committed to be treated this way?" She then ran towards the mountain, and it split open, revealing a wide path. The bandits also chased her into the mountain, and the mountain closed back up, as if nothing had happened. Their heads emerged outside the mountain, and E disappeared, never to be seen again. The container she left behind turned into a stone, resembling a chicken. The locals named the mountain Stone Chicken Mountain and the stream E's Pond. [25]

    晋元帝世，有甲者，衣冠族姓，暴病亡。见人将上天诣司命，司命更推校，算历未尽，不应枉，召主者发遣令还。甲尤脚痛，不能行，无缘得归。主者数人共愁，相谓曰：“甲若卒以脚痛不能归，我等坐枉人之罪。”遂相率具白司命，司命思之良久，曰：“适新召胡人康乙者，在西门外，此人当遂死，其脚甚健，易之，彼此无损。”主者承敕出，将易之。胡形体甚丑，脚殊可恶，甲终不肯。主者曰：“君若不易，便长决留此耳？”不获已，遂听之。 [26]

    Relevant Explanation

    Explanation

    [26]

    Idiomatic Expressions

    • 晋元帝世 - Refers to the reign of Emperor Yuan of Jin, the first emperor of the Eastern Jin dynasty.
    • 衣冠族姓 - A term for a person of noble or distinguished lineage, often associated with the gentry class.
    • 暴病亡 - Means 'died suddenly from a severe illness.'
    • 见人将上天诣司命 - A euphemistic expression for being taken to the afterlife, where '司命' refers to the deity or official who oversees life and death.
    • 算历未尽 - Means 'the lifespan has not yet been fully determined' or 'the allotted years are not yet complete.'
    • 不应枉 - Means 'should not be wronged' or 'should not die prematurely.'
    • 主者发遣令还 - Means 'the official in charge ordered him to be sent back to life.'
    • 脚痛 - Refers to a physical ailment, specifically pain in the feet.
    • 无缘得归 - Means 'no way to return' or 'unable to return.'
    • 主者数人共愁 - Means 'several officials were troubled together.'
    • 相谓曰 - Means 'they said to each other.'
    • 卒以脚痛不能归 - Means 'if he dies suddenly because of his foot pain and cannot return.'
    • 长决留此耳 - Means 'will be permanently detained here.'
    • 不获已 - Means 'reluctantly' or 'with great reluctance.'
    • 遂听之 - Means 'so they agreed' or 'so they allowed it.'
    • 胡形体甚丑 - Means 'the barbarian's body was very ugly.'
    • 脚殊可恶 - Means 'his feet were particularly disgusting.'
    • 终不肯 - Means 'finally refused.'
    • 便长决留此耳 - Means 'will be permanently detained here.'

    Cultural and Historical Allusions

    • 晋元帝 - Emperor Yuan of Jin, the first emperor of the Eastern Jin dynasty, who ruled from 317 to 323 CE.
    • 衣冠族姓 - A term for individuals from noble or distinguished families, often associated with the gentry class.
    • 司命 - In Chinese mythology, the deity or official who oversees life and death, often referred to as the 'Fates' or 'Destinies.'
    • 算历未尽 - Refers to the belief in predestined lifespans, where one's lifespan is determined by fate or destiny.
    • 枉 - In this context, it means dying prematurely or being wronged in terms of one's lifespan.
    • 主者 - Refers to officials or authorities in charge of various tasks, including those related to the afterlife.
    • 胡人 - Refers to non-Han Chinese people, often from the northern regions, who were considered barbarians in Chinese culture.
    • 康乙 - A specific name given to the tramp, which may have been a real person or a fictional character in the story.

    Proper Nouns

    • 晋元帝 - Emperor Yuan of Jin.
    • 甲 - The main character, a nobleman.
    • 司命 - The deity or official overseeing life and death.
    • 康乙 - The tramp who replaces the nobleman.

    During the reign of Emperor Yuan of Jin, there was a man named Jia, a member of a distinguished family, who suddenly died from a severe illness. He was seen being taken to the afterlife to meet the official in charge of life and death. The official reviewed his fate and found that his lifespan had not yet been fully determined, and he should not die prematurely. The official ordered the official in charge to send him back to life. However, Jia was suffering from severe foot pain and could not move, making it impossible for him to return. Several officials were troubled by this and said to each other, "If Jia dies suddenly because of his foot pain and cannot return, we will be held responsible for wronging him." They then collectively reported the situation to the official in charge of life and death. After much thought, the official said, "I recently summoned a tramp named Kang Yi outside the west gate. He is about to die, and his feet are very healthy. Let us replace Jia with him; this will cause no harm to either party." The officials carried out the order and prepared to replace Jia. The tramp had an extremely ugly appearance and particularly disgusting feet, and Jia finally refused. The officials said, "If you do not agree to the replacement, you will be permanently detained here!" With great reluctance, Jia eventually agreed. [26]

    主者令二人并闭目，倏忽，二人脚已各易矣。仍即遣之，豁然复生。具为家人说，发视果是胡脚，丛毛连结，且胡臭。甲本士，爱玩手足，而忽得此，了不欲见，虽获更活，每惆怅殆欲如死。旁人见识此胡者，死犹殡，家近在茄子浦。甲亲往视胡尸，果见其脚着胡体，正当殡敛，对之泣。胡儿并有至性，每节朔，儿并悲思，驰往抱甲脚号咷。忽行路想遇，便攀援啼哭。为此每出入时，恒令人守门，以防胡子。终身憎秽，未尝悞视。虽三伏盛暑，必复重衣，无暂露也。 [27]

    Relevant Explanation

    Explanation

    [27]

    Idiomatic Expressions

    • 倏忽 - Classical adverb meaning 'suddenly' or 'in a moment', emphasizing the speed of the event.
    • 豁然复生 - Compound expression meaning 'suddenly revived' or 'suddenly came back to life', describing the sudden recovery of the two individuals.
    • 惆怅殆欲如死 - Literary expression meaning 'deeply sad and almost like death', conveying the intense emotional state of the individual.
    • 攀援啼哭 - Vivid expression meaning 'to grab and cry', describing the emotional reaction of the tramps' children when they see the person with the tramp's feet.
    • 恒令人守门 - Phrase meaning 'always have someone guarding the door', indicating the constant precaution taken to prevent encounters with the tramps.
    • 三伏盛暑 - Term referring to the hottest period of the year, specifically the three periods of intense heat in the summer.
    • 复重衣 - Phrase meaning 'wear multiple layers of clothing', suggesting the need for extra clothing due to the tramp's feet causing discomfort.

    Cultural and Historical Allusions

    • 茄子浦 - Place name, likely a village or town in the region where the story takes place.
    • 殡 - Term referring to the funeral rites and the preparation of the body for burial, indicating the proper funeral arrangements for the tramp.
    • 三伏 - Traditional Chinese term for the hottest period of the year, divided into three ten-day periods, each with increasing heat.
    • 节朔 - Term used in traditional Chinese calendar to denote the first day of the month, often associated with rituals and observances.

    Proper Nouns

    • 茄子浦 - Place name, likely a village or town in the region where the story takes place.
    • 胡 - Term used to refer to the tramp, possibly indicating a foreign or non-Han ethnicity.
    • 甲 - Proper name, likely the name of the character who undergoes the magical exchange.

    The official ordered the two individuals to close their eyes, and in an instant, their feet had been exchanged. They were then released, and they suddenly came back to life. They recounted the entire incident to their family, and upon examination, it was indeed the tramp's feet, covered in thick hair and emitting a foul odor. The scholar, who had previously enjoyed admiring hands and feet, was now deeply repulsed by this new appendage. Although he had been revived, he was often filled with sorrow, feeling as if he were already dead. The people who recognized this tramp, even in death, still followed proper funeral rites, and his family lived nearby in Qiezi Village. The scholar personally went to inspect the tramp's body and saw that the tramp's feet were still attached to his body, and the funeral preparations were underway. He wept at the sight. The tramp's children also showed deep affection, and on the first day of each month, they would grieve and rush to embrace the scholar's feet, wailing in sorrow. If they happened to meet him on the road, they would grab and cry. Because of this, the scholar always had someone guarding the door whenever he went out, to prevent encounters with the tramps. He loathed and despised the feet, never looking at them even once. Even during the hottest days of summer, he would wear multiple layers of clothing, never letting them be exposed for a moment. [27]

    王敦召吴猛，猛至江口，入水中，命船人并进。船至大雷，见猛行水上，从东北还逆船。弟子问其故，猛云：“水神数兴波浪，贼害行旅，暂过约敕。”以真珠一握为信。
    　　王敦近吴猛，恶之于坐，欻然失去。乃附载还南，一宿行千里，同行客视船下有两龙载船，皆不着水。
    　　晋有干庆者，无疾而终。时有术士吴猛，语庆之子曰：“干侯算未穷，方为请命，未可殡殓。”尸卧静舍，惟心下稍暖。居七日，时盛暑，庆形体向坏，猛凌晨至，教令属候气续为作水，令以洗，并饮漱，如此便退。日中许，庆苏焉，旋遂张目开口。尚未发声，阖门皆悲喜。猛又令以水含洒，遂起，吐腐血数升，稍能言语。三日，平复如常。说初见十数人来，执缚桎梏到狱。同辈十余人，以次语对。次未至，俄而见吴君北面陈释断之，王遂敕脱械令归。所经官府，莫不迎接。请谒吴君，而吴君皆与之抗礼，即不知悉何神也。 [28]

    Relevant Explanation

    Explanation

    [28]

    Idiomatic Expressions

    • 召吴猛 - Summoned Wu Meng, a straightforward action but note that Wu Meng is a historical figure known for his magical abilities.
    • 入水中，命船人并进 - Entered the water, ordering the boatmen to advance, describes Wu Meng's supernatural action.
    • 大雷 - Da Lei, a location in ancient China, likely referring to a specific area or landmark.
    • 见猛行水上，从东北还逆船 - Seeing Wu Meng walking on the water, returning from the northeast against the current, describes Wu Meng's supernatural journey.
    • 弟子问其故 - The disciple asked the reason, a common phrase in classical texts where disciples seek explanations.
    • 水神数兴波浪，贼害行旅 - The water god often stirred up waves, harming travelers, describes the water god's malevolent behavior.
    • 暂过约敕 - Temporarily passed an order, implies a temporary measure to control the water god.
    • 以真珠一握为信 - Using a handful of pearls as a token, indicates a symbolic gesture to seal the agreement.
    • 王敦近吴猛，恶之于坐，欻然失去 - Wang Dun was near Wu Meng, and he suddenly disappeared in anger, describes a sudden and dramatic event.
    • 附载还南，一宿行千里 - Attached to the boat and returned south, traveling a thousand miles in one night, emphasizes the supernatural speed.
    • 同行客视船下有两龙载船，皆不着水 - Travelers saw two dragons carrying the boat, none touching the water, describes the supernatural support.
    • 无疾而终 - Died without illness, a common phrase indicating an unexpected or natural death.
    • 干侯算未穷，方为请命 - Master Gan's fate was not yet exhausted, he was about to plead for his life, suggests a divine intervention.
    • 尸卧静舍，惟心下稍暖 - The body lay in a quiet room, only the heart slightly warm, describes the state of the corpse.
    • 猛凌晨至，教令属候气续为作水 - Wu Meng arrived early in the morning, instructing to wait for the breath and continue making water, describes a ritual or magical process.
    • 令以洗，并饮漱 - Ordering to wash and rinse, indicates a cleansing ritual.
    • 苏焉，旋遂张目开口 - Awakened, soon opened his eyes and mouth, describes the revival.
    • 阖门皆悲喜 - The entire family was both sad and happy, captures the emotional state of the family.
    • 请谒吴君，而吴君皆与之抗礼 - Asked Wu Jun for help, and Wu Jun treated them with equal respect, describes the interaction with Wu Jun.

    Cultural and Historical Allusions

    • 王敦 - A historical figure, a powerful military general during the Jin Dynasty.
    • 吴猛 - A historical figure known for his magical abilities and as a Taoist priest.
    • 干庆 - A historical figure, the subject of the second story.
    • 吴君 - A reference to Wu Jun, a powerful figure in the story, possibly a deity or a high-ranking official.
    • 术士 - Refers to a magician or sorcerer, a common figure in classical Chinese literature.
    • 干侯 - A title or name used in the context of the story, possibly referring to a divine or semi-divine figure.

    Proper Nouns

    • 王敦 - Wang Dun, a historical figure.
    • 吴猛 - Wu Meng, a historical figure known for his magical abilities.
    • 干庆 - Gan Qing, a historical figure.
    • 吴君 - Wu Jun, a powerful figure in the story, possibly a deity or a high-ranking official.
    • 术士 - Magician or sorcerer.
    • 干侯 - A title or name used in the context of the story.

    Wang Dun summoned Wu Meng. When Wu Meng reached the river mouth, he entered the water and ordered the boatmen to advance. The boat arrived at Da Lei, where they saw Wu Meng walking on the water, returning from the northeast against the current. A disciple asked him why he was doing this, and Wu Meng replied, "The water god often stirs up waves, harming travelers. I have temporarily passed an order to control him." He used a handful of pearls as a token.

    Wang Dun was near Wu Meng and suddenly disappeared in anger. He then attached himself to the boat and returned south, traveling a thousand miles in one night. Travelers saw two dragons carrying the boat, none touching the water.

    During the Jin Dynasty, there was a man named Gan Qing who died without illness. At that time, there was a magician named Wu Meng who told Gan Qing's son, "Master Gan's fate is not yet exhausted; he is about to plead for his life. Do not bury him yet." The body lay in a quiet room, only the heart slightly warm. After seven days, during the hot summer, Gan Qing's body began to decay. Wu Meng arrived early in the morning and instructed to wait for the breath and continue making water, ordering to wash and rinse. He then left. Around noon, Gan Qing revived, soon opening his eyes and mouth. Before speaking, the entire family was both sad and happy. Wu Meng then ordered to rinse with water, and Gan Qing got up, spitting out several liters of rotten blood and gradually regaining the ability to speak. After three days, he was back to normal. He said that at first, he saw several people come, bind and handcuff him to prison. There were over a dozen people of the same rank, answering in turn. Before his turn, he suddenly saw Wu Jun bowing northward, presenting a release and decision. Wu Jun then ordered to release him and let him return. All the officials they passed greeted him. They asked Wu Jun for help, and Wu Jun treated them with equal respect, but it is unknown who he was. [28]

    王丞相见郭景纯，请为一卦。卦成，郭意甚恶，云有震厄，能命驾西出数里，得一柏树，截如公长，置常寝处，灾可消也。王从之，数日果震，柏木粉碎。
    　　王丞相茂弘梦人欲以百万钱买大儿长豫，丞相甚恶之。潜为祈祷者备炭作屋，得一窖钱，料之。百万亿。大惧，一皆藏闭。俄而长豫亡。
    　　中书郎王长豫有美名，父丞相导，至所珍爱。遇疾转笃，导忧念特至。正在北床上坐，不食已积日。忽见一人，形状甚壮，着铠持刀，王问：“君是何人？”答曰：“仆是蒋侯也，公儿不佳，欲为请命，故来耳。勿复忧。”王欣喜动容，即求食，食至数升，内外咸未达所以。食毕，忽复惨然谓王曰：“中书命尽，非可救者。”言终不见也。
    　　蔡谟在厅事上坐，忽闻邻左复魄声，乃出庭前望。正见新死之家，有一老妪，上着黄罗半袖，下着缥裙，飘然升天。闻一唤声，辄回顾，三唤三顾，徘徊良久。声既绝，亦不复见。问丧家，云亡者衣服如此。 [29]

    Relevant Explanation

    Explanation

    [29]

    Idiomatic Expressions

    • 請為一卦 - Ask to cast a divination; a common practice in ancient China.
    • 卦成 - The divination was completed.
    • 能命駕西出數里 - If you can drive west for several miles; a specific action to be taken.
    • 得一柏樹，截如公長 - Get a pine tree and cut it to your length; a specific ritual object.
    • 置常寢處，災可消也 - Place it in your usual sleeping place, and the disaster can be eliminated; a belief in the power of ritual objects.
    • 王從之，數日果震 - Wang followed his advice, and after several days, there was indeed an earthquake; showing the outcome of the ritual.
    • 柏木粉碎 - The pine wood was shattered; describing the result of the earthquake.
    • 中書郎王長豫有美名 - Wang Changyu, the Middle Secretariat Attendant, had a good reputation; highlighting his status.
    • 父丞相導，至所珍愛 - His father, the Chancellor Dao, cherished him greatly; emphasizing the familial relationship.
    • 遇疾轉篤，導憂念特至 - He fell ill and became more serious, and Dao was particularly worried; showing the severity of the illness.
    • 忽見一人，形狀甚壯，著鎧持刀 - Suddenly he saw a man who was very strong, wearing armor and holding a sword; describing a supernatural encounter.
    • 公兒不佳，欲為請命，故來耳 - Your son is not well, I want to plead for his life, so I came; explaining the purpose of the encounter.
    • 中書命盡，非可救者 - The Middle Secretariat Attendant's life is over, it cannot be saved; indicating the inevitability of death.
    • 蔡謨在廳事上坐，忽聞鄰左復魄聲 - Cai Mo was sitting on the hall, and suddenly he heard a sound of returning soul from the left side of his neighbor; describing a supernatural event.
    • 正見新死之家，有一老媼，上著黃羅半袖，下著緞裙，飄然升天 - He saw a woman from a newly deceased family, wearing yellow silk half-sleeves and a brocade skirt, ascending to heaven gracefully; describing a ghostly apparition.
    • 聞一喚聲，輒回顧，三喚三顧，徘徊良久 - He heard a call, and turned back, three times called and three times looked back, lingering for a long time; describing the interaction between the ghost and the observer.
    • 聲既絕，亦不復見 - After the sound stopped, she was no longer seen; indicating the end of the apparition.
    • 問喪家，云亡者衣服如此 - He asked the bereaved family, and they said the deceased wore such clothes; confirming the description of the ghost.

    Cultural and Historical Allusions

    • 王丞相 - Refers to Wang Dao, a prominent official during the Eastern Jin Dynasty.
    • 郭景純 - Refers to Guo Jingchun, a famous alchemist and scholar.
    • 中書郎 - Refers to a high-ranking official in the imperial secretariat.
    • 王長豫 - Refers to Wang Changyu, the son of Wang Dao.
    • 蔡謨 - Refers to Cai Mo, a notable figure during the Eastern Jin Dynasty.

    Proper Nouns

    • 王丞相 - Wang Dao
    • 郭景純 - Guo Jingchun
    • 中書郎 - Middle Secretariat Attendant
    • 王長豫 - Wang Changyu
    • 蔡謨 - Cai Mo

    Wang Dao, the Chancellor, met with Guo Jingchun and asked him to cast a divination. After the divination was completed, Guo felt very uneasy and said there was an earthquake disaster. He advised Wang to drive west for several miles, find a pine tree, cut it to his length, and place it in his usual sleeping place to eliminate the disaster. Wang followed his advice, and after several days, there was indeed an earthquake, and the pine wood was shattered.

    Wang Dao, the Chancellor, had a dream where someone wanted to buy his eldest son Changyu for a million coins. The Chancellor was very displeased by this. He secretly had the priests prepare a charcoal house for prayers and found a pit full of coins, counting them, they amounted to a hundred million. Filled with great fear, he hid all the coins. Soon after, Changyu died.

    Wang Changyu, the Middle Secretariat Attendant, had a good reputation. His father, the Chancellor Dao, cherished him greatly. When Changyu fell ill and became more serious, Dao was particularly worried. He was sitting on the north bed, not eating for several days. Suddenly, he saw a man who was very strong, wearing armor and holding a sword. Wang asked, "Who are you?" The man replied, "I am Lord Jiang, your son is not well, I want to plead for his life, so I came. Do not worry anymore." Wang was delighted and his expression changed. He immediately asked for food, and ate several bowls, but neither inside nor outside understood why. After finishing his meal, the man suddenly looked sad and said to Wang, "The Middle Secretariat Attendant's life is over, it cannot be saved." After he finished speaking, he disappeared.

    Cai Mo was sitting on the hall, and suddenly he heard a sound of returning soul from the left side of his neighbor. He went out to the courtyard to look. He saw a woman from a newly deceased family, wearing yellow silk half-sleeves and a brocade skirt, ascending to heaven gracefully. He heard a call, and turned back, three times called and three times looked back, lingering for a long time. After the sound stopped, she was no longer seen. He asked the bereaved family, and they said the deceased wore such clothes. [29]

    某郡张甲者，与司徒蔡谟上有亲，侨住谟家。暂行数宿，过期不反。谟昼眠，梦甲云：“暂行忽暴病，患心腹胀满，不得吐痢，某时死，主人殡殓。”谟悲涕相对。又云：“我病名干霍乱，自可治也。但人莫知其药，故今死耳。”谟曰：“何以治之？”甲曰：“取蜘蛛，生断取脚而吞之，则愈。”谟觉，使人往甲行所验之，果死。问主人，病与时日，皆与梦符。后有患干霍乱者，谟试用，辄差。
    　　晋建武中，剡县冯法作贾。夕宿荻塘，见一女子，着缞服，白晳，形状短小，求寄载。明旦，船欲发，云暂上取行资。既去，法失绢一匹，女抱二束蒭置船中。如此十上，失十绢。法疑非人，乃缚两足，女云：“君绢在前草中。”化形作大白鹭，烹食之，肉不甚美。 [30]

    Relevant Explanation

    Explanation

    [30]

    Idiomatic Expressions

    • 某郡張甲者 - A certain Zhang Jia from a certain prefecture, introducing the subject of the story.
    • 與司徒蔡谟上有亲 - Had a close relationship with Sima Mo, the Grand Tutor, indicating a familial or close social connection.
    • 侨住谟家 - Lived temporarily in Sima Mo's household, suggesting a transient residence.
    • 暂行数宿 - Traveled for a few days, indicating a short journey.
    • 过期不反 - Failed to return on time, suggesting a delay or absence.
    • 昼眠 - Daytime sleep, indicating a nap or sleep during the day.
    • 梦甲云 - Dreamed of Zhang Jia saying, introducing a dream sequence.
    • 暂行忽暴病 - Suddenly fell gravely ill, indicating a sudden and severe illness.
    • 患心腹胀满 - Suffered from abdominal distension, describing a medical condition.
    • 不得吐痢 - Unable to vomit or defecate, indicating a blockage.
    • 某时死 - Died at a certain time, specifying the time of death.
    • 主人殡殓 - The host performed the burial rites, indicating the proper funeral procedures.
    • 干霍乱 - Acute gastroenteritis, a medical term for a sudden illness.
    • 自可治也 - Could be treated, indicating the possibility of cure.
    • 人莫知其药 - No one knew the remedy, indicating a lack of knowledge about the treatment.
    • 故今死耳 - So he died, explaining the cause of death.
    • 取蜘蛛，生断取脚而吞之 - Take a spider, cut off its legs while it's alive, and swallow it, describing a specific treatment.
    • 觉 - Awakened, indicating the end of the dream.
    • 使人往甲行所验之 - Sent someone to Zhang Jia's place to verify it, indicating a verification process.
    • 果死 - Indeed died, confirming the outcome.
    • 问主人，病与时日 - Asked the host about the illness and the time, indicating a follow-up inquiry.
    • 皆与梦符 - All matched the dream, confirming the accuracy of the dream.
    • 后有患干霍乱者 - Later, those who suffered from acute gastroenteritis, introducing a new context.
    • 辄差 - Were cured, indicating the effectiveness of the treatment.
    • 晋建武中 - During the Jianwu era of the Jin dynasty, providing a historical context.
    • 剡县冯法作贾 - Feng Fa from Shan County went on a business trip, introducing a new character and context.
    • 夕宿荻塘 - Stayed overnight at the reed pond, indicating a place of lodging.
    • 着缞服 - Wearing mourning clothes, indicating a person in mourning.
    • 白皙 - Fair-skinned, describing the appearance.
    • 形状短小 - Small in stature, describing the physical appearance.
    • 求寄载 - Asked to be carried, indicating a request for transportation.
    • 明旦 - The next morning, indicating the following day.
    • 云暂上取行资 - Said she would go up to fetch some traveling funds, indicating a reason for her absence.
    • 既去 - After she left, indicating the sequence of events.
    • 失绢一匹 - Lost a bolt of silk, indicating a theft.
    • 抱二束蒭置船中 - Carried two bundles of straw and placed them in the boat, indicating a suspicious action.
    • 如此十上 - This happened ten times, indicating a repeated action.
    • 失十绢 - Lost ten bolts of silk, indicating the total loss.
    • 疑非人 - Suspected she was not human, indicating a suspicion of supernatural activity.
    • 乃缚两足 - Then tied her feet, indicating an action to restrain her.
    • 云君绢在前草中 - She said your silk is in the grass ahead, indicating a clue.
    • 化形作大白鹭 - Transformed into a large white heron, indicating a supernatural transformation.
    • 烹食之 - Cooked and ate it, indicating the final action.

    Cultural and Historical Allusions

    • 司徒蔡谟 - Sima Mo, a high-ranking official during the Jin dynasty.
    • 干霍乱 - Acute gastroenteritis, a medical term for a sudden and severe illness.
    • 晋建武中 - The Jianwu era of the Jin dynasty, a specific historical period.
    • 剡县 - Shan County, a place name in ancient China.
    • 冯法 - Feng Fa, a character in the story.
    • 荻塘 - Reed pond, a specific location.
    • 缞服 - Mourning clothes, a cultural practice in ancient China.
    • 白鹭 - White heron, a bird often associated with purity and transformation in Chinese culture.

    Proper Nouns

    • 张甲 - Zhang Jia, a character in the story.
    • 司徒蔡谟 - Sima Mo, a high-ranking official during the Jin dynasty.
    • 晋建武中 - The Jianwu era of the Jin dynasty.
    • 剡县 - Shan County, a place name in ancient China.
    • 冯法 - Feng Fa, a character in the story.
    • 荻塘 - Reed pond, a specific location.

    A certain Zhang Jia from a certain prefecture had a close relationship with Sima Mo, the Grand Tutor, and lived temporarily in Sima Mo's household. After traveling for a few days, he failed to return on time. One day, Sima Mo took a nap and dreamed of Zhang Jia, who said, "I suddenly fell gravely ill during my travels, suffering from abdominal distension and unable to vomit or defecate. I died at a certain time, and the host performed the burial rites." Sima Mo wept bitterly at this news. Zhang Jia continued, "My illness is called acute gastroenteritis, which can be treated. But no one knew the remedy, so I died." Sima Mo asked, "How can it be treated?" Zhang Jia replied, "Take a spider, cut off its legs while it's alive, and swallow it, and you will recover." Upon waking, Sima Mo sent someone to Zhang Jia's place to verify the situation, and indeed, Zhang Jia had died. He then asked the host about the illness and the time, and all matched the dream. Later, those who suffered from acute gastroenteritis were treated by Sima Mo using this method, and they were cured.

    During the Jianwu era of the Jin dynasty, Feng Fa from Shan County went on a business trip. One evening, he stayed overnight at the reed pond and saw a woman wearing mourning clothes, fair-skinned, and small in stature. She asked to be carried. The next morning, when the boat was about to depart, she said she would go up to fetch some traveling funds. After she left, Feng Fa lost a bolt of silk. The woman returned carrying two bundles of straw and placed them in the boat. This happened ten times, and Feng Fa lost ten bolts of silk. Suspecting she was not human, he tied her feet. The woman said, "Your silk is in the grass ahead." She then transformed into a large white heron, and Feng Fa cooked and ate her. The meat was not very delicious. [30]

    晋司空郗方回葬妇于离山，使会稽郡吏史泽治墓，多平夷古墓。后坏一冢，构制甚伟，器物殊盛。冢发，内闻鼓角声。时郗公自来覌墓，俄而罕然，自是多如此。
    　　晋南顿王平新营一宅，始移，梦见一人云：“平舆令王欲以一器金赂暴胜之，为暴所戮，埋金在吾上。见镇迮甚，若君复筑室，无复出入涯。”平明旦即凿壁下入五尺，果得金。
    　　巴丘县有巫师舒礼，晋永昌元年病死，土地神将送诣太山。俗人谓巫师为道人，路过冥司福舍前，土地神问吏：“此是何等舍？”吏曰：“道人舍。”土地神曰：“是人亦道人。”便以相付。礼入门，见数千间瓦屋，皆悬竹帘，自然床榻，男女异处，有诵经者，呗偈者，自然饮食者，快乐不可言。礼文书名已到太山门，而身不至。推问土地神，神云：“道见数千间瓦屋，即问吏，言是道人，即以付之。”于是遣神更录取。礼覌未遍，见有一人，八手四眼，提金杵，逐欲撞之。 [31]

    Relevant Explanation

    Explanation

    [31]

    Idiomatic Expressions

    • 葬妇于离山 - Refers to burying a wife in Lí Mountain.
    • 使会稽郡吏史泽治墓 - Means sending the local official Shǐ Zé to oversee the tomb construction.
    • 多平夷古墓 - Means demolishing many ancient tombs to level the ground.
    • 后坏一冢 - Means later, one tomb was damaged.
    • 构制甚伟 - Means the construction was very grand.
    • 器物殊盛 - Means the artifacts were unusually abundant.
    • 冢发，内闻鼓角声 - Means when the tomb was opened, drum and trumpet sounds were heard inside.
    • 时郗公自来覌墓 - Means at that time, Xī Fānghuí came to inspect the tomb himself.
    • 俄而罕然 - Means soon after, the sounds stopped.
    • 自是多如此 - Means from then on, such things happened frequently.
    • 晋南顿王平新营一宅 - Means Prince Píng of Nándùn, the Prince of the Jin dynasty, newly built a residence.
    • 始移 - Means when he first moved in.
    • 梦见一人云 - Means he dreamed of a person who said...
    • 平舆令王欲以一器金赂暴胜之 - Means the magistrate of Píngyú, Wang, wanted to bribe Bào Shèng with a container of gold.
    • 为暴所戮 - Means was killed by Bào Shèng.
    • 埋金在吾上 - Means the gold was buried above me.
    • 见镇迮甚 - Means I feel greatly compressed.
    • 若君复筑室 - Means if you rebuild your house.
    • 无复出入涯 - Means there will be no more room to enter or exit.
    • 巴丘县有巫师舒礼 - Refers to a shaman named Shū Lǐ in Bāqiū County.
    • 晋永昌元年病死 - Means died in the first year of the Yǒngchāng era of the Jin dynasty.
    • 土地神将送诣太山 - Means the land deity was going to send him to Tai Mountain.
    • 俗人谓巫师为道人 - Means common people call shamans 'Dao people.'
    • 路过冥司福舍前 - Means as he passed by the happy dwelling of the underworld.
    • 土地神问吏 - Means the land deity asked the official.
    • 此是何等舍 - Means what kind of dwelling is this?
    • 道人舍 - Means a dwelling for Dao people.
    • 是人亦道人 - Means this person is also a Dao person.
    • 便以相付 - Means then he handed him over.
    • 礼文书名已到太山门，而身不至 - Means Shū Lǐ's name had already been recorded at the gate of Tai Mountain, but he did not arrive.
    • 推问土地神 - Means the land deity was questioned.
    • 神云 - Means the deity said...
    • 道见数千间瓦屋 - Means he saw thousands of tile-roofed houses.
    • 即问吏，言是道人，即以付之 - Means he immediately asked the official, who said it was a dwelling for Dao people, and handed him over.
    • 于是遣神更录取 - Means then the deity sent another spirit to retrieve him.
    • 礼覌未遍 - Means Shū Lǐ had not yet finished observing.
    • 见有一人，八手四眼 - Means he saw a person with eight hands and four eyes.
    • 提金杵，逐欲撞之 - Means holding a golden pestle, chasing to strike him.

    Cultural and Historical Allusions

    • 晋司空郗方回 - Refers to Xī Fānghuí, a prominent official during the Jin dynasty.
    • 离山 - Refers to Lí Mountain, a specific location in the story.
    • 会稽郡 - Refers to Huìjī Prefecture, an administrative region in the Jin dynasty.
    • 南顿王平 - Refers to Prince Píng of Nándùn, a member of the Jin royal family.
    • 巴丘县 - Refers to Bāqiū County, a specific location in the story.
    • 巫师舒礼 - Refers to Shū Lǐ, a shaman in the story.
    • 晋永昌元年 - Refers to the first year of the Yǒngchāng era (322 AD) of the Jin dynasty.
    • 土地神 - Refers to the land deity, a common figure in Chinese folk religion.
    • 太山 - Refers to Tai Mountain, a mythical mountain in Chinese mythology associated with the afterlife.
    • 冥司福舍 - Refers to the happy dwelling of the underworld, a concept in Chinese afterlife beliefs.
    • 道人 - Refers to a Daoist practitioner or shaman.
    • 八手四眼 - Refers to a supernatural being with eight hands and four eyes, often associated with demons or deities in Chinese folklore.

    Proper Nouns

    • 晋司空郗方回 - Xī Fānghuí, a high-ranking official during the Jin dynasty.
    • 离山 - Lí Mountain, a specific location in the story.
    • 会稽郡 - Huìjī Prefecture, an administrative region in the Jin dynasty.
    • 史泽 - Shǐ Zé, a local official in Huìjī Prefecture.
    • 南顿王平 - Prince Píng of Nándùn, a member of the Jin royal family.
    • 巴丘县 - Bāqiū County, a specific location in the story.
    • 舒礼 - Shū Lǐ, a shaman in the story.
    • 晋永昌元年 - The first year of the Yǒngchāng era (322 AD) of the Jin dynasty.
    • 土地神 - The land deity, a common figure in Chinese folk religion.
    • 太山 - Tai Mountain, a mythical mountain in Chinese mythology associated with the afterlife.

    Xī Fānghuí, the high-ranking official of the Jin dynasty, buried his wife in Lí Mountain and sent the local official Shǐ Zé to oversee the tomb construction, leveling many ancient tombs in the process. Later, one tomb was damaged, revealing a grand construction with an unusually abundant array of artifacts. When the tomb was opened, drum and trumpet sounds were heard inside. At that time, Xī Fānghuí came to inspect the tomb himself, and soon after, the sounds stopped. From then on, such things happened frequently.

    Prince Píng of Nándùn, the Prince of the Jin dynasty, newly built a residence and moved in. He dreamed of a person who said, "The magistrate of Píngyú, Wang, wanted to bribe Bào Shèng with a container of gold. He was killed by Bào Shèng, and the gold was buried above me. I feel greatly compressed. If you rebuild your house, there will be no more room to enter or exit." The next morning, Prince Píng immediately dug into the wall five feet deep and indeed found the gold.

    In Bāqiū County, there was a shaman named Shū Lǐ, who died in the first year of the Yǒngchāng era of the Jin dynasty. The land deity was going to send him to Tai Mountain. Common people call shamans "Dao people." As he passed by the happy dwelling of the underworld, the land deity asked the official, "What kind of dwelling is this?" The official replied, "A dwelling for Dao people." The land deity said, "This person is also a Dao person." Then he handed him over. Shū Lǐ entered the dwelling, seeing thousands of tile-roofed houses, all with bamboo curtains, natural beds and tables, men and women separated, some reciting scriptures, some chanting verses, and some enjoying natural food, all in a state of indescribable joy. Shū Lǐ's name had already been recorded at the gate of Tai Mountain, but he did not arrive.
