The land deity was questioned, and he said, "He saw thousands of tile-roofed houses and immediately asked the official, who said it was a dwelling for Dao people, and handed him over." Then the deity sent another spirit to retrieve him. Shū Lǐ had not yet finished observing when he saw a person with eight hands and four eyes, holding a golden pestle, chasing to strike him. [31]

    便怖走还出门，神已在门迎，捉送太山。太山府君问礼：“卿在世间，皆何所为？”礼曰：“事三万六千神，为人解除祠祀，或杀牛犊猪羊鸡鸭。”府君曰：“汝佞神杀生，其罪应上热熬。”使吏牵着熬所。见一物，牛头人身，捉铁叉，叉礼着投铁床上，宛转身体焦烂，求死不得。经一宿二日，备极冤楚。府君问主者：“礼寿命应尽？为顿夺其命？”校禄籍，余算八年。府君曰：“录来。”牛首人复以铁叉叉着熬边。府君曰：“今遣卿归，终毕余算。勿复杀生淫祀。”礼忽还活，遂不复作巫师。
    　　晋太宁元年，余杭人姓王，失其名，往上舍，过庙乞福。既去，亡履，已行五六里，懒复更反取，一白衣人持履后至，云：“官使还君。”化为鹄，飞入田中。 [32]

    Relevant Explanation

    Explanation

    [32]

    Idiomatic Expressions

    • 便怖走还出门 - Implies a sudden and panicked retreat; '便怖' means 'immediately frightened,' and '走还' means 'ran back.'
    • 宛转身体焦烂 - Describes intense physical suffering; '宛转' means 'twisting and turning,' and '身体焦烂' means 'body burned and charred.'

    Cultural and Historical Allusions

    • 太山府君 - Refers to the Lord of Mount Tai, a significant deity in Chinese mythology and local religion.
    • 热熬 - A term for a fiery furnace or hellish place where souls are punished.
    • 牛头人身 - Describes a creature with a cow's head and a human body, often associated with deities or demons in Chinese folklore.
    • 晋太宁元年 - Refers to the first year of the Tai Ning era during the Jin Dynasty (323 AD).
    • 余杭人姓王 - A man named Wang from Yuhang, indicating a specific individual from a particular place.
    • 化为鹄 - Transformed into a crane, a common motif in Chinese mythology where supernatural beings can change form.

    Proper Nouns

    • 太山府君 - The Lord of Mount Tai.
    • 热熬 - The fiery furnace or hellish place.
    • 牛头人身 - The creature with a cow's head and a human body.
    • 晋太宁元年 - The first year of the Tai Ning era during the Jin Dynasty (323 AD).
    • 余杭人姓王 - A man named Wang from Yuhang.
    • 上舍 - A higher-level temple or shrine.
    • 乞福 - To pray for blessings or good fortune.
    • 化为鹄 - Transformed into a crane.

    Suddenly, he was terrified and ran back out the door. The deity was already waiting at the door, grabbed him, and took him to Mount Tai. The Lord of Mount Tai asked Li, "What did you do in the world?" Li replied, "I served 36,000 deities, performed sacrifices and offerings for people, sometimes slaughtering calves, pigs, sheep, chickens, and ducks." The Lord of Mount Tai said, "You flattered the deities and killed living beings. Your crime should be punished in the fiery furnace." He ordered the guards to take him to the furnace. Li saw a creature with a cow's head and a human body holding an iron fork, which he used to thrust Li onto an iron bed. Li twisted and turned, his body burned and charred, suffering immensely and unable to die. After one night and two days, he endured extreme torment. The Lord of Mount Tai asked the registrar, "Is Li's lifespan supposed to end? Or was it suddenly taken away?" Checking the life record, it was found that Li had eight years left. The Lord of Mount Tai said, "Record this." The creature with the cow's head and human body again thrust Li toward the edge of the furnace. The Lord of Mount Tai said, "Now I send you back, to finish the remaining years of your life. Do not repeat the acts of killing living beings and excessive sacrifices." Li suddenly came back to life and never became a shaman again.

    In the first year of the Tai Ning era of the Jin Dynasty, a man named Wang from Yuhang went to a higher-level temple to pray for blessings. After leaving, he lost his shoe and had already walked five or six li, but he was too lazy to go back and get it. A white-clad person held the shoe and followed him. The person said, "The official sent it back to you." The person then transformed into a crane and flew into the fields. [32]

    晋太兴二年，吴氏华隆好猎，养一快犬，名曰的尾，常将自随，隆后至江边伐荻，犬暂出渚次。隆为大蛇所围，绕周身。犬还，便咋蛇，蛇死。隆僵仆无所知，犬彷佛涕泣。走还船，复反草中。其伴怪其所以，随往，见隆闷绝委地。将归家二日，犬为不食。隆复苏，乃始进饭。隆愈爱惜，同于亲戚。后忽失之，二年寻求，见在显山。
    　　晋咸和初，徐精远行，梦与妻寝，有身。明年归，妻果产，后如其言矣。
    　　牵腾以咸和三年为沛郡太守，出行不节，梦乌衣人告云：“何数出不辍？唯当断马足。”腾后出行，马足自断。腾行近郭外，忽然而暗。有一人，长丈余，玄冠白衣，遥叱将车人，使避之。俄而长人至，以马鞭击御者，即倒。既明，从人视车空，觅腾所在，行六七十步，见在榛莽中，隐几而坐，云了不自知。腾后五十日被诛。 [33]

    Relevant Explanation

    Explanation

    [33]

    Idiomatic Expressions

    • 好猎 - Means 'enjoys hunting.'
    • 养一快犬 - Means 'raised a fast dog.'
    • 名曰的尾 - Means 'named it 'De Wei'.'
    • 常将自随 - Means 'always kept it with him.'
    • 隆后至江边伐荻 - Means 'later, when he went to the riverbank to cut reeds.'
    • 犬暂出渚次 - Means 'the dog temporarily went out to the sandbank.'
    • 隆为大蛇所围 - Means 'Hua Long was surrounded by a large snake.'
    • 绕周身 - Means 'wrapped around his entire body.'
    • 犬还，便咋蛇 - Means 'the dog returned and bit the snake.'
    • 蛇死 - Means 'the snake died.'
    • 隆僵仆无所知 - Means 'Hua Long lay motionless and unconscious.'
    • 犬彷佛涕泣 - Means 'the dog seemed to weep.'
    • 走还船 - Means 'ran back to the boat.'
    • 复反草中 - Means 'then returned to the grass.'
    • 其伴怪其所以 - Means 'his companions were surprised by what had happened.'
    • 见隆闷绝委地 - Means 'they saw Hua Long lying unconscious on the ground.'
    • 将归家二日 - Means 'they took him home for two days.'
    • 犬为不食 - Means 'the dog refused to eat.'
    • 隆复苏 - Means 'Hua Long regained consciousness.'
    • 乃始进饭 - Means 'only then did he start eating.'
    • 隆愈爱惜，同于亲戚 - Means 'Hua Long loved and cherished the dog as if it were a close relative.'
    • 后忽失之 - Means 'later, he suddenly lost the dog.'
    • 二年寻求 - Means 'for two years, he searched for it.'
    • 见在显山 - Means 'he found it in Xian Mountain.'
    • 徐精远行 - Means 'Xu Jing went on a long journey.'
    • 梦与妻寝，有身 - Means 'dreamed of sleeping with his wife and becoming pregnant.'
    • 明年归，妻果产 - Means 'the next year, when he returned, his wife indeed gave birth.'
    • 后如其言矣 - Means 'as he had said.'
    • 牵腾以咸和三年为沛郡太守 - Means 'Qian Teng was appointed as the governor of Pei Commandery in the third year of the reign of Emperor Mu of Jin.'
    • 出行不节 - Means 'his travels were not regular.'
    • 梦乌衣人告云 - Means 'he dreamed of a man in black clothing telling him.'
    • 何数出不辍 - Means 'why do you go out so frequently without stopping?'
    • 唯当断马足 - Means 'you should only cut off the horse's hooves.'
    • 腾后出行 - Means 'later, when Qian Teng went out.'
    • 马足自断 - Means 'the horse's hooves broke off on their own.'
    • 腾行近郭外 - Means 'Qian Teng walked near the city gates.'
    • 忽然而暗 - Means 'suddenly, it became dark.'
    • 有一人，长丈余 - Means 'there was a man, over ten feet tall.'
    • 玄冠白衣 - Means 'wearing a black hat and white robe.'
    • 遥叱将车人 - Means 'he shouted at the driver from a distance.'
    • 使避之 - Means 'to get out of the way.'
    • 俄而长人至 - Means 'soon, the tall man arrived.'
    • 以马鞭击御者 - Means 'he struck the driver with a horsewhip.'
    • 即倒 - Means 'the driver fell down.'
    • 既明 - Means 'when it became light.'
    • 从人视车空 - Means 'his attendants saw the carriage empty.'
    • 觅腾所在 - Means 'searched for where Qian Teng was.'
    • 行六七十步 - Means 'after walking sixty to seventy steps.'
    • 见在榛莽中 - Means 'they found him in the thicket.'
    • 隐几而坐 - Means 'sitting hidden behind a table.'
    • 云了不自知 - Means 'he said he did not know what had happened.'
    • 腾后五十日被诛 - Means 'fifty days later, Qian Teng was executed.'

    Cultural and Historical Allusions

    • 晋太兴二年 - The year 319 AD during the reign of Emperor Yuan of Jin.
    • 晋咸和初 - The early years of the reign of Emperor Mu of Jin.
    • 吴氏华隆 - A person named Hua Long from the Wu family.
    • 徐精 - A person named Xu Jing.
    • 牵腾 - A person named Qian Teng.
    • 沛郡太守 - The governor of Pei Commandery.
    • 乌衣人 - A man in black clothing, often associated with supernatural beings or omens.
    • 显山 - Xian Mountain, a location in the story.
    • 梦与妻寝，有身 - A dream of becoming pregnant, which is a common theme in Chinese folklore and literature.
    • 出行不节 - Irregular travel, which may be seen as a sign of impending misfortune.
    • 梦乌衣人告云 - A dream of a supernatural being giving advice or warning.
    • 何数出不辍 - Frequent travel, which is criticized in the dream.
    • 唯当断马足 - A cryptic warning that is interpreted as a sign of impending doom.
    • 马足自断 - The horse's hooves breaking off, which is a literal fulfillment of the dream.
    • 忽然而暗 - Sudden darkness, which is a common motif in supernatural stories.
    • 有一人，长丈余 - A tall man, often associated with supernatural beings.
    • 玄冠白衣 - A black hat and white robe, which are traditional Chinese clothing items.
    • 遥叱将车人 - Shouting at the driver from a distance, which is a command to get out of the way.
    • 使避之 - To get out of the way.
    • 俄而长人至 - The tall man arriving, which is a continuation of the supernatural event.
    • 以马鞭击御者 - Striking the driver with a horsewhip, which is a violent act.
    • 即倒 - The driver falling down, which is a result of the violent act.
    • 既明 - When it became light, which marks the end of the supernatural event.
    • 从人视车空 - The attendants seeing the carriage empty, which is a sign of Qian Teng's disappearance.
    • 觅腾所在 - Searching for where Qian Teng was, which is a common reaction to a sudden disappearance.
    • 行六七十步 - Walking sixty to seventy steps, which is a specific distance.
    • 见在榛莽中 - Finding Qian Teng in the thicket, which is a hidden location.
    • 隐几而坐 - Sitting hidden behind a table, which is a sign of confusion or disorientation.
    • 云了不自知 - Saying he did not know what had happened, which is a sign of amnesia or confusion.
    • 腾后五十日被诛 - Qian Teng being executed fifty days later, which is a tragic ending.

    During the second year of the Taixing era of the Jin dynasty, Hua Long of the Wu family was an avid hunter and kept a swift dog named De Wei, whom he always took with him. Later, when Hua Long went to the riverbank to cut reeds, the dog temporarily went out to the sandbank. Hua Long was then surrounded by a large snake that wrapped itself around his entire body. The dog returned and bit the snake, causing it to die. Hua Long lay motionless and unconscious, while the dog seemed to weep. The dog ran back to the boat and then returned to the grass. Hua Long's companions were surprised by what had happened and went with him, finding him lying unconscious on the ground. They took him home for two days, during which time the dog refused to eat. When Hua Long regained consciousness, he only then started eating again. Hua Long loved and cherished the dog as if it were a close relative. Later, he suddenly lost the dog and searched for it for two years before finding it in Xian Mountain.

    In the early years of the Xianhe era of the Jin dynasty, Xu Jing went on a long journey and dreamed of sleeping with his wife, who became pregnant. The next year, when he returned, his wife indeed gave birth, as he had predicted.

    Qian Teng was appointed as the governor of Pei Commandery in the third year of the Xianhe era. His travels were not regular, and he dreamed of a man in black clothing telling him, "Why do you go out so frequently without stopping? You should only cut off the horse's hooves." Later, when Qian Teng went out, the horse's hooves broke off on their own. As he walked near the city gates, it suddenly became dark. There was a man over ten feet tall, wearing a black hat and white robe, who shouted at the driver from a distance to get out of the way. Soon, the tall man arrived and struck the driver with a horsewhip, causing the driver to fall down. When it became light, his attendants saw the carriage empty and searched for where Qian Teng was. After walking sixty to seventy steps, they found him in the thicket, sitting hidden behind a table, saying he did not know what had happened. Fifty days later, Qian Teng was executed. [33]

    晋咸康中，豫州刺史毛宝戍邾城。有一军人于武昌市买得一白龟，长四五寸，置瓮中养之。渐大，放江中。后邾城遭石氏败，赴江者莫不沉溺。所养人被甲入水中，觉如堕一石上。须臾视之，乃是先放白龟。既得至岸，回顾而去。
    　　庾崇者，建元中于江州溺死，尔日即还家。见形一如平生，多在妻乐氏室中。妻初恐惧，每呼诸从女作伴。于是作伴渐疏，时或暂来，辄恚骂云：“贪与生者接耳！反致疑恶，岂副我归意邪？”从女在内纺绩，忽见纺绩之具在空中，有物拨乱，或投之于地，从女怖惧皆去。鬼即常见。有一男，才三岁，就母求食，母曰：“无钱，食那可得？”鬼乃凄怆，抚其儿头曰：“我不幸早世，令汝穷乏，愧汝念汝，情何极也！”忽见将二百钱置妻前，云可为儿买食。如此经年，妻转贫苦不立。鬼云：“卿既守节，而贫苦若此，直当相迎耳！”未几，妻得疾亡，鬼乃寂然。 [34]

    Relevant Explanation

    Explanation

    [34]

    Idiomatic Expressions

    • 晋咸康中 - Refers to the time period of the Jin Dynasty during the reign of Emperor Mu, specifically the Xiankang era (345-346 AD).
    • 豫州刺史毛宝戍邾城 - Indicates that Mao Bao, the governor of Yuzhou, was stationed in Zucheng.
    • 武昌市买得一白龟 - Means 'bought a white turtle in Wuchang market.'
    • 置瓮中养之 - Means 'placed it in a jar to raise it.'
    • 渐大，放江中 - Means 'gradually grew larger and was released into the river.'
    • 石氏败 - Refers to the defeat of the Shi clan, likely referring to the Later Zhao (Shi clan) during the Sixteen Kingdoms period.
    • 赴江者莫不沉溺 - Means 'those who jumped into the river did not escape drowning.'
    • 被甲入水中 - Means 'wearing armor, he entered the water.'
    • 觉如堕一石上 - Means 'felt as if he fell onto a stone.'
    • 先放白龟 - Means 'the white turtle that was previously released.'
    • 回顾而去 - Means 'looked back and then left.'
    • 庾崇者，建元中于江州溺死 - Means 'Yu Chong, who drowned in Jiangzhou during the Jianyuan era.'
    • 尔日即还家 - Means 'that day, he returned home.'
    • 见形一如平生 - Means 'appeared just like he was alive.'
    • 多在妻乐氏室中 - Means 'often appeared in his wife Le's room.'
    • 每呼诸从女作伴 - Means 'often called his sisters-in-law to keep him company.'
    • 作伴渐疏 - Means 'the company became less frequent.'
    • 时或暂来 - Means 'sometimes came briefly.'
    • 辄恚骂 - Means 'would then scold angrily.'
    • 贪与生者接耳 - Means 'greedy to interact with the living.'
    • 反致疑恶 - Means 'instead, causing suspicion and dislike.'
    • 岂副我归意邪 - Means 'how does this match my intention to return?'
    • 纺绩 - Refers to spinning and weaving.
    • 纺绩之具 - Means 'spinning and weaving tools.'
    • 抚其儿头 - Means 'stroked his son's head.'
    • 我不幸早世 - Means 'unfortunately, I died young.'
    • 令汝穷乏 - Means 'leaving you in poverty.'
    • 愧汝念汝 - Means 'feeling ashamed and thinking of you.'
    • 情何极也 - Means 'how deep are my feelings!'
    • 忽见将二百钱置妻前 - Means 'suddenly, he saw him placing two hundred coins in front of his wife.'
    • 可为儿买食 - Means 'to buy food for the child.'
    • 妻转贫苦不立 - Means 'his wife became increasingly poor and destitute.'
    • 直当相迎耳 - Means 'I will just come to meet you.'
    • 妻得疾亡 - Means 'his wife fell ill and died.'
    • 鬼乃寂然 - Means 'the spirit then became silent.'

    Cultural and Historical Allusions

    • 晋咸康中 - The Xiankang era (345-346 AD) of the Eastern Jin Dynasty.
    • 豫州刺史毛宝 - Mao Bao, a historical figure who served as the governor of Yuzhou during the Jin Dynasty.
    • 武昌市 - Wuchang, a city in modern-day Hubei Province.
    • 石氏败 - Refers to the defeat of the Shi clan, likely the Later Zhao (Shi clan) during the Sixteen Kingdoms period.
    • 庾崇 - Yu Chong, a historical figure who drowned in Jiangzhou during the Jianyuan era.
    • 建元中 - The Jianyuan era, a period during the Eastern Jin Dynasty.

    Proper Nouns

    • 晋咸康中 - The Xiankang era of the Eastern Jin Dynasty.
    • 豫州刺史毛宝 - Mao Bao, the governor of Yuzhou.
    • 邾城 - Zucheng, a city in modern-day Hubei Province.
    • 武昌市 - Wuchang, a city in modern-day Hubei Province.
    • 石氏 - The Shi clan, likely referring to the Later Zhao (Shi clan).
    • 庾崇 - Yu Chong, a historical figure.
    • 建元中 - The Jianyuan era of the Eastern Jin Dynasty.
    • 江州 - Jiangzhou, a region in modern-day Jiangxi Province.
    • 乐氏 - Le, the wife of Yu Chong.

    During the Xiankang era of the Eastern Jin Dynasty, Mao Bao, the governor of Yuzhou, was stationed in Zucheng. A soldier from his army bought a white turtle in the Wuchang market, about four to five inches long, and placed it in a jar to raise it. As the turtle grew larger, he released it into the river. Later, Zucheng was defeated by the Shi clan, and those who jumped into the river all drowned. The man who had raised the turtle, wearing armor, entered the water and felt as if he fell onto a stone. Shortly afterward, he looked down and saw that it was the white turtle he had previously released. Once he reached the shore, the turtle looked back at him and then left.

    Yu Chong, who drowned in Jiangzhou during the Jianyuan era, returned home that same day. He appeared just as he had been in life and often appeared in his wife Le's room. His wife was initially frightened and often called her sisters-in-law to keep him company. Over time, the company became less frequent, and he would sometimes come briefly, then scold angrily, saying, "Greedy to interact with the living! Instead, causing suspicion and dislike. How does this match my intention to return?" When the sisters-in-law were spinning and weaving inside, they suddenly saw the spinning and weaving tools in the air, being disturbed by something, and sometimes thrown to the ground. The sisters-in-law, terrified, all left. The ghost was then often seen. They had a son, only three years old, who went to his mother asking for food. His mother said, "We have no money, how can we get food?" The ghost then became sad and stroked his son's head, saying, "Unfortunately, I died young, leaving you in poverty. Feeling ashamed and thinking of you, how deep are my feelings!" Suddenly, he saw him placing two hundred coins in front of his wife, saying they could buy food for the child. This continued for a year, and his wife became increasingly poor and destitute. The ghost said, "You have maintained your virtue, yet you are so poor. I will just come to meet you!" Soon after, his wife fell ill and died, and the ghost then became silent. [34]

    石勒问佛图澄：“刘曜可擒，兆可见不？”澄令童子斋七日，取麻油掌中研之，燎旃檀而咒。有顷，举手向童子，掌内晃然有异。澄问：“有所见不？”曰：“唯见一军人，长大白晳，有异望，以朱缚其肘。”澄曰：“此即曜也。”其年，果生擒曜。
    　　石虎时，太武殿图贤人之像，头忽悉缩入肩中。
    　　新城县民陈绪家，晋永和中，旦闻扣门，自通云陈都尉。便有车马声，不见形，径进，呼主人共语曰：“我应来此，当权住君家，相为致福。”令绪施设床帐于斋中。或人诣之，斋持酒礼求愿，所言皆验。每进酒食，令人跪拜授闱里，不得开视。复有一身，疑是狐狸之类，因跪急把取，此物却还床后，大怒曰：“何敢嫌试都尉？”此人心痛欲死，主人为扣头谢，良久意解。自后众不敢犯，而绪举家无恙。每事益利，此外无多损益也。 [35]

    Relevant Explanation

    Explanation

    [35]

    Idiomatic Expressions

    • 為之一空 - Set phrase meaning completely emptied; fields were left barren.
    • 可擒 - Implies the possibility of capture.
    • 兆可见不 - Polite way of asking if something is possible or observable.
    • 斋七日 - Fasting for seven days, often associated with religious or spiritual practices.
    • 取麻油掌中研之 - Taking sesame oil and grinding it in the palm, describing a ritualistic action.
    • 燎旃檀而咒 - Burning sandalwood and chanting, indicating a religious or magical ritual.
    • 有顷，举手向童子 - After a while, he raises his hand toward the young boy, describing a physical gesture.
    • 掌内晃然有异 - Inside his palm, there is a strange light, where '晃然' suggests a bright or unusual glow.
    • 此即曜也 - This is Liu Yao, where '即' means 'is' or 'is the same as.'
    • 果生擒曜 - Indeed, Liu Yao was captured alive, where '果' means 'indeed' or 'as expected.'
    • 头忽悉缩入肩中 - The heads suddenly all retract into the shoulders, describing a supernatural or magical phenomenon.
    • 旦闻扣门 - In the morning, he heard someone knocking on the door, where '旦' means 'in the morning.'
    • 自通云陈都尉 - Introducing himself as General Chen, where '都尉' is a military rank.
    • 便有车马声 - Suddenly there is the sound of carriages and horses, describing a sudden appearance.
    • 不见形，径进 - Without appearing, he enters directly, indicating a mysterious or supernatural presence.
    • 呼主人共语 - He calls the host to talk, where '共语' means to converse.
    • 我应来此，当权住君家，相为致福 - I should come here, so I will stay at your house for now and bring you good fortune, where '权住' means 'to stay temporarily.'
    • 令绪施设床帐于斋中 - He orders Chen Xu to set up beds and curtains in the hall, where '斋' refers to a hall or room.
    • 或人诣之 - Some people visit him, where '诣' means to visit or pay a call.
    • 斋持酒礼求愿 - They bring wine and offerings to ask for wishes, where '斋持' means to bring offerings.
    • 所言皆验 - What he says all comes true, where '验' means 'to verify' or 'to come true.'
    • 每进酒食 - Whenever he offers wine and food, where '进' means to offer or present.
    • 令人跪拜授闱里 - He makes people kneel and bow, handing them through the door, where '闱里' refers to a door or entrance.
    • 不得开视 - They cannot open or look, indicating a restriction.
    • 复有一身 - There is another one, referring to another person or entity.
    • 疑是狐狸之类 - Suspected to be like a fox, where '狐狸' refers to a fox or a cunning person.
    • 因跪急把取 - Because of this, they kneel and quickly grab it, describing an action.
    • 此物却还床后 - This thing returns to the back of the bed, indicating a movement.
    • 大怒曰 - It gets very angry and says, where '大怒' means 'to get very angry.'
    • 何敢嫌试都尉 - How dare you doubt or test the General? where '嫌试' means to doubt or test.
    • 此人心痛欲死 - This person's heart pains as if they are dying, describing intense pain.
    • 主人为扣头谢 - The host kneels and apologizes, where '扣头' means to kneel and bow.
    • 良久意解 - After a long time, the anger subsides, where '意解' means 'the anger subsides.'
    • 自后众不敢犯 - After this, no one dares to offend, indicating a change in behavior.
    • 而绪举家无恙 - And Chen Xu's entire family remains unharmed, where '无恙' means 'unharmed' or 'safe.'
    • 每事益利 - Everything becomes more prosperous, where '益利' means 'more prosperous.'
    • 此外无多损益 - There is no significant loss or gain, where '损益' means 'loss or gain.'

    Cultural and Historical Allusions

    • 石勒 - Shi Le, a Xiongnu chieftain who founded the Later Zhao dynasty during the Sixteen Kingdoms period.
    • 佛图澄 - Fotu Chen, a prominent Buddhist monk who served as an advisor to Shi Le.
    • 刘曜 - Liu Yao, a ruler of the Former Zhao dynasty.
    • 兆 - Zhao, likely referring to a person or omen, possibly another historical figure.
    • 太武殿 - The Ta Wu Hall, a building in the Later Zhao palace.
    • 晋永和中 - During the Yonghe period of the Jin dynasty (345-356 CE), a regnal era name.
    • 新城县 - Xincheng County, a location in the Jin dynasty.
    • 陈绪 - Chen Xu, a resident of Xincheng County.
    • 陈都尉 - General Chen, a military rank holder.
    • 狐狸 - Fox, often associated with cunning and trickery in Chinese folklore.

    Shi Le asked Fotu Chen, "Can Liu Yao be captured? Can Zhao be seen?" Chen ordered a young boy to fast for seven days, then took sesame oil and ground it in his palm, burned sandalwood, and chanted. After a while, he raised his hand toward the young boy, and inside his palm there was a strange light. Chen asked, "Did you see anything?" The boy replied, "I only saw one soldier, tall and fair-skinned, with a distinguished appearance, bound at the elbows with red ropes." Chen said, "This is Liu Yao." That year, Liu Yao was indeed captured alive.

    During the reign of Shi Hu, the heads of the images of virtuous people in the Ta Wu Hall suddenly all retracted into their shoulders.

    In the household of Chen Xu in Xincheng County during the Yonghe period of the Jin dynasty, in the morning they heard someone knocking on the door, who introduced himself as General Chen. Suddenly there was the sound of carriages and horses, but without appearing, he entered directly. He called the host to talk and said, "I should come here, so I will stay at your house for now and bring you good fortune." He ordered Chen Xu to set up beds and curtains in the hall. Some people visited him, bringing wine and offerings to ask for wishes, and what he said all came true. Whenever he offered wine and food, he made people kneel and bow, handing them through the door, and they could not open or look. There was another one, suspected to be like a fox, because of this, they knelt and quickly grabbed it, and this thing returned to the back of the bed, getting very angry and saying, "How dare you doubt or test the General?" This person's heart pained as if they were dying, the host knelt and apologized, and after a long time, the anger subsided. After this, no one dares to offend, and Chen Xu's entire family remains unharmed. Everything becomes more prosperous, and there is no significant loss or gain. [35]

    晋升平元年，剡县陈素家富，娶妇十年，无儿。夫欲娶妾，妇祷祠神明，忽然有身。邻家小人妇亦同有，因货邻妇云：“我生若男，天愿也；若是女，汝是男者，当交易之。”便共将许。邻人生男，此妇后三日生女，便交取之。素忻喜，养至十三，当祠祀。家有老婢，素见鬼，云：“见府君先人，来至门首便住。但见一群小人来座所，食啖此祭。”父甚疑怪，便迎见鬼人至，祠时转令看，言语皆同。素便入问妇，妇惧，具说言此事。还男本家，唤女归。 [36]

    Relevant Explanation

    Explanation

    [36]

    Idiomatic Expressions

    • 晋升平元年 - A specific year in the Eastern Jin dynasty, indicating a historical context.
    • 祷祠神明 - Pray to the gods or deities, a common practice in ancient Chinese culture.
    • 忽然有身 - Literally 'suddenly became pregnant,' but can also mean 'unexpectedly conceived.'
    • 货邻妇 - Literally 'to sell the neighbor's wife,' but in this context, means 'to trade with the neighbor's wife.'
    • 府君先人 - Refers to the ancestors of the deceased husband, where '府君' is a respectful term for a deceased official or man of high social standing.
    • 食啖此祭 - Literally 'to eat this offering,' but implies that the spirits were consuming the food offered during the ritual.

    Cultural and Historical Allusions

    • 剡县 - A county in present-day Zhejiang Province, historically known for its cultural significance.
    • 陈素 - The name of a wealthy man from剡县, indicating a local historical figure.
    • 府君 - A respectful term for a deceased official or man of high social standing, often used in funerary contexts.
    • 先人 - Refers to ancestors, which were highly revered in ancient Chinese culture.
    • 祠祀 - A ritual of worship or sacrifice, typically performed to honor ancestors or deities.

    Proper Nouns

    • 剡县 - A specific place name in present-day Zhejiang Province.
    • 陈素 - The name of a wealthy man from剡县.
    • 府君 - A respectful term for a deceased official or man of high social standing.

    In the first year of the Jincheng era, Chen Su of Shan County was wealthy and had been married for ten years without a son. His husband wished to take a concubine, and his wife prayed to the gods. Suddenly, she became pregnant. A woman from a neighboring household also became pregnant at the same time. They agreed to exchange their children, saying, "If I bear a boy, it is the will of heaven; if I bear a girl, and you bear a boy, we shall exchange them." They agreed to this arrangement. The neighbor gave birth to a boy, and three days later, Chen Su's wife gave birth to a girl. They exchanged the children. Chen Su was very happy and raised the girl until she was thirteen years old, when he prepared to hold a sacrificial ceremony. One of his old servants, who could see ghosts, reported, "I saw the ancestors of your deceased husband come to the gate and stop. They then brought a group of small people to the place of the offering and ate the food." Chen Su was greatly puzzled and suspicious, so he invited the ghost-seer to come and watch during the ceremony. The ghost-seer confirmed that everything was as described. Chen Su then went to ask his wife about this, and she was frightened and told him everything. He returned the boy to his original family and called for the girl to come back. [36]

    晋升平末，故章县老公有一女，居深山，余杭□广求为妇，不许。公后病死，女上县买棺，行半道，逢广。女具道情事。女因曰：“穷逼，君若能往家守父尸，须吾还者，便为君妻。”广许之。女曰：“我栏中有猪，可为杀以饴作儿。”广至女家，但闻屋中有抃掌欣舞之声。广披离，见众鬼在堂，共捧弄公尸。广把杖大呼入门，群鬼尽走。广守尸，取猪杀。至夜，见尸边有老鬼，伸手乞肉。广因捉其臂，鬼不得去，持之愈坚。但闻户外有诸鬼共呼云：“老奴贪食至此，甚快。”广语老鬼：“杀公者必是汝，可速还精神，我当放汝；汝若不还者，终不置也。”老鬼曰：“我儿等杀公。”比即唤鬼子：“可还之。”公渐活，因放老鬼。女载棺至，相见惊悲，因取女为妇。 [37]

    Relevant Explanation

    Explanation

    [37]

    Idiomatic Expressions

    • 晋升平末 - Refers to the end of the Jin Ping period, a specific time frame in Chinese history.
    • 故章县老公 - "故" means "former" or "deceased," and "老公" refers to an old man. Together, it likely means a deceased elderly man from the former Zhang County.
    • 余杭□广 - "余杭" is a place name (Yuhang), and "□广" is unclear; it might be a name or a title. The square indicates a missing character.
    • 不许 - A simple negative verb meaning "refused."
    • 穷逼 - A colloquial expression meaning "desperate" or "in a tight spot."
    • 若能往家守父尸 - "若能" means "if you can," "往家" means "to the house," and "守父尸" means "guard the father's body."
    • 便为君妻 - A conditional promise meaning "then I will marry you."
    • 许之 - A simple verb meaning "agreed."
    • 栏中猪 - "栏" means "pen" or "enclosure," and "猪" means "pig." Together, it means "pig in the pen."
    • 可为杀以饴作儿 - "可为" means "can be," "杀" means "killed," "以饴" means "to feed," and "作儿" means "the children." Together, it means "can be killed to feed the children."
    • 披离 - A verb meaning "to enter" or "to intrude."
    • 抃掌欣舞 - A vivid description of clapping and dancing joyfully.
    • 群鬼尽走 - "群鬼" means "a group of ghosts," and "尽走" means "all fled."
    • 比即唤鬼子 - "比即" means "immediately," and "唤鬼子" means "call the ghost's child."
    • 可还之 - A command meaning "return it."
    • 渐活 - Means "gradually revived."
    • 甚快 - A colloquial expression meaning "very happy" or "very pleased."
    • 杀公者必是汝 - "杀公者" means "the one who killed him," and "必是汝" means "must be you."
    • 可速还精神 - "可速" means "quickly," "还" means "return," and "精神" means "spirit." Together, it means "quickly return your spirit."
    • 终不置也 - "终" means "finally," "不置" means "will not let go," and "也" is a particle. Together, it means "finally, I will not let go."
    • 老鬼曰 - "老鬼" means "old ghost," and "曰" means "said."
    • 我儿等杀公 - "我儿等" means "my children," and "杀公" means "killed him."
    • 载棺至 - "载棺" means "carried the coffin," and "至" means "arrived."
    • 相见惊悲 - "相见" means "met," "惊" means "surprised," and "悲" means "sad." Together, it means "met in surprise and sadness."
    • 取女为妇 - "取女" means "took the woman," and "为妇" means "as a wife." Together, it means "took the woman as a wife."

    Cultural and Historical Allusions

    • 晋升平 - The Jin Ping period was a specific era in Chinese history, but the exact dates are not provided in the text.
    • 故章县 - Zhang County is a historical place name, but its exact location is not specified.
    • 余杭 - Yuhang is a well-known ancient city in Zhejiang Province, China.
    • □广 - The missing character makes it difficult to determine the full name or title.
    • 鬼 - Ghosts are a common theme in Chinese folklore and literature, often representing supernatural beings.
    • 棺 - Coffins are used in funerary practices, symbolizing death and mourning.
    • 猪 - Pigs are a common farm animal in Chinese culture and are often used in stories and fables.

    Proper Nouns

    • 晋升平 - The Jin Ping period.
    • 故章县 - Zhang County.
    • 余杭 - Yuhang.
    • □广 - Unclear; likely a name or title.
    • 猪 - Pig.

    During the end of the Jin Ping period, there was an old man from the former Zhang County who had a daughter living deep in the mountains. A man from Yuhang named Guang sought her hand in marriage, but she refused. After the old man fell ill and died, the daughter went to the county to buy a coffin. On her way back, she met Guang halfway. She explained the situation to him in detail. She then said, "I'm in a desperate situation. If you can go to my house and guard my father's body until I return, I will marry you." Guang agreed. The daughter said, "I have a pig in my pen; you can kill it to feed the children." When Guang arrived at the daughter's home, he heard clapping and joyful dancing inside the house. He entered and saw a group of ghosts in the hall, playing with the old man's corpse. Guang grabbed a stick and shouted, causing all the ghosts to flee. He stayed to guard the body and killed the pig. That night, he saw an old ghost by the corpse, reaching out for meat. Guang grabbed the ghost's arm, preventing it from leaving. The ghost held on more tightly. Outside, the other ghosts called out, "Old fool, you're so greedy to come here; it's very amusing." Guang told the old ghost, "The one who killed your father must be you. Quickly return his spirit, and I will let you go; if you don't, I won't let you go." The old ghost said, "My children killed him." Immediately, he called out to his ghostly children, "Return him!" The old man gradually revived, and Guang released the old ghost. The daughter arrived with the coffin, and they met in surprise and sadness. She then took the woman as her wife. [37]

    苻坚时，有射师经嵩山。望见松柏上有一双白鸟，似鹄而大。至树下，又见一蛇，长五丈许，上树取鸟。未至鸟一丈，鸟便欲飞，蛇张口翕之，鸟不得去。缤纷一食顷，鸟转欲困，射师壳弩射三矢，蛇陨而鸟得扬。去树百余步，山边整理毛羽。须臾，云晦雷发，惊耳骇目，射师慑，不得旋踵。见向鸟徘徊其上，毛落纷纷，似如相援。如此数阵，雷息电灭，射师得免，鸟亦高飞。 [38]

    Relevant Explanation

    Explanation

    [38]

    Idiomatic Expressions

    • 為之一空 - Set phrase meaning completely emptied; fields were left barren.

    Proper Nouns

    • 紹興 - Era name of the Southern Song; rendered as Shaoxing.

    During the reign of Fu Jian, there was an archer who passed through Mount Song. He saw a pair of white birds on a pine tree, resembling cranes but larger. Upon reaching the base of the tree, he also saw a snake, about five zhang (approximately 15 meters) long, climbing the tree to catch the birds. The snake was just one zhang away from the birds when they tried to fly, but the snake opened its mouth and swallowed them, preventing them from escaping. After a moment, the birds became increasingly exhausted, and the archer shot three arrows with his crossbow, causing the snake to die and the birds to fly away. The birds flew away from the tree, about a hundred steps distant, and began grooming their feathers on the mountain side. Suddenly, dark clouds gathered and thunder roared, startling both the archer's ears and eyes. Terrified, the archer was frozen in place. He saw the birds hovering above him, their feathers falling in large quantities, as if they were trying to help or support each other. This scene repeated several times, and eventually, the thunder ceased and the lightning disappeared. The archer was saved, and the birds flew high into the sky. [38]

    晋司空桓豁在荆州，有司空翦五月五日鸲鹆舌，教令学语，遂无所不名，与人相问。顾参军善弹琵琶，鸲鹆每立听移时。又善能效人语笑声。司空大会吏佐，令悉效四座语，无不绝似。有生齆鼻，语难学，学之不似，因内头于瓮中以效焉，遂与齆者语声不异。主典人于鸲鹆前盗物，参军如厕，鸲鹆伺无人，密白主典人盗某物，将军衔之而未发。后盗牛肉，鸲鹆复白，参军曰：“汝云盗肉，应有验。”鸲鹆曰：“以新荷裹着屏风后。”检之，果获，痛加治，而盗者患之，以热汤灌杀。参军为之悲伤累日，遂请杀此人，以报其怨。司空教曰：“原杀鸲鹆之痛，诚合治杀，不可以禽鸟故，极之于法。”令止五岁刑也。 [39]

    Relevant Explanation

    Explanation

    [39]

    Idiomatic Expressions

    • 五月五日 - Fifth day of the fifth lunar month, significant in Chinese culture, often associated with the Dragon Boat Festival.
    • 鸲鹆舌 - Literal meaning 'magpie tongue,' refers to a parrot or bird trained to mimic human speech.
    • 學語 - Means 'to learn to speak' or 'to mimic speech.'
    • 與人相問 - Means 'to converse with people.'
    • 效人語笑聲 - Means 'to imitate human speech and laughter.'
    • 悉效四座語 - Means 'to imitate the speech of everyone present.'
    • 齆鼻 - Refers to a person with a stuffy or blocked nose, often used metaphorically to describe someone who is difficult to deal with.
    • 密白 - Means 'to secretly report.'
    • 主典人 - Refers to a supervisor or manager, often a figure of authority in a household or organization.
    • 如厕 - Means 'to go to the toilet.'
    • 內頭於瓮中 - Means 'to put one's head in a jar.'
    • 盜物 - Means 'to steal something.'
    • 痛加治 - Means 'to punish severely.'
    • 患之 - Means 'to worry about it.'
    • 以熱湯灌殺 - Means 'to kill by pouring hot water over it.'
    • 請殺此人 - Means 'to ask to kill this person.'
    • 報其怨 - Means 'to avenge the grudge.'
    • 原殺鸲鹆之痛 - Means 'to consider the pain of killing the parrot.'
    • 極之於法 - Means 'to punish it to the fullest extent of the law.'
    • 五歲刑 - Refers to a five-year sentence.

    Cultural and Historical Allusions

    • 晋司空桓豁 - Refers to Huan Hua, a high-ranking official during the Eastern Jin Dynasty.
    • 司空 - A high-ranking government official, equivalent to a prime minister.
    • 荆州 - A historical region in central China, often a center of political and military power.
    • 五月五日 - A traditional Chinese festival, the Dragon Boat Festival, known for its dragon boat races and customs.
    • 鸲鹆 - A type of parrot or bird known for its ability to mimic human speech.
    • 琵琶 - A traditional Chinese musical instrument, often associated with elegance and refinement.
    • 齆鼻 - A physical condition, often used metaphorically to describe someone who is difficult to deal with.
    • 主典人 - A supervisor or manager, often a figure of authority in a household or organization.
    • 屏风 - A traditional Chinese screen, often used to divide spaces or provide privacy.
    • 法 - Refers to the legal system or laws of the time.

    Proper Nouns

    • 晋司空桓豁 - Huan Hua, a high-ranking official during the Eastern Jin Dynasty.
    • 司空 - A high-ranking government official.
    • 荆州 - A historical region in central China.
    • 五月五日 - The fifth day of the fifth lunar month.
    • 鸲鹆 - A type of parrot or bird.
    • 琵琶 - A traditional Chinese musical instrument.
    • 齆鼻 - A physical condition.
    • 主典人 - A supervisor or manager.
    • 屏风 - A traditional Chinese screen.
    • 法 - The legal system or laws of the time.

    During the Eastern Jin Dynasty, the Prime Minister Huan Hua was stationed in Jing Province. On the fifth day of the fifth lunar month, a parrot with a trained tongue was presented to him. The parrot was taught to mimic human speech and could imitate the speech of everyone present. The parrot was also skilled at imitating human laughter. The Prime Minister held a gathering of his officials and ordered the parrot to mimic the speech of everyone present. The parrot imitated the speech of all the attendees without fail.

    One of the officials, a supervisor, stole something in front of the parrot. The parrot waited for no one to be around and secretly reported the theft to the supervisor. The general, however, held the information without taking action. Later, the supervisor stole some beef, and the parrot again reported the theft. The supervisor, worried about the parrot's report, hid the beef, wrapped in a new lotus leaf, behind a screen. The general, upon learning of the theft, punished the supervisor severely. The supervisor, in turn, killed the parrot by pouring hot water over it.

    The officer, deeply saddened by the parrot's death, eventually asked to kill the thief to avenge the parrot. The Prime Minister, however, considered the pain of killing the parrot and ruled that it would not be just to punish the thief to the fullest extent of the law, citing that the parrot was an animal. The final decision was to impose a five-year sentence on the thief. [39]

    桓冲镇江陵，正会夕当烹牛。牛忽熟视帐下都督甚久，目中泣下。都督咒之曰；“汝若能向我跪者，当启活也。”牛应声而拜，众甚异之。都督复谓曰：“汝若须活，遍拜众人者，直往。”牛涕殒如雨，遂拜不止。值冲醉，不得启，遂杀牛。冲醉止得启，冲闻之叹息，都督痛加鞭罚。
    　　晋桓豹奴为江州时，有甘录事者，家在临川郡治下。儿年十三，遇病死，埋着家东群冢之间。旬日，忽闻东路有打鼓倡乐声，可百许人，径到甘家，问：“录事在否？故来相诣，贤子亦在此。”止闻人声，亦不见其形也。乃出数瓮酒与之，俄顷失去，两瓮皆空。始闻有鼓声，临川太守谓是人戏，必来诣己，既而寂尔不到。甘说之，大惊。
    　　王辅嗣注《易》，辄笑郑玄为儒，云“老奴甚无意。”于时夜分，忽然闻门外阁有着屐声。须臾进，自云郑玄，责之曰：“君年少，何以轻穿文凿句，而妄讥诮老子邪？”极有忿色，言竟便退。辅心生畏恶，经少时，遇厉疾卒。 [40]

    Relevant Explanation

    Explanation

    [40]

    Idiomatic Expressions

    • 桓沖镇江陵 - Defending Jingling or commanding at Jingling.
    • 正会夕当烹牛 - Formal gathering or banquet, evening when a cow was to be cooked.
    • 熟视帐下都督甚久 - To look at carefully for a long time.
    • 目中泣下 - The cow's eyes shedding tears.
    • 汝若能向我跪者，当启活也 - If you can kneel before me, I will request your life.
    • 应声而拜 - Kneeling immediately.
    • 众甚异之 - Found it very strange.
    • 值冲醉，不得启 - When Huán Chōng was drunk, could not make a request.
    • 遂杀牛 - The cow was killed.
    • 晋桓豹奴为江州时 - When he was the governor of Jiangzhou.
    • 有甘录事者 - The name of a person.
    • 儿年十三，遇病死 - Son aged thirteen, died of illness.
    • 埋着家东群冢之间 - Buried among the cemeteries east of his home.
    • 旬日 - Ten days.
    • 闻东路有打鼓倡乐声，可百许人 - Drumming and singing sounds from the eastern road, about a hundred people.
    • 径到甘家 - Went straight to the home of Gan.
    • 问：“录事在否？故来相诣，贤子亦在此。” - Asked, 'Is the recorder here? Came to visit, your talented son is also here.'
    • 止闻人声，亦不见其形也 - Only heard human voices, but did not see their forms.
    • 乃出数瓮酒与之 - Then took out several jars of wine and gave it to them.
    • 俄顷失去，两瓮皆空 - Disappeared in a moment, both jars were empty.
    • 始闻有鼓声，临川太守谓是人戏，必来诣己，既而寂尔不到 - First heard drum sounds, thought it was a human prank, expected them to come to visit, but they eventually did not come.
    • 甘说之，大惊 - Gan told him, and he was greatly surprised.
    • 王辅嗣注《易》，辄笑郑玄为儒，云“老奴甚无意。” - Commented on the *Yi Jing*, often laughed at Zheng Xuan as a scholar, saying, 'This old servant has no intention.'
    • 于时夜分，忽然闻门外阁有着屐声 - At midnight, suddenly heard the sound of wooden clogs outside the gate.
    • 须臾进，自云郑玄 - Soon entered, introduced himself as Zheng Xuan.
    • 责之曰：“君年少，何以轻穿文凿句，而妄讥诮老子邪？” - Rebuked him saying, 'You are young, why do you lightly dissect texts and rashly mock the old man?'
    • 极有忿色，言竟便退 - With great anger, finished speaking and then left.
    • 辅心生畏恶，经少时，遇厉疾卒 - Wang felt fear and disgust, after a short time, suffered from a severe illness and died.

    Cultural and Historical Allusions

    • 桓冲 - A historical figure during the Eastern Jin Dynasty, known for his military and political roles.
    • 江州 - An administrative region in ancient China, roughly corresponding to modern-day Jiangxi Province.
    • 甘录事 - A historical figure, likely a recorder or clerk in the government.
    • 郑玄 - A famous scholar and commentator on the *Yi Jing* during the Han Dynasty.
    • 王辅嗣 - A historical figure, likely a scholar or official who commented on the *Yi Jing*.

    Syntactic Ambiguities

    • This sentence describes a sequence of events: Huán Chōng was commanding at Jingling, a cow was to be cooked at a formal gathering, the cow looked at the subordinate for a long time, and its eyes shed tears.
    • The cow was asked to kneel if it wanted to live, and it knelt immediately, which surprised the crowd.
    • When Huán Chōng was drunk, he could not make a request, so the cow was killed.
    • During the Jin Dynasty, Huán Bào'ér was the governor of Jiangzhou, and there was a recorder named Gan who lived in Linchuan. His son, aged thirteen, died of illness and was buried among the cemeteries east of his home.
    • Ten days later, there was suddenly a sound of drums and singing from the eastern road, about a hundred people, who went straight to Gan's house, asking, 'Is the recorder here? We came to visit, and your talented son is also here.' Only human voices were heard, and their forms were not seen. They then took out several jars of wine and gave it to them, which disappeared in a moment, leaving both jars empty.
    • The governor of Linchuan first heard drum sounds, thought it was a human prank, expected them to come to visit him, but they eventually did not come. Gan told him, and he was greatly surprised.
    • Wang Fusu commented on the *Yi Jing*, often laughed at Zheng Xuan as a scholar, saying, 'This old servant has no intention.' At midnight, suddenly heard the sound of wooden clogs outside the gate. Soon entered, introduced himself as Zheng Xuan. Rebuked him saying, 'You are young, why do you lightly dissect texts and rashly mock the old man?' With great anger, finished speaking and then left. Wang felt fear and disgust, after a short time, suffered from a severe illness and died.

    Huán Chōng, while commanding at Jingling, was preparing to cook a cow at a formal evening gathering. Suddenly, the cow looked at the subordinate standing below it for a long time, and tears welled up in its eyes. The subordinate then said to the cow, "If you can kneel before me, I will request your life." The cow knelt immediately, which surprised the crowd. The subordinate then told the cow, "If you need to live, you must bow to everyone present." The cow wept profusely and continued to kneel. When Huán Chōng was drunk, he could not make a request, so the cow was killed. Huán Chōng, upon waking from his drunkenness, heard about it and sighed, while the subordinate was severely punished with a whip.

    During the Jin Dynasty, when Huán Bào'ér was the governor of Jiangzhou, there was a recorder named Gan who lived in Linchuan. His son, aged thirteen, died of illness and was buried among the cemeteries east of his home. Ten days later, there was suddenly a sound of drums and singing from the eastern road, about a hundred people, who went straight to Gan's house, asking, "Is the recorder here? We came to visit, and your talented son is also here." Only human voices were heard, and their forms were not seen. They then took out several jars of wine and gave it to them, which disappeared in a moment, leaving both jars empty. The governor of Linchuan first heard drum sounds, thought it was a human prank, expected them to come to visit him, but they eventually did not come. Gan told him, and he was greatly surprised.

    Wang Fusu, who commented on the Yi Jing, often laughed at Zheng Xuan as a scholar, saying, "This old servant has no intention." At midnight, he suddenly heard the sound of wooden clogs outside the gate. Soon, a figure entered and introduced himself as Zheng Xuan. Zheng Xuan rebuked Wang Fusu, saying, "You are young, why do you lightly dissect texts and rashly mock the old man?" He spoke with great anger and then left. Wang Fusu felt fear and disgust, and after a short time, he suffered from a severe illness and died. [40]

    谢安石当桓温之世，恒惧不全。夜忽梦乘桓舆行十六里，见一白鸡而止，不得复前，莫有解此梦者。温死后，果代居宰相，历十六年，而得疾。安方悟云：“乘桓舆者，代居其位也；十六里者，得十六年也；见白鸡住者，今太岁在酉，吾病殆将不起乎？”少日而卒。
    　　陈相子，吴兴乌程人，始见佛家经，遂学升霞之术。及在人间斋，辄闻空中殊音妙香，芬芳清越。
    　　安开者，安城之俗巫也，善于幻术。每至祠神时，击鼓宰三牲，积薪然火盛炽，束带入火中，章纸烧尽，而开形体衣服犹如初。时王凝之为江州，伺王当行，阳为王刷头，簪荷叶以为帽，与王着。当是亦不觉帽之有异，到坐之后，荷叶乃见，举坐惊骇，王不知。 [41]

    Relevant Explanation

    Explanation

    [41]

    Idiomatic Expressions

    • 恒惧不全 - Constantly fearing not to be complete or always fearing for his safety.
    • 乘桓舆 - Riding in Huan Wen's carriage, symbolizing taking over his position.
    • 见白鸡而止 - Seeing a white chicken and stopping, a metaphor for the end of his reign.
    • 不得复前 - Unable to proceed further, indicating the end of his term.
    • 代居宰相 - Taking over the position of Chancellor.
    • 历十六年 - Serving for sixteen years.
    • 得疾 - Contracting an illness.
    • 乘桓舆者，代居其位也 - Riding in Huan Wen's carriage means taking over his position.
    • 十六里者，得十六年也 - Sixteen miles represents sixteen years.
    • 见白鸡住者，今太岁在酉，吾病殆将不起乎 - Seeing the white chicken stop means that the current year is the Year of the Rooster, and my illness will likely not recover.
    • 少日而卒 - Dying shortly after.
    • 学升霞之术 - Learning the technique of ascending to the celestial realms.
    • 在人间斋 - Practicing fasting in the world.
    • 殊音妙香 - Unusual sounds and fragrant aromas.
    • 芬芳清越 - Sweet and clear fragrances.
    • 善于幻术 - Skilled in magic.
    • 击鼓宰三牲 - Beating drums and sacrificing three animals.
    • 积薪然火盛炽 - Piling up firewood and setting it ablaze, creating intense flames.
    • 束带入火中 - Binding oneself and entering the fire.
    • 章纸烧尽 - The paper burning completely.
    • 开形体衣服犹如初 - His body and clothes remained unchanged.
    • 王凝之为江州 - Wang Ningzhi served as the governor of Jiangzhou.
    • 伺王当行 - Waiting for Wang to go somewhere.
    • 阳为王刷头 - Acting as if brushing Wang's head.
    • 簪荷叶以为帽 - Placing a lotus leaf as a hat.
    • 与王着 - Giving it to Wang.
    • 当是亦不觉帽之有异 - Wang did not notice anything unusual about the hat.
    • 到坐之后 - After arriving at the seat.
    • 荷叶乃见 - The lotus leaf became visible.
    • 举坐惊骇 - Everyone was shocked.
    • 王不知 - Wang did not know.

    Cultural and Historical Allusions

    • 谢安石 - Xie An, a key figure in the Eastern Jin Dynasty, known for his political and military prowess.
    • 桓温 - Huan Wen, a powerful general and politician who sought to usurp the throne.
    • 宰相 - Chancellor, a high-ranking official in ancient Chinese government.
    • 太岁在酉 - The Year of the Rooster, according to the Chinese zodiac.
    • 陈相子 - Chen Xiangzi, a historical figure known for his practice of Taoist alchemy.
    • 吴兴乌程人 - Wucheng, a place in the Wuxing region, known for its cultural significance.
    • 佛家经 - Buddhist scriptures, which were introduced to China during the Han Dynasty.
    • 升霞之术 - Techniques for ascending to the celestial realms, a practice associated with Taoist alchemy.
    • 安开者 - An Kai, a folk magician known for his illusions.
    • 祠神时 - Worship of deities, a common practice in ancient Chinese religion.
    • 击鼓宰三牲 - Sacrificing animals as part of religious rituals.
    • 积薪然火盛炽 - Piling up firewood and setting it ablaze, a common method of creating intense flames.
    • 束带入火中 - Binding oneself and entering the fire, a dramatic act often associated with magic.
    • 章纸烧尽 - The paper burning completely, indicating the completion of a ritual.
    • 开形体衣服犹如初 - His body and clothes remained unchanged, suggesting the magician's supernatural abilities.
    • 王凝之为江州 - Wang Ningzhi, a historical figure who served as the governor of Jiangzhou.
    • 荷叶以为帽 - Placing a lotus leaf as a hat, a unique and symbolic act.

    Proper Nouns

    • 谢安石 - Xie An
    • 桓温 - Huan Wen
    • 陈相子 - Chen Xiangzi
    • 吴兴乌程人 - Wucheng, Wuxing
    • 佛家经 - Buddhist scriptures
    • 安开者 - An Kai
    • 王凝之 - Wang Ningzhi
    • 江州 - Jiangzhou

    Xie An, during the time of Huan Wen, constantly feared for his safety. One night, he suddenly dreamed of riding in Huan Wen's carriage for sixteen miles, seeing a white chicken and stopping, unable to proceed further. No one could interpret this dream. After Huan Wen's death, Xie An indeed took over the position of Chancellor, serving for sixteen years before falling ill. At this point, Xie An realized, "Riding in Huan Wen's carriage means taking over his position; sixteen miles represent sixteen years; seeing the white chicken stop means that the current year is the Year of the Rooster, and my illness will likely not recover." He died shortly after.

    Chen Xiangzi, from Wucheng, Wuxing, first encountered Buddhist scriptures and then began to learn the technique of ascending to the celestial realms. When he practiced fasting in the world, he would often hear unusual sounds and fragrant aromas in the air, sweet and clear.

    An Kai, a folk magician from Ancheng, was skilled in magic. Every time during the worship of deities, he would beat drums and sacrifice three animals, pile up firewood and set it ablaze, creating intense flames. He would bind himself and enter the fire, and when the paper burned completely, his body and clothes remained unchanged. At the time, Wang Ningzhi served as the governor of Jiangzhou. An Kai waited for Wang to go somewhere and acted as if brushing his head, placing a lotus leaf as a hat and giving it to Wang. Wang did not notice anything unusual about the hat. After arriving at the seat, the lotus leaf became visible, and everyone was shocked. Wang did not know. [41]

    晋左军琅邪王凝之夫人谢氏，顿亡二男，痛惜过甚，衔泪六年。后忽见二儿俱还，并着械，慰其母曰：“可自割，儿并有罪谪，宜为作福。”于是得止哀，而勤为求请。
    　　晋世王彪之，年少未官。尝独坐斋中，前有竹，忽闻有叹声，彪之惕然，怪似其母，因往看之，见母衣服如昔。彪之跪拜歔欷，母曰：“汝方有奇厄，自今已去。当日见一白狗，若能东行出千里，三年，然后可得免灾。”忽不复见。彪之悲怅达旦。既明，独见一白狗，恒随行止。便经营行装，将往会稽。及出千里外，所见便萧然都尽。过三年乃归，斋中复闻前声，往见母如先，谓曰：“能用吾言，故来庆汝。汝自今已后，年逾八十，位班台司。”后皆如母言。 [42]

    Relevant Explanation

    Explanation

    [42]

    Idiomatic Expressions

    • 頓亡二男 - Literal meaning: 'suddenly lost two sons,' indicating a sudden and tragic loss.
    • 痛惜過甚 - Literal meaning: 'grieved excessively,' emphasizing the depth of her sorrow.
    • 衔泪六年 - Literal meaning: 'held tears for six years,' indicating prolonged mourning.
    • 可自割 - Literal meaning: 'you must make a sacrifice,' suggesting a difficult decision.
    • 儿并有罪谪 - Literal meaning: 'both sons have committed sins and been exiled,' indicating their punishment.
    • 勤为求请 - Literal meaning: 'diligently make requests,' suggesting persistent efforts to help them.
    • 年少未官 - Literal meaning: 'young and not yet in office,' indicating his early stage in life and career.
    • 尝独坐斋中 - Literal meaning: 'once sat alone in his study,' indicating a moment of solitude.
    • 前有竹 - Literal meaning: 'there was bamboo in front,' indicating the setting.
    • 惕然 - Literal meaning: 'startled,' indicating a sudden feeling of fear or surprise.
    • 怪似其母 - Literal meaning: 'seemed like his mother,' indicating a resemblance.
    • 年逾八十 - Literal meaning: 'over eighty years old,' indicating advanced age.
    • 位班台司 - Literal meaning: 'hold a high position in the government,' indicating a high-ranking official.

    Cultural and Historical Allusions

    • 晋左军琅邪王凝之夫人谢氏 - Refers to Lady Xie, the wife of Wang Ningzhi, a nobleman during the Eastern Jin Dynasty.
    • 晋世王彪之 - Refers to Wang Biao, a prominent official during the Eastern Jin Dynasty.
    • 白狗 - In Chinese folklore, a white dog often symbolizes a messenger or a supernatural being.
    • 会稽 - Refers to Kuaiji, an ancient city in Zhejiang Province, known for its cultural and historical significance.

    Proper Nouns

    • 晋左军琅邪王凝之夫人谢氏 - Lady Xie, wife of Wang Ningzhi.
    • 晋世王彪之 - Wang Biao.
    • 会稽 - Kuaiji, an ancient city in Zhejiang Province.
    • 白狗 - A white dog, often symbolizing a messenger or a supernatural being in Chinese folklore.

    Lady Xie, the wife of Wang Ningzhi, the Left Army Commander of Langya, suddenly lost two sons, grieving excessively for six years. Later, she suddenly saw both sons return, wearing shackles, and comforted her mother, saying, "You must make a sacrifice; both sons have committed sins and been exiled; it is appropriate to pray for their welfare." Thus, she ceased her mourning and diligently made requests for their well-being.

    During the Eastern Jin Dynasty, Wang Biao was young and not yet in office. Once, he sat alone in his study, with bamboo in front of him. Suddenly, he heard a sigh, startling him, and it seemed like his mother. He went to see her, and found her dressed as she was before. Wang Biao knelt and wept, and his mother said, "You will soon face a great misfortune. From now on, you will see a white dog. If you can travel east for a thousand miles and stay there for three years, then you can avoid disaster." She suddenly disappeared. Wang Biao was filled with sorrow until dawn. When it was light, he saw a white dog following him constantly. He then prepared his travel gear and headed to Kuaiji. After traveling a thousand miles, what he saw vanished completely. Three years later, he returned, and in his study, he heard the same sound again. He went to see his mother, who was as she was before, and she said, "Because you followed my words, I have come to congratulate you. From now on, you will live to be over eighty and hold a high position in the government." All these events happened as his mother had predicted. [42]

    晋海西公时，有一人母终，家贫，无以葬。因移柩深山，于其侧志孝结坟，昼夜不休。将暮，有一妇人抱儿来寄宿。转夜，孝子未作竟，妇人每求眠，而于火边睡，乃是一狸抱一乌鸡。孝子因打杀，掷后坑中。明日，有男子来问：“细小昨行，遇夜寄宿，今为何在？”孝子云：“止有一狸，即已杀之。”男子曰：“君枉杀吾妇，何得言狸？狸今何在？”因共至坑视，狸已成妇人，死在坑中。男子因缚孝子付官，应偿死。孝子乃谓令曰：“此实妖魅，但出猎犬，则可知魅。”令因问猎事：“能别犬否？”答云：“性畏犬，亦不别也。”因放犬，便化为老狸，则射杀。视之，妇人已还成狸。 [43]

    Relevant Explanation

    Explanation

    [43]

    Idiomatic Expressions

    • 晉海西公時 - Refers to the reign of Emperor Hai of Jin, a specific historical period.
    • 移柩深山 - Literally 'move the coffin into a deep mountain,' indicating the act of relocating a body to a remote location.
    • 志孝結坟 - A compound phrase meaning 'to build a tomb with filial piety,' emphasizing the moral duty of the son.
    • 轉夜 - Means 'through the night,' indicating a continuous period of activity.
    • 因打殺，擲後坑中 - Literally 'therefore he beat and killed it, throwing it into a pit behind,' describing the son's actions.
    • 應償死 - Means 'should be punished with death,' indicating the legal consequence.
    • 此實妖魅 - Means 'this is indeed a demon,' identifying the nature of the creature.
    • 但出猎犬，则可知魅 - Means 'but if a hunting dog is brought out, then the demon can be identified,' suggesting a method to detect the demon.
    • 因共至坑視 - Literally 'therefore they went together to the pit to look,' describing the action of investigating the pit.
    • 視之，婦人已還成狸 - Literally 'looking at it, the woman had returned to being a fox,' indicating the transformation back to its original form.

    Cultural and Historical Allusions

    • 晉海西公 - Refers to Emperor Hai of Jin, who ruled during the Eastern Jin dynasty.
    • 狸 - A common animal in Chinese folklore, often associated with trickery and transformation.
    • 烏雞 - A type of chicken, possibly used to add detail to the story.
    • 孝子 - Refers to a son who is filially pious, emphasizing the moral duty of caring for one's parents.
    • 男子 - Refers to a man, possibly a local or a passerby.
    • 令 - Refers to a local official or magistrate, who has the authority to judge and punish.
    • 獵犬 - Refers to hunting dogs, which are known for their ability to detect and track animals.

    Proper Nouns

    • 晉海西公 - Emperor Hai of Jin.
    • 孝子 - The filial son.
    • 男子 - The man who questions the son.
    • 令 - The local official or magistrate.

    During the reign of Emperor Hai of Jin, there was a man whose mother had passed away. He was poor and could not afford a proper burial. Therefore, he moved her coffin to a deep mountain and built a tomb with filial piety, working day and night without rest. As evening approached, a woman carrying a child came to ask for shelter. Throughout the night, the son continued his work, and the woman often asked to sleep, lying by the fire. She turned out to be a fox holding a chicken. The son, in anger, beat and killed them, throwing them into a pit behind the tomb. The next morning, a man came to ask, "Where are the small people who stayed overnight?" The son replied, "I saw only a fox and a chicken, and I killed them." The man said, "You wrongfully killed my wife. How can you call her a fox? Where is the fox now?" They then went together to the pit to look, and they found the fox had transformed back into a woman, dead in the pit. The man tied up the son and handed him over to the official, who said he should be punished with death. The son then told the official, "This is indeed a demon. But if a hunting dog is brought out, then the demon can be identified." The official asked about hunting dogs: "Can you distinguish them?" The son replied, "They are afraid of dogs, so they cannot be distinguished." The official then let out a hunting dog, and the fox transformed into an old fox, which was shot and killed. When they looked at it, the woman had returned to being a fox. [43]

    桓温北征姚襄，在伊水上，许逊曰：“不见得襄而有大功，见襄走入太玄中。”问曰：“太玄是何等也？”答曰：“南为丹野，北为太玄，必西北走也。”果如其言。
    　　桓大司马镇赭圻时，有何参军晨出，行于田野中，溺死人髑髅上。还昼寝，梦一妇人语云：“君是佳人，何以见秽污？暮当令知之！”是时有暴虎，人无敢行夜出者，何常穴壁作溺穴。其夜，趋穴欲溺，虎怒溺，断阴茎，即死。
    　　桓温内怀无君之心，时比丘尼从远来，夏五月，尼在别室浴，温窃窥之。见尼裸身，先以刀自破腹，出五藏，次断两足，及斩头手。有顷浴竟，温问：“向窥见尼，何得自残毁如此？”尼云：“公作天子，亦当如是。”温惆怅不悦。
    　　陈郡袁真在豫州，送妓女阿薛、阿郭、阿马三人与桓宣武。至经时，三人共出庭前覌望，见一流星，直堕盆水中。薛、郭二人更以瓢取，皆不得；阿马最后取星，正入瓢中。使饮之，即觉有妊，遂生桓玄。 [44]

    Relevant Explanation

    Explanation

    [44]

    Idiomatic Expressions

    • 桓温北征姚襄 - Refers to the northern expedition led by Huan Wen against Yao Xiang.
    • 伊水上 - Specifies the location as the Yi River.
    • 不见得襄而有大功 - Means 'without seeing Yao Xiang, there will be great merit.'
    • 见襄走入太玄中 - Means 'if you see Yao Xiang, he will enter the Tai Xuan.'
    • 南为丹野，北为太玄 - Describes the directions as 'south as Dan Ye' and 'north as Tai Xuan.'
    • 西北走也 - Means 'he will head northwest.'
    • 果如其言 - Means 'as he said.'
    • 桓大司马镇赭圻时 - Refers to the time when Huan Wen, the Grand Marshal, was stationed at Zheqi.
    • 何参军晨出，行于田野中 - Describes He, the military officer, going out in the morning and walking in the fields.
    • 溺死人髑髅上 - Means 'he drowned on a dead man's skull.'
    • 还昼寝 - Means 'he returned to sleep during the day.'
    • 梦一妇人语云 - Means 'he dreamed of a woman who said.'
    • 君是佳人，何以见秽污 - Means 'You are a fine person, why do you see filth?'
    • 暮当令知之 - Means 'Tonight I will make you know.'
    • 暴虎 - Refers to a wild tiger.
    • 人无敢行夜出者 - Means 'no one dared to go out at night.'
    • 何常穴壁作溺穴 - Means 'He often made a hole in the wall for urination.'
    • 趋穴欲溺 - Means 'he rushed to the hole to urinate.'
    • 虎怒溺 - Means 'the tiger was angry and urinated.'
    • 断阴茎，即死 - Means 'it severed his penis, and he died.'
    • 桓温内怀无君之心 - Means 'Huan Wen harbored a heart without a ruler.'
    • 比丘尼从远来 - Refers to a nun who came from afar.
    • 夏五月 - Specifies the time as the fifth month of summer.
    • 尼在别室浴 - Means 'the nun was bathing in a separate room.'
    • 温窃窥之 - Means 'Huan Wen secretly peeped at her.'
    • 见尼裸身，先以刀自破腹，出五藏，次断两足，及斩头手 - Means 'he saw the nun naked, first cutting open her abdomen to remove her five organs, then severing her two feet, and finally chopping off her head and hands.'
    • 有顷浴竟 - Means 'after a while, she finished bathing.'
    • 向窥见尼，何得自残毁如此 - Means 'Why did you see me and destroy yourself like this?'
    • 公作天子，亦当如是 - Means 'If you become an emperor, you will do the same.'
    • 惆怅不悦 - Means 'he was sad and unhappy.'
    • 陈郡袁真在豫州 - Refers to Yuan Zhen of Chen, who was in Yuzhou.
    • 送妓女阿薛、阿郭、阿马三人与桓宣武 - Means 'he sent three courtesans, A Xue, A Guo, and A Ma, to Huan Xuanwu.'
    • 至经时 - Means 'after some time.'
    • 三人共出庭前观望 - Means 'the three women went out to the courtyard to look around.'
    • 见一流星，直堕盆水中 - Means 'they saw a shooting star fall directly into a basin of water.'
    • 薛、郭二人更以瓢取，皆不得 - Means 'Xue and Guo tried to scoop it with a ladle but failed.'
    • 阿马最后取星，正入瓢中 - Means 'A Ma scooped it last, and it fell right into the ladle.'
    • 使饮之，即觉有妊，遂生桓玄 - Means 'she made her drink it, and she immediately felt pregnant, eventually giving birth to Huan Xuan.'

    Cultural and Historical Allusions

    • 桓温 - A prominent general and politician of the Eastern Jin Dynasty.
    • 姚襄 - A leader of the Xiongnu people who rebelled against the Eastern Jin.
    • 伊水 - A river in Henan Province.
    • 太玄 - A term that can refer to a place or a state of being, often associated with the *Tai Xuan Jing* (Book of the Supreme Mystery).
    • 丹野 - A place name, possibly referring to a specific region.
    • 赭圻 - A place name, likely a military post or fort.
    • 暴虎 - A wild tiger, often used metaphorically to describe dangerous situations.
    • 比丘尼 - A nun in Buddhism.
    • 桓宣武 - Another name for Huan Wen, emphasizing his role as a general.
    • 陈郡袁真 - Yuan Zhen, a military official from Chen County.
    • 豫州 - A region in central China, often a strategic area during the Jin Dynasty.
    • 妓女 - Courtesans or entertainers.
    • 桓玄 - A later figure, son of Huan Xuanwu, who became a significant historical figure.

    Proper Nouns

    • 桓温 - Huan Wen, a general and politician.
    • 姚襄 - Yao Xiang, a rebel leader.
    • 伊水 - Yi River.
    • 太玄 - Tai Xuan.
    • 丹野 - Dan Ye.
    • 赭圻 - Zheqi.
    • 暴虎 - Wild tiger.
    • 比丘尼 - Nun.
    • 桓宣武 - Huan Wen, another name.
    • 陈郡袁真 - Yuan Zhen, a military official.
    • 豫州 - Yuzhou.
    • 妓女 - Courtesans.
    • 桓玄 - Huan Xuan, a significant historical figure.

    Huan Wen, in his northern campaign against Yao Xiang, was at the Yi River. Xu Xun said, "Without seeing Yao Xiang, there will be great merit; if you see Yao Xiang, he will enter the Tai Xuan." Asked, "What is Tai Xuan?" he replied, "South is Dan Ye, north is Tai Xuan, and he will surely head northwest." Indeed, as he said.

    When Huan Wen, the Grand Marshal, was stationed at Zheqi, one of his military officers, He, went out in the morning and walked in the fields, where he drowned on a dead man's skull. He returned to sleep during the day and dreamed of a woman who said, "You are a fine person, why do you see filth? Tonight I will make you know!" At the time, there was a wild tiger, and no one dared to go out at night. He often made a hole in the wall for urination. That night, he rushed to the hole to urinate, and the tiger, angered by this, urinated and severed his penis, causing him to die.

    Huan Wen harbored a heart without a ruler. At that time, a nun came from afar. In the fifth month of summer, the nun was bathing in a separate room, and Huan Wen secretly peeped at her. He saw the nun naked, first cutting open her abdomen to remove her five organs, then severing her two feet, and finally chopping off her head and hands. After a while, she finished bathing, and Huan Wen asked, "Why did you see me and destroy yourself like this?" The nun replied, "If you become an emperor, you will do the same." Huan Wen was sad and unhappy.

    Yuan Zhen of Chen, while in Yuzhou, sent three courtesans, A Xue, A Guo, and A Ma, to Huan Xuanwu. After some time, the three women went out to the courtyard to look around and saw a shooting star fall directly into a basin of water. Xue and Guo tried to scoop it with a ladle but failed. A Ma scooped it last, and it fell right into the ladle. She made her drink it, and she immediately felt pregnant, eventually giving birth to Huan Xuan. [44]

    习凿齿为荆州主簿，从桓宣武出猎，见黄物，射之，即死，是老雄狐，臂带绛绫香囊。
    　　桓大司马温时，有参军夜坐，忽见屋梁栋间，有一伏兔，张目切齿而向之，甚可畏。兔来转近，遂引刀而斫之，见正中兔，而实反伤其膝，流血滂沱。深怪此意，命家中悉藏刀刃，不以自近。后忽复见如前，意回惑，复索刀重斫，因伤委顿。幸刀不利，故不至死，再过而止。
    　　顾长康在江陵爱一女子，还家，长康思之不已，乃画作女形，簪着壁上。簪处正刺心，女行十里，忽心痛如刺，不能进。
    　　刘琮善弹琴，忽得困病，许逊曰：“近见蒋家女鬼相录在山石间，专使弹琴作乐，恐欲致灾也。”琮曰：“吾常梦见女子将吾宴戏，恐必不免。”逊笑曰：“蒋姑相爱重，恐不能相放耳。已为诔之，今去，当无患也。”琮渐差。 [45]

    Relevant Explanation

    Explanation

    [45]

    Idiomatic Expressions

    • 荆州主簿 - Title for an official position in the state of Jingzhou, indicating administrative or clerical duties.
    • 桓宣武 - Title for a military leader, likely referring to a general or governor, possibly a historical figure.
    • 黄物 - Vague term meaning 'yellow thing,' possibly referring to a creature or object with a yellow color.
    • 老雄狐 - Male fox, likely old and experienced.
    • 臂带绛绫香囊 - Red silk pouch worn on the arm, suggesting a decorative or functional item.
    • 大司马温 - Title for a high-ranking military official, possibly referring to a historical figure.
    • 参军 - Military officer or assistant, typically in a subordinate role.
    • 伏兔 - Rabbit-like creature, possibly a mythological or supernatural being.
    • 张目切齿而向之 - Creature opens its eyes and bares its teeth, indicating aggression or hostility.
    • 深怪此意 - Person finds the situation deeply strange or unsettling.
    • 悉藏刀刃 - To hide all knives and blades, indicating a desire to avoid further conflict.
    • 顾长康 - Person's name, possibly a historical or literary figure.
    • 簪着壁上 - To pin or attach something to the wall, likely referring to a hairpin or similar object.
    • 簪处正刺心 - Pin is positioned directly over the heart, suggesting a symbolic or literal threat.
    • 刘琮 - Person's name, possibly a historical or literary figure.
    • 许逊 - Person's name, possibly a historical or literary figure.
    • 蒋家女鬼 - Female ghost from the Jiang family, indicating a supernatural or ghostly presence.
    • 专使弹琴作乐 - Specifically tasked with playing the zither and making music.
    • 恐欲致灾也 - Worried that it might cause disaster or harm.
    • 吾常梦见女子将吾宴戏 - Often dream of a woman entertaining me, suggesting a recurring dream or memory.
    • 蒋姑相爱重 - Jiang family aunt loves and respects me, indicating a familial relationship.
    • 诔之 - To compose a eulogy or lament, suggesting a formal act of mourning or remembrance.
    • 渐差 - Gradually improve or recover, indicating a process of healing or recovery.

    Cultural and Historical Allusions

    • 荆州 - Historical region in China, known for its strategic importance.
    • 桓宣武 - Likely refers to a historical figure, possibly a military leader or governor.
    • 大司马温 - High-ranking military official, possibly a historical figure.
    • 顾长康 - Person's name, possibly a historical or literary figure.
    • 刘琮 - Person's name, possibly a historical or literary figure.
    • 许逊 - Person's name, possibly a historical or literary figure.
    • 蒋家女鬼 - Female ghost from the Jiang family, indicating a supernatural or ghostly presence.

    Proper Nouns

    • 习凿齿 - Person's name, possibly a historical or literary figure.
    • 桓宣武 - Title for a military leader, possibly a historical figure.
    • 大司马温 - Title for a high-ranking military official, possibly a historical figure.
    • 顾长康 - Person's name, possibly a historical or literary figure.
    • 刘琮 - Person's name, possibly a historical or literary figure.
    • 许逊 - Person's name, possibly a historical or literary figure.
    • 蒋家女鬼 - Female ghost from the Jiang family.

    Xizaochi, serving as an official in Jingzhou, accompanied General Huan Xuanwu on a hunting trip. They encountered a yellow creature, which he shot and killed. Upon closer inspection, it turned out to be an old male fox wearing a red silk pouch.

    During the time of Grand Marshal Wen, a military officer was sitting alone at night when he suddenly saw a rabbit-like creature in the beams and rafters of his house. The creature opened its eyes and bared its teeth, appearing very menacing. As the rabbit approached, the officer drew his knife and struck it, believing he had hit the creature. However, he actually injured his own knee, causing him to bleed profusely. Deeply unsettled by this, he ordered all knives and blades to be hidden and kept away from himself. Later, he saw the same creature again and, feeling confused, drew his knife once more and struck it, resulting in further injury and exhaustion. Fortunately, the knife was not sharp enough to cause serious harm, and the incident ended there.

    Gu Changkang, while in Jiangling, fell in love with a woman. After returning home, he could not stop thinking about her and decided to draw her likeness, pinning it to the wall. The pin was positioned directly over the heart. The woman, upon seeing this, felt a sharp pain in her chest and could not continue walking for ten li.

    Liu Cong was skilled at playing the zither but suddenly fell ill. Xu Xun said, "I recently saw a ghost from the Jiang family in a cave, who was specifically tasked with playing the zither and making music. I fear it might bring disaster." Liu replied, "I often dream of a woman entertaining me, and I fear I cannot escape this fate." Xu laughed and said, "The Jiang family aunt loves and respects you, and she will not let you go. I have already composed a eulogy for her, and now she has left, so there should be no more trouble." Liu gradually recovered from his illness. [45]

    陶公在寻阳西南一塞取鱼，自谓其池曰“鹤门。”
    　　许逊少孤，不识祖墓，倾心所感，忽见祖语曰：“我死三十余年，于今得正葬，是汝孝悌之至。”因举标榜曰：“可以此下求我。”于是迎丧，葬者曰：“此墓中当出一侯及小县长。”
    　　桂阳罗君章，二十许，都未有意，不属意学问。常昼寝，梦得一鸟卵，五色杂耀，不似人间物，梦中因取吞之。于是渐有志向。遂勤学，读九经，以清才闻。
    　　桓玄时，牛大疫，有一人食死牛肉，因得病亡。死时，见人执录，将至天上，有一贵人问云：“此人何罪？”对曰：“此人坐食疫死牛肉。”贵人云：“今须牛以转输，既不能肉以充百姓食，何故复杀之？”催令还。既更生，具说其言。于是食牛肉者，无复有患。 [46]

    Relevant Explanation

    Explanation

    [46]

    Idiomatic Expressions

    • 孝悌之至 - Refers to the utmost filial piety and fraternal respect.
    • 清才 - Means excellent talent.

    Cultural and Historical Allusions

    • 陶公 - Tao Kan, a famous general and statesman of the Eastern Jin dynasty.
    • 許逊 - Xu Xun, a historical figure known for his filial piety and later deified as a deity in Chinese folk religion.
    • 桂阳罗君章 - Luo Junzhang, a historical figure whose story illustrates the power of dreams and divine intervention in shaping one's destiny.
    • 桓玄 - Huan Xuan, a powerful warlord who briefly usurped the throne of the Eastern Jin dynasty.

    Proper Nouns

    • 陶公 - Tao Kan
    • 許逊 - Xu Xun
    • 桂阳罗君章 - Luo Junzhang
    • 桓玄 - Huan Xuan

    Tao Kan was in a dam in the southwest of Xunyang, where he caught fish. He called his pond "Crane Gate."
    Xu Xun lost his father at a young age and did not know the location of his ancestral tomb. Deeply moved by his heart, he suddenly saw his ancestor say, "I have been dead for more than thirty years, and now I can be buried properly, thanks to your utmost filial piety and fraternal respect." The ancestor then pointed and said, "You can find me here." Xu Xun then arranged for the burial, and the one who buried him said, "This tomb will produce a marquis and a small county official."
    Luo Junzhang of Guiyang, around twenty years old, had no interest in learning. One day, while napping, he dreamed of obtaining a bird's egg with various colors shining together, unlike anything in the world. In the dream, he took and swallowed it. From then on, he gradually developed aspirations. He then studied diligently, read the Nine Classics, and became known for his excellent talent.
    During the reign of Huan Xuan, there was a great epidemic among cattle. A man ate the meat of a dead cow and subsequently fell ill and died. At the time of his death, he saw someone taking and recording him, bringing him to heaven. A noble person asked, "What is this man's crime?" The response was, "This man ate the meat of a cow that died from the epidemic." The noble person said, "Now we need cattle for transportation, and we cannot eat their meat to feed the people. Why did you kill it?" The man was then sent back. After he was revived, he recounted the words. From then on, those who ate beef had no more problems. [46]

    吴北寺终祚道人卧斋中，鼠从坎出，言终祚后数日必当死。终祚呼奴令买犬，鼠云：“亦不畏此也。但令犬入此户，必死。”犬至，果然。终祚乃下声语其奴曰：“明日市雇十担水来。”鼠已逆知之，云：“止！欲水浇取我？我穴周流，无所不至。”竟日浇灌，了无所获。密令奴更借三十余人，鼠云：“吾上屋居，奈我何？”至时，处在屋上。奴名周，鼠云：“阿周盗二十万钱叛。”后试开库，实如所言也。奴亦叛去。终祚当为商贾，闭其户而谓鼠曰：“汝正欲使我富耳！今有远行，勤守吾房中，勿令有所零失也。”时桓温在南州禁杀牛，甚急。终祚载数万钱，窃买牛皮还东。货之，得二十万。还，室犹闭，一无所失，其怪亦绝。遂大富。 [47]

    Relevant Explanation

    Explanation

    [47]

    Idiomatic Expressions

    • 終祚道人 - Refers to a monk named Zongzu, likely a historical or fictional figure.
    • 從坎出 - Literally means 'emerged from a hole,' used here to describe the mouse's appearance.
    • 市雇十担水来 - Means 'buy ten loads of water,' a command given to the servant.
    • 密令奴更借三十余人 - Means 'secretly ordered the servant to hire more than thirty people,' indicating a covert action.
    • 阿周盗二十万钱叛 - Means 'Ah Zhou stole twenty thousand coins and defected,' a statement about the servant's betrayal.
    • 闭其户而谓鼠曰 - Means 'closed his door and said to the mouse,' indicating a private conversation.
    • 桓温在南州禁杀牛 - Means 'Huan Wen in Nan Zhou banned the killing of oxen,' referring to a historical figure and event.
    • 窃买牛皮还东 - Means 'secretly bought ox hides and returned east,' indicating a covert transaction.
    • 竟日浇灌，了无所获 - Means 'all day long pouring water, but without any result,' describing a futile effort.
    • 遂大富 - Means 'thus became very wealthy,' indicating a significant outcome.

    Cultural and Historical Allusions

    • 终祚道人 - A monk named Zongzu, possibly a historical figure or a character in a story.
    • 桓温 - A prominent historical figure during the Eastern Jin dynasty, known for his military and political influence.
    • 南州 - Refers to the southern region, likely the area where Huan Wen was active.
    • 禁杀牛 - A ban on killing oxen, which was a common practice in ancient China due to the importance of oxen in agriculture and labor.
    • 牛皮 - Ox hides, which were valuable for making various items such as leather goods and armor.
    • 东 - Refers to the eastern region, likely the area where Zongzu lived or conducted business.
    • 大富 - Becoming very wealthy, indicating a significant economic success.

    Proper Nouns

    • 终祚道人 - Monk Zongzu.
    • 桓温 - Huan Wen.
    • 南州 - Southern region.
    • 东 - Eastern region.
    • 阿周 - Servant Zhou.

    Monk Zongzu of Wu Bei Temple was lying in his study when a mouse emerged from a hole and predicted that Zongzu would die within a few days. Zongzu called his servant and ordered him to buy a dog. The mouse said, "I don't fear this either. But if you let the dog enter this house, it will die." The dog arrived, and as predicted, Zongzu died. Zongzu then whispered to his servant, "Tomorrow, buy ten loads of water." The mouse had anticipated this and said, "Stop! Do you want to pour water on me? My holes are everywhere, and I can move freely." They poured water all day long but found nothing. Zongzu secretly ordered the servant to hire more than thirty people, but the mouse said, "I live on the roof; what can you do to me?" When the time came, the mouse was indeed on the roof. The servant's name was Zhou, and the mouse said, "Ah Zhou stole twenty thousand coins and defected." Later, when they checked the treasury, it was found to be true. The servant also defected. Zongzu decided to become a merchant, closed his door, and told the mouse, "You want me to become rich! Now I have a long journey, so guard my room well and do not let anything be lost." At that time, Huan Wen in Nan Zhou had a strict ban on killing oxen. Zongzu secretly bought ox hides and returned east. He sold the ox hides and made twenty thousand coins. Upon returning, his room was still closed, and nothing was missing; the strange occurrences ceased. Thus, he became very wealthy. [47]

    桓玄既肆无君之心，使御史害太傅道子于安城。玄在南州坐，忽见一平上帻人，持马鞭，通云：“蒋侯来。”玄惊愕然，便见阶下奴子御幰车，见一士大夫，自云是蒋子文：“君何以害太傅？与为伯仲。”顾视之间，便不复见。
    　　桓玄在南郡国第居时，出诣殷荆州，于鹄穴逢一老公，驱一青牛，形色瑰异，桓即以所乘马易牛。乘至零陵溪，牛忽骏驶非常。因息驾饮牛，牛径入水不出。桓遣人觇守，经日绝迹也。 [48]

    Relevant Explanation

    Explanation

    [48]

    Idiomatic Expressions

    • 肆无君之心 - Phrase meaning 'to act with disregard for authority' or 'to behave without respect for the sovereign'; suggests a bold or rebellious attitude.
    • 御史 - Refers to an official in charge of inspection and punishment, often with judicial powers.
    • 害 - Here it means 'to harm' or 'to kill,' specifically in the context of an assassination.
    • 顾视之间 - Phrase meaning 'in the blink of an eye' or 'in a moment'; emphasizes the brief duration of the encounter.
    • 径入水不出 - Phrase meaning 'to go straight into the water and not come out'; indicates the ox's sudden and mysterious disappearance.

    Cultural and Historical Allusions

    • 桓玄 - A prominent figure in the late Eastern Jin dynasty, known for his ambition and eventual rebellion against the imperial court.
    • 蒋子文 - A legendary figure from the Shang dynasty, often associated with the founding of Jiangning (modern-day Nanjing) and known for his wisdom and military prowess.
    • 零陵溪 - A specific location, possibly a river or stream in the region.

    Proper Nouns

    • 桓玄 - A historical figure and political leader.
    • 御史 - An official title, often translated as 'censor' or 'inspector'.
    • 太傅道子 - A high-ranking official and teacher of the emperor.
    • 安城 - A place name, possibly a specific location.
    • 蒋子文 - A legendary figure from the Shang dynasty.
    • 殷荆州 - A governor of Jingzhou.
    • 南郡国第 - A place name, possibly a residence.
    • 零陵溪 - A specific location, possibly a river or stream.

    桓玄既肆无君之心，使御史害太傅道子于安城。玄在南州坐，忽见一平上帻人，持马鞭，通云：“蒋侯来。”玄惊愕然，便见阶下奴子御幰车，见一士大夫，自云是蒋子文：“君何以害太傅？与为伯仲。”顾视之间，便不复见。
    桓玄在南郡国第居时，出诣殷荆州，于鹄穴逢一老公，驱一青牛，形色瑰异，桓即以所乘马易牛。乘至零陵溪，牛忽骏驶非常。因息驾饮牛，牛径入水不出。桓遣人觇守，经日绝迹也。

    Translation:
    Huan Xuan, having acted with disregard for authority, ordered an inspector to assassinate Taifu Daozi in Ancheng. While seated in the southern state, Huan suddenly saw a man wearing a flat cap, holding a horsewhip, and announcing, "Jiang Hou is coming." Huan was startled and then saw a servant driving a carriage, and a scholar who introduced himself as Jiang Ziya: "Why do you harm Taifu? We are peers." In the blink of an eye, he was no longer visible.
    When Huan lived in the residence of the Southern State, he went to Jingzhou to meet Governor Yin. At the Eagle's Nest, he encountered an old man driving a green ox with a striking appearance. Huan immediately exchanged his horse for the ox. They traveled to the Zero-Ling Creek, where the ox suddenly ran very fast and unusually. Huan stopped to feed the ox, and the ox went straight into the water and did not come out. Huan sent people to watch and guard, but after several days, there was no trace of it. [48]

    索元在历阳疾病，西界一年少女子姓某，自言为神所降，来与元相闻，许为治护。元性刚直，以为妖惑，收以付狱，戮之中于市中。女临死曰：“却后十日，当令索元知其罪。”如期，元果亡。
