    • 唯有一户 - There was only one door.
    • 搜索，了无所见 - Searched but saw nothing.
    • 见一大蛇，隐在床脚，酒臭 - Saw a large snake hiding under the bed, smelling of alcohol.
    • 便斩蛇寸断，掷于后沟 - Cut the snake into pieces and threw it into the back ditch.
    • 经数日，而妇死 - After several days, his wife died.
    • 又数日，而重卒 - After several more days, Zhong died.
    • 经三日复生 - After three days, he revived.
    • 说始死时，有神人将重到一官府 - Said that when he first died, a divine being took him to an official building.
    • 见官寮，问：“何以杀人？” - Saw officials, who asked, 'Why did you kill someone?'
    • 实不曾行凶 - I did not commit any crime.
    • 寸断掷在后沟，此是何物？ - Cut into pieces and thrown into the back ditch, what is this?
    • 此是蛇，非人 - This is a snake, not a person.
    • 府君愕然而悟曰 - The official was surprised and realized.
    • 我常用为神，而敢淫人妇，又妄讼人 - I have always been a god, but dared to have sexual relations with a woman and falsely accused someone.
    • 敕左右召来 - Ordered the attendants to bring him.
    • 吏卒乃领一人来，着平巾帻 - The officials brought a man, wearing a flat-capped hat.
    • 具诘其淫妻之过 - Questioned him thoroughly about his immoral behavior towards his wife.
    • 将付狱 - Intended to send him to prison.
    • 重乃令人送还 - Zhong sent him back.

    Cultural and Historical Allusions

    • 会稽 - Ancient Chinese prefecture known for its cultural and historical significance.
    • 谢祖 - Historical figure from the Eastern Jin Dynasty.
    • 薛重 - Official from the county of Mobian in Kuaiji Prefecture.
    • 鄮县 - County in Kuaiji Prefecture, known for its historical and cultural significance.
    • 官府 - Government building or office where official business is conducted.
    • 官寮 - Officials or government officials, indicating the presence of authority figures.

    Syntactic Ambiguities

    • The sequence describes a woman giving birth to a boy and then a snake, which is a supernatural event.
    • The sequence describes the snake's immediate departure after being born.
    • The sequence indicates a significant time lapse and the woman's natural death.
    • The sequence describes the sudden appearance of a large snake, indicating a supernatural event.
    • The sequence describes the snake's actions and emotional reaction, indicating a supernatural visit.
    • The sequence indicates the snake's prolonged presence and eventual departure.
    • The sequence describes an official returning home after a period of absence.
    • The sequence sets the scene for a mysterious and supernatural event.
    • The sequence describes the husband's sudden and aggressive reaction to the mysterious presence.
    • The sequence describes the wife's reaction to her husband's question and her response.
    • The sequence describes the husband's search for the mysterious presence.
    • The sequence describes the discovery of the snake and its smell.
    • The sequence describes the husband's violent action against the snake.
    • The sequence describes the immediate consequences of the husband's actions.
    • The sequence describes the husband's miraculous resurrection and his supernatural experience.
    • The sequence describes the interrogation by officials.
    • The sequence describes the husband's defense and the officials' question.
    • The sequence describes the husband's explanation.
    • The sequence describes the official's realization and accusation.
    • The sequence describes the official's command and the arrival of a person.
    • The sequence describes the interrogation and the intended punishment.
    • The sequence describes the husband's action to send the person back.

    In Kuaiji, the wife of Xie Zu gave birth to a boy and then a snake, which was about two feet long and immediately left through the door. After several decades, the woman died of old age. Suddenly, Xie Zu heard the sound of wind and rain from the northwest. Soon after, he saw a snake that was about ten or more zhang long, with a body circumference of more than ten chi. The snake entered the house and approached the spirit altar. It then went to the coffin, circled it several times, hit the coffin with its head, and both blood and tears flowed from its eyes. After a long time, the snake left.

    In Kuaiji, an official from the county of Mobian named Xue Zhong got leave to return home. At night, with the door closed, he heard a man's snoring on his wife's bed. He called his wife, who came out of bed. Before the door was opened, Zhong drew a knife and asked his wife, "Who is the drunk person?" His wife was greatly surprised and shocked and said, "There was no one there." Zhong's house had only one door. He searched but saw nothing. He saw a large snake hiding under the bed, smelling of alcohol. Zhong cut the snake into pieces and threw it into the back ditch. After several days, his wife died. After several more days, Zhong died. After three days, he revived and said that when he first died, a divine being took him to an official building. He saw officials who asked, "Why did you kill someone?" Zhong said, "I did not commit any crime." They asked, "Cut into pieces and thrown into the back ditch, what is this?" Zhong said, "This is a snake, not a person." The official was surprised and realized, "I have always been a god, but dared to have sexual relations with a woman and falsely accused someone. I ordered the attendants to bring him!" The officials brought a man, wearing a flat-capped hat, and questioned him thoroughly about his immoral behavior towards his wife. They intended to send him to prison. Zhong sent him back. [80]

    曲阿虞晚所居宅内，有一皂荚，大十余围，高十余丈，枝条扶疏，阴覆数家，诸鸟依其上。晚令奴斫上枝，因坠殆死。空中有骂者曰：“虞晚，汝何意伐我家居？”便以瓦石掷之，大小并委顿。如此二年，渐消灭。
    　　虞晚家有皂荚树，有神。隔路有大榆树，古传曰：是雌雄。晚被斫，此树枯死。
    　　太原王仲德，年少时遭乱，避胡贼，绝粒三日，草中卧。忽有人扶其头，呼云：“可起啖枣。”王便寤。瞥见天小儿，长四尺，即隐。乃有一囊干枣在前，啖之，小有气力，便起。
    　　安定人周敬，种瓜时亢旱，鬼为槤水浇瓜，瓜大滋繁。问姓名，不答。还白父：“尝有惠于人否？”父曰：“西郭樊营，先作郡吏，偿官数百斛米，我时以百斛助之。其人已死。” [81]

    Relevant Explanation

    Explanation

    [81]

    Idiomatic Expressions

    • 扶疏 - Means 'sprawling,' describing the tree's branches.
    • 委顿 - Means 'great distress,' describing the distress caused by the attack.

    Cultural and Historical Allusions

    • 曲阿 - A place name, likely referring to a town or region in ancient China.
    • 皂荚 - A type of tree, known for its black jujube fruit.
    • 天小儿 - A small child in the sky, likely a supernatural or divine being.
    • 太原 - A place name, referring to a region in northern China.
    • 胡贼 - Refers to nomadic tribes or bandits, often used in historical contexts.
    • 安定 - A place name, referring to a region in western China.
    • 亢旱 - Severe drought, a significant natural disaster in agricultural societies.
    • 槤水 - Rainwater, used for irrigation.
    • 西郭樊营 - A person's name, likely a historical figure.
    • 郡吏 - A county official, a local government position.
    • 斛 - A unit of volume, used for measuring grains.

    Proper Nouns

    • 曲阿 - A place name.
    • 虞晚 - A person's name.
    • 太原 - A place name.
    • 王仲德 - A person's name.
    • 安定 - A place name.
    • 周敬 - A person's name.
    • 西郭樊营 - A person's name.
    • 郡吏 - A title.
    • 斛 - A unit of volume.

    In the house of Yu Wan from Qu'a, there was a black jujube tree, over ten zhi in circumference, over ten zhang tall, with sprawling branches that shaded several households. Birds perched on its branches. One day, Yu Wan ordered his servant to cut the upper branches, and as a result, he almost died. Someone in the air shouted: "Yu Wan, why do you cut down my home?" Then they threw tiles and stones at him, causing him great distress. This continued for two years, gradually fading away.

    There was a black jujube tree in Yu Wan's house, which had a spirit. Across the road was a large elm tree, and it was said that it was male and female. When Yu Wan was cut down, this tree withered and died.

    Wang Zhongde from Taiyuan, when young, encountered chaos and fled from the Hu bandits, abstaining from food for three days and lying in the grass. Suddenly someone lifted his head and called out: "You can get up and eat dates." Wang Zhongde then stood up. He briefly saw a small child in the sky, four chi tall, who then disappeared. Then there was a bag of dried dates in front of him, and after eating them, he gained a little strength and stood up.

    Zhou Jing from Anding, when planting melons during a severe drought, had a ghost water them with rainwater, and the melons grew abundantly. When asked his name, he did not respond. He returned to tell his father: "Have you ever done a favor for someone?" His father said: "Xiguo Fan Ying, who first served as a county official, repaid the government hundreds of dou of rice, and I contributed a hundred dou to him at that time. That person is already dead." [81]

    有人家甚富，止有一男，宠恣过常。游市，见一女子美丽，卖胡粉，爱之，无由自达。乃托买粉，日往市，得粉便去，初无所言。积渐久，女深疑之。明日复来，问曰：“君买此粉，将欲何施？”答曰：“意相爱乐，不敢自达。然恒欲相见，故假此以覌姿耳！”女怅然有感，遂相许以私，克以明夕。其夜，安寝堂屋，以俟女来。薄暮，果到，男不胜其悦，把臂曰：“宿愿始伸于此！”欢踊遂死。女惶惧，不知所以。因遁去，明还粉店。至食时，父母怪男不起，往视，已死矣。当就殡敛。发箧笥中，见百余裹胡粉，大小一积。其母曰：“杀吾儿者，必此粉也。”入市遍买胡粉，次此女，比之，手迹如先，遂执问女曰：“何杀我儿？”女闻呜咽，具以实陈。父母不信，遂以诉官。女曰：“妾岂复吝死？乞一临尸尽哀！”县令许焉。径往，抚之恸哭，曰：“不幸致此，若死魂而灵，复何恨哉？”男豁然更生，具说情状，遂为夫妇，子孙繁茂。 [82]

    Relevant Explanation

    Explanation

    [82]

    Idiomatic Expressions

    • 止有一男 - Emphasizes the rarity and value of the male child.
    • 宠恣过常 - Indicates excessive pampering.
    • 无由自达 - Suggests a lack of means to communicate feelings.
    • 托买粉 - Common euphemism for seeking an excuse to interact with someone.
    • 积渐久 - Indicates a prolonged period.
    • 深疑之 - Shows the girl's suspicion.
    • 意相爱乐 - Expresses a desire for mutual affection.
    • 不敢自达 - Indicates shyness or fear.
    • 恒欲相见 - Shows persistent desire.
    • 假此以覌姿耳 - Explains the pretense.
    • 怅然有感 - Indicates emotional response.
    • 相许以私 - Suggests a secret affair.
    • 克以明夕 - Sets a specific time.
    • 安寝堂屋 - Indicates a place of rest.
    • 俟女来 - Shows anticipation.
    • 不胜其悦 - Expresses intense happiness.
    • 把臂曰 - Indicates a physical gesture and speech.
    • 宿愿始伸于此 - Expresses satisfaction.
    • 豁然更生 - Indicates a miraculous recovery.
    • 具说情状 - Provides a detailed account.
    • 子孙繁茂 - Indicates prosperity.

    Cultural and Historical Allusions

    • 胡粉 - Lead-based cosmetic powder, a common beauty product in ancient China.
    • 堂屋 - Main hall, a significant room in traditional Chinese homes, often used for important gatherings.
    • 殡敛 - Funeral and burial, referring to the process of preparing the body for burial.
    • 临尸尽哀 - Ritual act of showing grief.
    • 豁然更生 - Metaphor for a miraculous recovery, often associated with supernatural intervention.

    Proper Nouns

    • 胡粉 - Lead-based cosmetic powder.
    • 堂屋 - Main hall.
    • 殡敛 - Funeral and burial.
    • 临尸尽哀 - Mourning at the corpse.
    • 豁然更生 - Suddenly came back to life.

    A wealthy family had only one son, who was overindulged beyond the norm. While wandering in the market, he saw a beautiful woman selling lead-based cosmetic powder and fell in love with her, but he had no way to express his feelings. So he pretended to buy powder and went to the market every day, getting the powder and leaving without saying anything. Over time, the woman became deeply suspicious of him. The next day, she asked, "Why do you buy this powder? What will you do with it?" He replied, "I intend to love and enjoy you, but I'm afraid to express myself. However, I always want to see you, so I use this as an excuse to see your beauty!" The woman felt sad and moved, and they agreed to a private relationship, setting a meeting for the next night. That night, the son stayed in the main hall, waiting for the girl to come. As dusk fell, she arrived, and the son was overwhelmed with joy. He held her arm and said, "My long-held wish is finally fulfilled here!" They were so happy that he died suddenly. The girl was terrified and didn't know what to do. She fled and returned to the powder shop the next morning. By mealtime, the parents were surprised that the son hadn't gotten up and went to check on him, only to find that he had died. They prepared for his funeral and burial. When they opened the chest, they found over a hundred packages of lead-based cosmetic powder, large and small. The mother said, "The powder that killed my son must be this!" They went to the market and bought all the lead-based cosmetic powder, including the one the girl had sold. Comparing the handwriting, they found it matched. They confronted the girl and asked, "Why did you kill my son?" The girl wept and told the truth. The parents didn't believe her and took the case to the authorities. The girl said, "I wouldn't hesitate to die. Please let me mourn at his corpse!" The county magistrate agreed. She went directly to the corpse, touched it, and cried bitterly, saying, "It's so unfortunate that this happened. If your spirit is still alive, what more could you regret?" The son suddenly came back to life and explained the situation in detail. They became husband and wife, and their descendants flourished. [82]

    许攸梦乌衣吏奉漆案，案上有六封文书。拜跪曰：“府君当为北斗君，明年七月。”复有一案，四封文书云：“陈康为主簿。”觉后，康至，曰：“今来当谒。”攸闻益惧，问康曰：“我作道师，死不过作社公。今日得北斗，主簿余为忝矣！”明年七月，二人同日而死。 [83]

    Xu You had a dream in which a black-clad official presented a lacquered tray, on which were six sealed documents. The official bowed and knelt, saying, "Your Excellency will become the Lord of the Big Dipper, in July next year." There was another tray with four sealed documents, each stating, "Chen Kang will be appointed as the Chief Clerk." After waking up, Chen Kang arrived and said, "I have come to pay my respects." Xu You heard this and became even more fearful, asking Chen Kang, "I am a Daoist master; after death, I will only become a village deity. Today, I am to become the Lord of the Big Dipper, and as the Chief Clerk, I am unworthy!" The following July, both Xu You and Chen Kang died on the same day. [83]

    广平太守冯孝将男马子，梦一女人，年十八九岁，言：“我乃前太守徐玄方之女，不幸早亡。亡来四年，为鬼所枉杀。按生箓，乃寿至八十余。今听我更生，还为君妻，能见聘否？”马子掘开棺视之，其女已活，遂为夫妇。
    　　京口有徐郎者，家甚褴缕，常于江边拾流柴。忽见江中连船盖川而来，径回入浦，对徐而泊，遣使往云：“天女今当为徐郎妻。”徐入屋角，隐藏不出。母兄妹劝励强出。未至舫，先令于别室为徐郎浴。水芬香，非世常有，赠以缯绛之衣。徐唯恐惧，累膝床端，夜无酬，接之礼。女然后发遣，以所赠衣物乞之而退。家大小怨情煎骂，遂懊叹卒。
    　　侯官县常有阁下神，岁终，诸吏杀牛祀之。沛郡武曾作令断之，经一年，曾迁作建威参军。神夜来问曾：“何以不还食？”声色极恶，甚相谴责。诸吏便于道中买牛，共谢之，此神乃去。 [84]

    Relevant Explanation

    Explanation

    [84]

    Idiomatic Expressions

    • 太守 - Refers to the administrative official in charge of a prefecture, similar to a governor.
    • 梦一女人 - Means 'dreamed of a woman.'
    • 不幸早亡 - Means 'unfortunately died young.'
    • 为鬼所枉杀 - Means 'was wrongfully killed by a ghost.'
    • 按生箓 - Means 'according to the register of life.'
    • 寿至八十余 - Means 'lived to be over eighty years old.'
    • 今听我更生 - Means 'now allow me to be reborn.'
    • 还为君妻 - Means 'to return as your wife.'
    • 能见聘否 - Means 'can you marry me?'
    • 掘开棺视之 - Means 'dug up her coffin to see her.'
    • 其女已活 - Means 'the girl was alive.'
    • 遂为夫妇 - Means 'they then became husband and wife.'
    • 京口有徐郎者 - Means 'In Jingkou, there was a man named Xu Lang.'
    • 家甚褴缕 - Means 'his family was very poor.'
    • 常于江边拾流柴 - Means 'often picked up floating wood by the river.'
    • 忽见江中连船盖川而来 - Means 'suddenly saw a fleet of boats covering the river coming towards him.'
    • 径回入浦，对徐而泊 - Means 'turned around and entered the bay, anchoring opposite Xu.'
    • 遣使往云 - Means 'sent someone to say.'
    • 天女今当为徐郎妻 - Means 'the celestial maiden will now become Xu Lang's wife.'
    • 徐入屋角，隐藏不出 - Means 'Xu hid in a corner of his house and did not come out.'
    • 母兄妹劝励强出 - Means 'his mother and siblings urged him to come out.'
    • 未至舫，先令于别室为徐郎浴 - Means 'before reaching the boat, they had Xu bathed in a separate room.'
    • 水芬香，非世常有 - Means 'the water was fragrant, unlike anything in the world.'
    • 赠以缯绛之衣 - Means 'they gave him silk and red clothing.'
    • 徐唯恐惧，累膝床端 - Means 'Xu was only afraid, sitting on the edge of the bed with his knees drawn up.'
    • 夜无酬，接之礼 - Means 'at night, there was no reciprocation of the greeting.'
    • 女然后发遣 - Means 'the woman then left.'
    • 以所赠衣物乞之而退 - Means 'she asked for the clothes she had given and left.'
    • 家大小怨情煎骂 - Means 'the whole family, big and small, complained and cursed.'
    • 遂懊叹卒 - Means 'he then died of sorrow and regret.'
    • 侯官县常有阁下神 - Means 'In Houguan County, there is a spirit beneath the hall.'
    • 岁终，诸吏杀牛祀之 - Means 'at the end of the year, officials sacrificed a cow to it.'
    • 沛郡武曾作令断之 - Means 'Pei Jun's Wu Zeng abolished it.'
    • 经一年，曾迁作建威参军 - Means 'after a year, Zeng was promoted to the position of Jianwei Sengjun.'
    • 神夜来问曾 - Means 'the spirit came at night to ask Zeng.'
    • 何以不还食 - Means 'why don't you offer food anymore?'
    • 声色极恶，甚相谴责 - Means 'the spirit was extremely angry and severely blamed him.'
    • 诸吏便于道中买牛，共谢之 - Means 'the officials bought a cow on the road and apologized to it.'
    • 此神乃去 - Means 'the spirit then left.'

    Cultural and Historical Allusions

    • 太守 - Refers to the administrative official in charge of a prefecture, similar to a governor.
    • 梦 - Dreams were often seen as omens or messages from the divine in ancient Chinese culture.
    • 鬼 - Ghosts were believed to have supernatural powers and could cause harm.
    • 生箓 - The register of life, a concept in Daoist and Buddhist beliefs about the fate and lifespan of individuals.
    • 天女 - Celestial maidens were often depicted as beautiful and benevolent beings in Chinese mythology.
    • 侯官县 - A county in ancient China, likely referring to a specific location in the region.
    • 阁下神 - A spirit associated with a specific place, often worshipped for protection and blessings.
    • 岁终 - The end of the year, a time for rituals and offerings to deities and spirits.

    Proper Nouns

    • 冯孝将 - The name of the man whose son had the dream.
    • 马子 - The name of the man who had the dream.
    • 徐玄方 - The name of the woman who died young.
    • 徐郎 - The name of the man from Jingkou.
    • 武曾 - The name of the official who abolished the ritual.
    • 建威参军 - The name of the position Wu Zeng was promoted to.
    • 侯官县 - The name of the county where the spirit was worshipped.

    In the prefecture of Guangping, the son of Prefect Feng Xiaojiang, named Ma Zi, dreamed of a woman about eighteen or nineteen years old who said, "I am the daughter of the former Prefect Xu Xuanfang, who unfortunately died young. I died four years ago and was wrongfully killed by a ghost. According to the register of life, I was supposed to live to be over eighty years old. Now, I am allowed to be reborn and return as your wife. Can you marry me?" Ma Zi dug up her coffin to see her, and the girl was alive. They then became husband and wife.

    In Jingkou, there was a man named Xu Lang, whose family was very poor. He often picked up floating wood by the river. One day, he suddenly saw a fleet of boats covering the river coming towards him. The boats turned around and entered the bay, anchoring opposite Xu. Someone was sent to say, "The celestial maiden will now become Xu Lang's wife." Xu hid in a corner of his house and did not come out. His mother and siblings urged him to come out. Before reaching the boat, they had Xu bathed in a separate room. The water was fragrant, unlike anything in the world. They gave him silk and red clothing. Xu was only afraid, sitting on the edge of the bed with his knees drawn up. At night, there was no reciprocation of the greeting. The woman then left, asking for the clothes she had given and departing. The whole family, big and small, complained and cursed. He then died of sorrow and regret.

    In Houguan County, there is a spirit beneath the hall. At the end of the year, officials sacrificed a cow to it. Pei Jun's Wu Zeng abolished this practice. After a year, Zeng was promoted to the position of Jianwei Sengjun. The spirit came at night to ask Zeng, "Why don't you offer food anymore?" The spirit was extremely angry and severely blamed him. The officials bought a cow on the road and apologized to it. The spirit then left. [84]

    甄冲，字叔让，中山人，为云社令，来至惠怀县。忽有一人来通云：“社郎须臾便至。”年少，容貌美凈。既坐，寒温云：“大人见使，贪慕高援，欲以妹与君婚，故来宣此意。”甄愕然曰：“仆长大，且已有家，何缘此理？”社郎复云：“仆妹年少，且令色少双，必欲得佳对，云何见拒？”甄曰：“仆老翁，见有妇，岂容违越？”相与反复数过，甄殊无动意。社郎有恚色，云：“大人当自来，恐不得违尔。”既去，便见两岸上有人，着帻，捉马鞭，罗列相随，行从甚多。社公寻至，卤簿导从如方伯，乘马舆，青幢赤络，覆车数乘。女郎乘四望车，锦步障数十张，婢十八人，来车前。衣服文彩，所未尝见。便于甄旁岸边上张幔屋，舒荐席。社公下，隐膝几，坐白旃坐褥。玉唾壶，以玳瑁为手巾笼，捉白麈尾。 [85]

    Relevant Explanation

    Explanation

    [85]

    Idiomatic Expressions

    • 相与反复数过 - Repeatedly debated or discussed back and forth.
    • 衣服文彩 - Clothes with elaborate patterns or colors.

    Cultural and Historical Allusions

    • 甄冲 - A historical figure or fictional character, likely a local official.
    • 中山 - A historical region in northern China, known for its cultural and political significance.
    • 社令 - A local official, possibly a magistrate or community leader.
    • 惠怀县 - A specific location, the historical context is not provided.
    • 社郎 - A local deity or person in charge of the community, possibly a figure of authority.
    • 大人 - A term of respect for a superior or elder.
    • 社公 - A senior figure or head of the community, possibly a local deity or respected elder.
    • 卤簿导从如方伯 - A ceremonial escort, indicating the high status of the person being accompanied.
    • 乘马舆 - A mode of transportation for high-ranking officials or dignitaries.
    • 青幢赤络 - Decorative elements on the carriage, symbolizing status and authority.
    • 四望车 - A type of carriage, possibly used for important occasions.
    • 锦步障数十张 - A luxurious screen made of brocade, often used in ceremonial or festive settings.
    • 婢十八人 - Maidservants, indicating the wealth and status of the person being described.

    Proper Nouns

    • 甄冲 - The main character, a local official.
    • 叔让 - His courtesy name.
    • 中山 - A historical region.
    • 云社令 - A local official or magistrate.
    • 惠怀县 - A specific location.
    • 社郎 - A local deity or person in charge of the community.
    • 大人 - A term of respect for a superior or elder.
    • 社公 - A senior figure or head of the community.
    • 方伯 - A high-ranking official.
    • 四望车 - A type of carriage.
    • 锦步障 - A luxurious screen.
    • 婢十八人 - Maidservants.

    甄冲，字叔让，是中山人，担任云社令，来到惠怀县。忽然有一个人前来通报说：“社郎很快就到。”这个人年轻，容貌清秀。坐下后，他问候道：“大人让我来，因为羡慕您的高地位，想把妹妹嫁给您，所以来传达这个意思。”甄冲惊讶地说：“我已经年长，而且已有家室，怎么会有这样的道理？”社郎又说：“我的妹妹年轻，而且容貌出众，必定想找一个好配偶，为什么拒绝呢？”甄冲回答：“我已经是老人，已有妻子，怎么能够违背礼法？”两人反复讨论多次，甄冲始终没有动心。社郎面露怒色，说：“大人会亲自来，恐怕您不能违背。”说完便离开了，甄冲随即看到两岸上有人戴着头巾，拿着马鞭，排列成行，随从众多。不久，社公到了，他的仪仗队如同地方长官，乘坐马车，车上有蓝色的旗和红色的流苏，遮盖着几辆马车。女郎乘坐四望车，车前有数十张锦缎屏风，十八个婢女跟随，衣服华丽，前所未见。他们在甄冲旁边的岸边搭起帐篷，铺上坐席。社公下车，坐在白色的毡垫上，面前有玉制的唾壶，用玳瑁制成的手巾笼，手中拿着白色的麈尾。 [85]

    女郎却在东岸，黄门白拂夹车立，婢子在前。社公引佐吏，令前坐，当六十人。命作乐，器悉如琉璃。社公谓甄曰：“仆有陋女，情所钟爱。以君体德令茂，贪结亲援，因遣小儿已具宣此旨。”甄曰：“仆既老悴，已有家室，儿子且大，虽贪贵聘，不敢闻命。”社公复云：“仆女年始二十，姿色淑令，四德克备。今在岸上，勿复为烦，但当成礼耳！”甄拒之转苦，谓是邪魅，便拔刀横膝上，以死拒之，不复与语。社公大怒，便令呼三斑两虎来，张口正赤，号呼裂地，径跳上，如此者数十次。相守至天明，无如之何，便去。留一牵车。将从数十人，欲以迎甄，甄便移惠怀上县中住。所迎车及人至门，中有一人，着单衣帻，向之揖，于此便住，不得前。甄停十余日，方敢去。故见二人着帻、捉马鞭随至家。至家少日，而妇病遂亡。 [86]

    Relevant Explanation

    Explanation

    [86]

    Idiomatic Expressions

    • 女郎却在东岸 - Literal phrase meaning 'the young lady was on the east bank.' The word '却' indicates a position or state.
    • 黄门白拂夹车立 - '黄门' refers to eunuchs or attendants, '白拂' means a white feather fan, and '夹车立' means standing on both sides of the carriage.
    • 社公引佐吏 - '社公' refers to the local deity or land god, and '引佐吏' means leading his assistants or subordinates.
    • 令前坐，当六十人 - '令前坐' means to sit in front, and '当六十人' means about sixty people.
    • 命作乐，器悉如琉璃 - '命作乐' means to command the making of music, and '器悉如琉璃' means the instruments were all made of glass.
    • 仆有陋女，情所钟爱 - '仆' is a humble term for 'I,' '陋女' means a humble daughter, and '情所钟爱' means deeply loved.
    • 贪结亲援 - '贪结' means eager to form, and '亲援' means kinship or alliance.
    • 已具宣此旨 - '已具宣' means already fully explained, and '此旨' means this intention.
    • 仆既老悴，已有家室 - '仆既老悴' means I am already old and frail, and '已有家室' means I already have a family.
    • 儿子且大 - '儿子' means son, and '且大' means already grown up.
    • 虽贪贵聘，不敢闻命 - '虽贪' means although eager, '贵聘' means a high dowry, and '不敢闻命' means I dare not accept the proposal.
    • 仆女年始二十，姿色淑令 - '仆女' means my daughter, '年始二十' means she is twenty years old, and '姿色淑令' means she is beautiful and virtuous.
    • 四德克备 - '四德' refers to the Four Virtues (morality, speech, appearance, and work), and '克备' means fully possessed.
    • 但当成礼耳 - '但' means just, '当成礼' means to perform the wedding ceremony, and '耳' is a particle indicating a mild tone.
    • 甄拒之转苦 - '甄' is the name of the person, '拒之' means to reject him, and '转苦' means increasingly strongly.
    • 谓是邪魅 - '谓' means to consider, '是' means this, '邪魅' means evil spirits or demons.
    • 便拔刀横膝上，以死拒之 - '便拔刀' means to draw a sword, '横膝上' means across the knee, and '以死拒之' means to resist to the point of death.
    • 不复与语 - '不复' means no longer, and '与语' means to talk to him.
    • 社公大怒 - '大怒' means greatly angry.
    • 便令呼三斑两虎来 - '便令' means to order, '呼' means to call, '三斑两虎' means three-spotted and two-striped tigers.
    • 张口正赤，号呼裂地 - '张口' means to open their mouths, '正赤' means bright red, '号呼' means to roar, and '裂地' means to shake the ground.
    • 径跳上，如此者数十次 - '径跳上' means to jump directly up, and '如此者数十次' means this happened dozens of times.
    • 相守至天明，无如之何 - '相守' means to confront each other, '至天明' means until dawn, and '无如之何' means unable to do anything.
    • 便去 - '便去' means to leave.
    • 留一牵车 - '留' means to leave behind, '一牵车' means a single carriage.
    • 将从数十人，欲以迎甄 - '将从' means to bring along, '欲以迎甄' means to come to welcome Zhen.
    • 甄便移惠怀上县中住 - '甄便移' means Zhen then moved, '惠怀' is a name, '上县' means an upper county, and '中住' means to live in the middle.
    • 所迎车及人至门 - '所迎' means the ones being welcomed, '及人' means and people, '至门' means to the door.
    • 中有一人，着单衣帻 - '中有一人' means there was one person, '着' means wearing, '单衣' means a simple robe, and '帻' means a headband.
    • 向之揖，于此便住，不得前 - '向之揖' means to bow to him, '于此便住' means to stop here, and '不得前' means unable to proceed.
    • 甄停十余日，方敢去 - '甄停' means Zhen stayed, '十余日' means more than ten days, and '方敢去' means finally dared to leave.
    • 故见二人着帻、捉马鞭随至家 - '故见' means then saw, '二人' means two people, '着帻' means wearing headbands, '捉马鞭' means holding riding whips, and '随至家' means following to the house.
    • 至家少日，而妇病遂亡 - '至家' means to the home, '少日' means a few days, '而妇病' means and the wife fell ill, and '遂亡' means eventually died.

    Cultural and Historical Allusions

    • 女郎 - Refers to a young lady or unmarried woman.
    • 黄门白拂 - Eunuchs or attendants with white feather fans, often used in ceremonial contexts.
    • 社公 - The local deity or land god, often associated with local folklore and superstitions.
    • 佐吏 - Assistants or subordinates of the local deity.
    • 四德 - The Four Virtues: morality, speech, appearance, and work, which were important in Confucian ethics.
    • 邪魅 - Evil spirits or demons, often associated with supernatural beliefs.
    • 三斑两虎 - Three-spotted and two-striped tigers, likely referring to mythical or supernatural creatures.
    • 惠怀 - A name, possibly a place or person, but without further context, it is difficult to determine its exact significance.

    A young lady was on the east bank, with eunuchs and attendants holding white feather fans standing on both sides of the carriage, and maids in front. The local deity led his assistants, and they were seated in front, numbering about sixty. He commanded the making of music, and the instruments were all made of glass. The local deity said to Zhen, "I have a humble daughter whom I deeply love. Considering your noble character and virtues, I am eager to form a kinship alliance, so I have already fully explained this intention through my young servant." Zhen replied, "I am already old and frail, and I already have a family. My son is grown up. Although I am eager for a high dowry, I dare not accept your proposal." The local deity then said, "My daughter is twenty years old, beautiful and virtuous, fully possessing the Four Virtues. She is right here on the bank. Do not make it complicated; just perform the wedding ceremony!" Zhen increasingly strongly rejected him, considering him an evil spirit, and drew a sword across his knee, resisting to the point of death, no longer talking to him. The local deity became greatly angry and ordered the summoning of three-spotted and two-striped tigers. They opened their bright red mouths, roared, and shook the ground, jumping directly up, and this happened dozens of times. They confronted each other until dawn, unable to do anything, and then left. He left behind a single carriage and brought along dozens of people, intending to come and welcome Zhen. Zhen then moved to live in the middle of the upper county of Huaihuai. The ones being welcomed, along with the people, arrived at the door. There was one person among them, wearing a simple robe and headband, who bowed to him, and they stopped there, unable to proceed. Zhen stayed more than ten days and finally dared to leave. Then he saw two people wearing headbands and holding riding whips following him to his home. After a few days, his wife fell ill and eventually died. [86]

    秣陵人赵伯伦曾往襄阳，船人以猪豕为祷，及祭，但豚肩而已。尔夕，伦等梦见一翁一姥，鬓首苍素，皆着布衣，手持桡楫，怒之。明发，辄触沙冲石，皆非人力所禁。更施厚馔，即获流通。
    　　桂阳人李经，与朱平带戟逐焉。行百余步，忽见一鬼，长丈余，止之曰：“李经有命，岂可杀之？无为，必伤汝手。”平乘醉直往经家，鬼亦随之。平既见经，方欲奋刃，忽屹然不动，如被执缚，果伤左手指焉。遂立庭间，至暮，乃醒而去。鬼曰：“我先语汝，云何不从？”言终而灭。 [87]

    Relevant Explanation

    Explanation

    [87]

    Idiomatic Expressions

    • 秣陵人 - Refers to someone from Moling (今南京), a place name.
    • 往襄阳 - Means 'went to Xiangyang,' indicating a journey.
    • 船人 - Refers to boatmen or sailors.
    • 以猪豕为祷 - Means 'to pray with pigs,' indicating a sacrificial act.
    • 祭 - Means 'to offer a sacrifice or prayer.'
    • 但豚肩而已 - Means 'only offered the shoulder of a pig,' indicating a partial offering.
    • 尔夕 - Means 'that night,' referring to a specific time.
    • 伦等 - Refers to 'Lun and others,' indicating a group of people.
    • 梦见一翁一姥 - Means 'dreamed of an old man and an old woman,' describing a dream encounter.
    • 鬓首苍素 - Describes the appearance of the old man and woman, meaning 'with white hair and simple clothes.'
    • 手持桡楫 - Means 'holding oars,' indicating their actions.
    • 怒之 - Means 'angered them,' expressing their reaction.
    • 明发 - Means 'the next morning,' indicating a specific time.
    • 辄触沙冲石 - Means 'would hit sand and rocks,' describing a recurring event.
    • 皆非人力所禁 - Means 'all were beyond human control,' indicating the severity of the situation.
    • 更施厚馔 - Means 'offered more generous food,' indicating a change in offering.
    • 即获流通 - Means 'immediately regained smooth sailing,' indicating a positive outcome.
    • 桂阳人李经 - Refers to 'a man named Li Jing from Guiyang,' a proper name.
    • 与朱平带戟逐焉 - Means 'with Zhu Ping, armed with spears, pursued them,' indicating an action.
    • 行百余步 - Means 'walked over a hundred steps,' indicating a distance.
    • 忽见一鬼 - Means 'suddenly saw a ghost,' describing a sudden encounter.
    • 长丈余 - Means 'over ten feet tall,' describing the ghost's size.
    • 止之曰 - Means 'stopped him and said,' indicating a dialogue.
    • 李经有命 - Means 'Li Jing has his fate,' indicating a belief in destiny.
    • 岂可杀之 - Means 'how can you kill him,' expressing a question.
    • 无为 - Means 'do nothing,' indicating an action.
    • 必伤汝手 - Means 'you will surely injure your hand,' predicting a consequence.
    • 平乘醉直往经家 - Means 'Ping, drunk, went straight to Li Jing's house,' indicating an action.
    • 鬼亦随之 - Means 'the ghost followed him,' indicating a follow-up action.
    • 平既见经 - Means 'Ping saw Li Jing,' indicating a meeting.
    • 方欲奋刃 - Means 'was about to draw his sword,' indicating a preparation.
    • 忽屹然不动 - Means 'suddenly became immobile,' indicating a sudden change.
    • 如被执缚 - Means 'as if bound,' indicating a state.
    • 果伤左手指焉 - Means 'indeed injured his left finger,' indicating a result.
    • 遂立庭间 - Means 'stood in the courtyard,' indicating a location.
    • 至暮 - Means 'until dusk,' indicating a time.
    • 乃醒而去 - Means 'then woke up and left,' indicating an action.
    • 我先语汝 - Means 'I told you earlier,' indicating a reminder.
    • 言终而灭 - Means 'after speaking, disappeared,' indicating an ending.

    Cultural and Historical Allusions

    • 秣陵 - Refers to Moling (今南京), a historical place name.
    • 襄阳 - Refers to Xiangyang, a historical place name.
    • 猪豕 - Refers to pigs, a common sacrificial animal.
    • 豚肩 - Refers to the shoulder of a pig, a specific part used in sacrifices.
    • 翁一姥 - Refers to an old man and an old woman, common figures in folklore.
    • 桡楫 - Refers to oars, tools used in boating.
    • 桂阳 - Refers to Guiyang, a historical place name.
    • 戟 - Refers to spears, weapons used in ancient times.
    • 鬼 - Refers to ghosts, supernatural beings in folklore.

    Proper Nouns

    • 秣陵 - A historical place name.
    • 襄阳 - A historical place name.
    • 赵伯伦 - A proper name.
    • 桂阳 - A historical place name.
    • 李经 - A proper name.
    • 朱平 - A proper name.

    A man from Moling named Zhao Beren once traveled to Xiangyang. The boatmen offered pigs as a sacrifice, and when they performed the ritual, they only offered the shoulder of a pig. That night, Beren and others dreamed of an old man and an old woman, with white hair and simple clothes, both wearing coarse garments and holding oars, who were angered by them. The next morning, the boat would hit sand and rocks, all beyond human control. They then offered more generous food, and immediately regained smooth sailing.

    A man from Guiyang named Li Jing, accompanied by Zhu Ping with spears, pursued them. After walking over a hundred steps, they suddenly saw a ghost, over ten feet tall, who stopped them and said, "Li Jing has his fate; how can you kill him? Do nothing, or you will surely injure your hand." Ping, drunk, went straight to Li Jing's house, and the ghost followed him. When Ping saw Li Jing, he was about to draw his sword, but suddenly became immobile, as if bound, and indeed injured his left finger. He then stood in the courtyard until dusk, when he woke up and left. The ghost said, "I told you earlier; why did you not listen?" After speaking, it disappeared. [87]

    剡县胡章与上虞管双喜好干戈。双死后，章梦见之，跃刃戏其前，觉，甚不乐。明日，以符帖壁。章欲近行，已泛舟理楫，忽见双来，攀留之云：“夫人相知，情贯千载。昨夜就卿戏，值眠，吾即去，今何故以符相厌？大丈夫不体天下之理，我畏符乎！”
    　　吴中人姓顾，往田舍。昼行去舍十余里，但闻西北隐隐。因举首，见四五百人，皆赤衣，长二丈，倏忽而至，三重围之。顾气奄奄不通，辗转不得。且至晡，围不解，口不得语，心呼北斗。又食顷，鬼相谓曰：“彼正心在神，可舍去。”豁如雾除。顾归舍，疲极卧。其夕，户前一处，火甚盛而不然，鬼纷纭相就，或往或来，呼顾谈，或入去其被，或上头，而轻如鸿毛。开晨失。 [88]

    Relevant Explanation

    Explanation

    [88]

    Idiomatic Expressions

    • 喜好干戈 - Literally 'love weapons,' but in this context, it likely refers to a fondness for conflict or violence.
    • 大丈夫不體天下之理 - Means 'a true man does not ignore the principles of the world,' suggesting a philosophical or moral stance.
    • 豁如霧除 - Means 'as clear as the fog lifting,' describing a sudden disappearance or resolution.
    • 疲極卧 - Means 'exhausted and lying down,' indicating extreme fatigue.

    Cultural and Historical Allusions

    • 剡縣 - Refers to Yandian County, a place in ancient China.
    • 上虞 - Refers to Shangyu, another place in ancient China.
    • 符 - Refers to talismans, which were used in ancient Chinese superstition for protection or to ward off evil spirits.
    • 干戈 - Literally 'weapons,' but often used to symbolize warfare or conflict.
    • 大丈夫 - Refers to a 'true man' or 'real man,' a term used to describe someone of high moral character.
    • 天下之理 - Refers to the principles or laws of the universe or society.

    Proper Nouns

    • 剡縣 - Yandian County.
    • 上虞 - Shangyu.
    • 胡章 - Hu Zhang.
    • 管雙 - Guan Shuang.

    Hu Zhang from Yandian County and Guan Shuang from Shangyu were fond of conflict. After Guan Shuang's death, Hu Zhang dreamed of him, with the ghost leaping with a sword and playing before him. Upon waking, Hu Zhang was very unhappy. The next day, he posted a talisman on the wall. Hu Zhang intended to make a nearby trip and had already prepared a boat and oars. Suddenly, he saw Guan Shuang come and cling to him, saying, "We have known each other for a long time, our friendship spanning a thousand years. Last night I came to play with you, but you were sleeping, so I left. Why are you using a talisman to repel me? A true man does not ignore the principles of the world. Do I fear a talisman?"

    A man from Wu named Gu went to a farm. During the day, he walked about ten miles from the farm and only heard a faint sound coming from the northwest. He raised his head and saw about four or five hundred people, all wearing red clothes and each two zhang tall, suddenly approaching and surrounding him in three layers. Gu felt his breath was barely passing through and could not move. By the time it was late afternoon, the encirclement did not lift, he could not speak, and in his heart, he called out to the North Star. After another mealtime, the ghosts said to each other, "He has a pure heart and is in the presence of the divine; we can let him go." Suddenly, it was as if the fog had lifted. Gu returned home, exhausted and lay down. That evening, there was a place in front of his door where a fire burned brightly but did not ignite. Ghosts gathered in confusion, some going and some coming, calling to Gu and talking to him, some entering and taking off his blanket, some climbing on his head, but it felt as light as a feather. At dawn, it was gone. [88]

    刘道锡与从弟康祖少不信有鬼，从兄兴伯少来见鬼，但辞论不能相屈。尝于京口长广桥宅东，云“有杀鬼在东篱上。”道锡便笑问其处，牵兴伯俱去，捉大刀，欲斫之。兴伯在后唤云：“鬼击汝！”道锡未及鬼处，便闻如有大仗声，道锡因倒地，经宿乃醒，一月日都差。兴伯复云：“厅事东头桑树上有鬼，形尚孺，长必害人。”康祖不信，问在树高下，指处分明。经十余日，是月晦夕，道锡逃暗中，以戟刺鬼所住便还，人无知者。明日，兴伯早来，忽惊曰：“此鬼昨夜那得人刺之？殆死，都不能复动，死亦当不久。”康祖大笑。 [89]

    Relevant Explanation

    Explanation

    [89]

    Idiomatic Expressions

    • 不信有鬼 - Literally 'do not believe in ghosts,' indicating skepticism about the existence of ghosts.
    • 少不信有鬼 - 'Young and skeptical about ghosts,' emphasizing the character's youth and disbelief.
    • 少来见鬼 - 'Young and often sees ghosts,' indicating frequent encounters with ghosts.
    • 辞论不能相屈 - 'Debates could not be resolved,' suggesting that the characters were unable to convince each other through argument.
    • 捉大刀 - 'Grab a large knife,' a dramatic action indicating readiness to confront the ghost.
    • 鬼击汝 - 'Ghost strikes you,' a warning from the ghost.
    • 大仗声 - 'Loud battle sound,' likely referring to a supernatural noise or disturbance.
    • 一月日都差 - 'One month later, everything was fine,' indicating recovery from the incident.
    • 厅事东头桑树上有鬼 - 'There is a ghost on the eastern side of the mulberry tree in the hall,' specifying the location of the ghost.
    • 形尚孺 - 'Still young in appearance,' describing the ghost's youthful form.
    • 长必害人 - 'Will surely harm people,' a prediction of future danger.
    • 不信 - 'Do not believe,' indicating skepticism.
    • 指处分明 - 'Pointed out clearly,' indicating clear directions.
    • 逃暗中 - 'Hide in the dark,' a strategic move to avoid detection.
    • 以戟刺鬼所住便还 - 'Stab the ghost's place with a spear and return,' a decisive action taken by Daoxi.
    • 殆死 - 'Almost died,' indicating near-death experience.
    • 不能复动 - 'Cannot move anymore,' indicating the ghost's incapacitation.
    • 死亦当不久 - 'Will die soon,' predicting the ghost's imminent demise.
    • 大笑 - 'Laughed loudly,' indicating amusement or disbelief.

    Cultural and Historical Allusions

    • 刘道锡 - A historical figure known for his skepticism towards supernatural phenomena.
    • 从弟康祖 - Daoxi's younger brother, who shares his skepticism.
    • 从兄兴伯 - Daoxi's older brother, who frequently encounters ghosts.
    • 京口长广桥宅东 - A specific location in Jingkou (modern-day Zhenjiang, Jiangsu Province), indicating a historical setting.
    • 鬼击汝 - A common supernatural threat in Chinese folklore, where ghosts warn or attack humans.
    • 大仗声 - A supernatural phenomenon often associated with ghostly activity, such as loud noises or disturbances.
    • 桑树 - Mulberry trees are often associated with supernatural elements in Chinese culture, sometimes serving as homes for spirits.
    • 戟 - A type of ancient Chinese spear, symbolizing martial prowess and decisiveness.

    Proper Nouns

    • 刘道锡 - A historical figure known for his skepticism.
    • 从弟康祖 - Daoxi's younger brother.
    • 从兄兴伯 - Daoxi's older brother.

    Liu Daoxi and his younger brother Kangzu were young and skeptical about ghosts, while his older brother Xingbo was young and often saw ghosts, but their debates could not be resolved. Once, at the eastern side of his house in Changguang Bridge, Jingkou, it was said, "There is a killing ghost on the eastern fence." Daoxi laughed and asked where it was, then took Xingbo with him, grabbed a large knife, and wanted to cut it down. Xingbo called out from behind, "The ghost is striking you!" Before Daoxi reached the ghost's location, he heard what sounded like a loud battle cry, and Daoxi fell to the ground, remaining unconscious until the next day, recovering fully after a month. Xingbo then said, "There is a ghost on the eastern side of the mulberry tree in the hall, still young in appearance, and will surely harm people." Kangzu did not believe him and asked for the exact location, which Xingbo pointed out clearly. After more than ten days, on the last day of the month, Daoxi hid in the dark, stabbed the ghost's place with a spear, and returned, without anyone knowing. The next morning, Xingbo came early and suddenly exclaimed, "How could this ghost be stabbed last night? It almost died, cannot move anymore, and will die soon." Kangzu laughed loudly. [89]

    邺县故尉赵吉，常在田陌间。昔日有一蹇人死，埋在陌边。后二十余年，有一远方人过赵所门外。远方人行十余步，忽作蹇，赵怪问其故，远人笑曰：“前有一蹇鬼，故效以戏耳！”
    　　东莱王明儿居在江西，死经一年，忽形见还家。经日命招亲好叙平生，云天曹许以暂归。言及将离语，便流涕问讯乡里，备有情焉。敕儿曰：“吾去人间，便已一周。思睹桑梓。”命儿同覌乡闾。行经邓艾庙，令烧之。儿大惊曰：“艾生时为征东将军，没而有灵，百姓祠以祈福，奈何焚之？”怒曰：“艾今在尚方摩铠，十指垂掘，岂其有神？”因云：“王大将军亦作牛驱驰殆毙，桓温为卒，同在地狱。此等并困剧理尽，安能为人损益？汝欲求多福者，正当恭顺尽忠孝，无恚怒，便善流无极。”又令可录指爪甲，死后可以赎罪。又使高作户限，鬼来入人室，记人罪过，越限拨脚，则忘事矣。 [90]

    Relevant Explanation

    Explanation

    [90]

    Idiomatic Expressions

    • 效以戏耳 - Imitating it for fun, indicating the traveler's playful explanation for his sudden lameness.
    • 善流无极 - Virtue flows endlessly, suggesting that good deeds have no limit.

    Cultural and Historical Allusions

    • 邺县 - Refers to Yexian, a county in ancient China, likely in the state of Wei during the Three Kingdoms period.
    • 邓艾庙 - Refers to a temple dedicated to Deng Ai, a prominent general and statesman of the Wei dynasty.
    • 桓温 - A famous general and politician of the Eastern Jin dynasty, known for his military campaigns and political influence.
    • 地狱 - Refers to the underworld or hell, a place of punishment for the wicked.

    Proper Nouns

    • 赵吉 - The name of a former county official.
    • 王明儿 - The name of a person who appears to have died and returned to visit his family.
    • 邓艾 - A famous general and statesman of the Wei dynasty.
    • 桓温 - A famous general and politician of the Eastern Jin dynasty.

    A Former County Magistrate of Yexian, Zhao Ji, often wandered in the fields. In the past, there was a lame person who died and was buried by the roadside. Twenty years later, a traveler passed by Zhao's gate. After walking a dozen steps, the traveler suddenly became lame. Zhao was surprised and asked him why. The traveler laughed and said, "There was a lame ghost ahead, so I imitated it for fun!"

    In Donglai, Wang Ming'er lived in Jiangxi. He died a year ago but suddenly appeared back home in a ghostly form. For a whole day, he summoned his relatives and friends to recount his life, saying that the heavenly authorities had allowed him to return temporarily. When he spoke of parting, he shed tears and inquired about his hometown, showing great emotion. He instructed his son, "I have been away from the human world for a full year. I miss my hometown." He ordered his son to visit their village together. They passed by the temple dedicated to Deng Ai and were told to burn it. The son was greatly surprised and said, "Deng Ai was a General of the East when he was alive. He is said to have spiritual powers, and people worship him to seek blessings. Why should we burn his temple?" The ghost was angry and said, "Deng Ai is now in the imperial workshop polishing armor, his fingers worn from hard labor. How can he have any spiritual power?" He then said, "General Wang, who was a farmer, and General Huán Wēn, who was a soldier, are both in hell. These people are all exhausted and have no more strength. How can they bring about good or bad for others? If you want to seek much happiness, you should be respectful, obedient, loyal, and filial. Do not harbor anger, and virtue will flow endlessly." He also instructed, "You can collect fingernails after death to redeem your sins." He further commanded, "Make the threshold of the door high. When ghosts enter a room, they record a person's sins. If they step over the threshold, they will forget everything." [90]

    广陵刘青松晨起，见一人着公服，赉板云：“召为鲁郡太守。”言讫便去。去后，亦不复见。至来日，复至曰：“君便应到职。”青松知必死，告妻子处分家事，沐浴。至晡，见车马，吏侍左右。青松奄忽而绝。家人咸见其升车，南出，百余步渐高而没。
    　　豫章太守贾雍有神术，出界讨贼，为贼所杀，失头，上马回营，胸中语曰：“战不利，为贼所伤，诸君视有头佳乎？无头佳乎？”吏涕泣曰：“有头佳。”雍云：“不然，无头亦佳。”言毕遂死。 [91]

    Relevant Explanation

    Explanation

    [91]

    Idiomatic Expressions

    • 晨起 - Literally 'rise in the morning,' a common phrase indicating the start of the day.
    • 赉板 - A classical term meaning 'to deliver a tablet' or 'to present a document,' often used in formal or ceremonial contexts.
    • 召为鲁郡太守 - A formal announcement of being summoned to serve as the Governor of Lu County.
    • 言讫便去 - Means 'after speaking, he left,' a concise way to describe someone departing after delivering a message.
    • 奄忽而绝 - A euphemism for sudden death, often used in classical texts to describe unexpected passing.
    • 南出 - Means 'southerly departure,' indicating the direction of movement.
    • 百余步渐高而没 - A vivid description of gradually ascending and disappearing, often used to depict supernatural or ethereal phenomena.

    Cultural and Historical Allusions

    • 广陵 - Refers to Guangling, an ancient name for Yangzhou, a region in Jiangsu Province, China.
    • 鲁郡 - An ancient administrative division, roughly corresponding to present-day Shandong Province.
    • 豫章太守贾雍 - Refers to Jia Yong, the Governor of Yuzhang (present-day Nanchang, Jiangxi Province), who is known for his supernatural abilities.
    • 神术 - Refers to magical or supernatural abilities, often attributed to figures in classical texts.
    • 出界讨贼 - Means 'to cross the border to fight bandits,' indicating military action beyond one's jurisdiction.
    • 失头 - Means 'losing one's head,' a metaphor for death or severe injury.
    • 上马回营 - Means 'to mount the horse and return to camp,' a common military action.
    • 胸中语 - Means 'speaking from the chest,' a metaphor for speaking or communicating after death.
    • 有头佳乎？无头佳乎？ - A rhetorical question asking whether it is better to have a head or not, reflecting the character's acceptance of his fate.

    Proper Nouns

    • 广陵 - The ancient name for Yangzhou, Jiangsu Province.
    • 鲁郡 - An ancient administrative division in Shandong Province.
    • 刘青松 - The name of the protagonist, Duan Qing Song.
    • 豫章太守 - The title of the Governor of Yuzhang (Nanchang, Jiangxi Province).
    • 贾雍 - The name of the Governor known for his supernatural abilities.
    • 鲁郡太守 - The title of the Governor of Lu County.
    • 战不利 - A term indicating a military defeat or unfavorable battle conditions.
    • 贼 - Refers to bandits or rebels, often used in the context of military conflict.

    Duan Qing Song of Guangling rose early one morning and saw a man dressed in official robes, presenting a tablet that said, "You are summoned to serve as the Governor of Lu County." After speaking, the man left, and after he departed, Duan saw him no more. The next day, the man returned and said, "You should take up your new post." Duan realized he was going to die, so he arranged his affairs with his wife and children, bathed, and prepared himself. By the afternoon, he saw a carriage and attendants standing by his side. Suddenly, Duan Qing Song passed away. His family saw him ascend into the carriage, moving south, and gradually ascending into the sky.

    Jia Yong, the Governor of Yuzhang, possessed supernatural abilities. He crossed the border to fight bandits and was killed by them, losing his head. Mounting his horse, he returned to camp, and from his chest, he spoke, "The battle was unfavorable, and I was wounded by the bandits. Do you think it is better to have a head or not?" The attendants wept and said, "It is better to have a head." Jia Yong replied, "Not so; it is also good without a head." After saying this, he died. [91]

    吕顺丧妇，更娶妻之从妹，因作三墓，构累垂就，辄无成。一日，顺昼卧，见其妇来，就同衾，体冷如冰，顺以死生之隔语使去。后妇又见其妹，怒曰：“天下男子独何限，汝乃与我共一婿！作冢不成，我使然也。”俄而，夫妇俱殪。
    　　衡阳太守王矩为广州。矩至长沙，见一人长丈余，着白布单衣，将奏在岸上呼矩奴子：“过我！”矩省奏，为杜灵之，入船共语，称叙希阔。矩问：“君京兆人，何时发来？”答矩：“朝发。”矩怪问之，杜曰：“天上京兆，身是鬼，见使来诣君耳！”矩大惧。因求纸笔，曰：“君必不解天上书。”乃更作，折卷之，从矩求一小箱盛之，封付矩曰：“君今无开，比到广州，可视耳。”矩到数月，悁悒，乃开视。书云：“令召王矩为左司命主簿。”矩意大恶，因疾卒。 [92]

    Relevant Explanation

    Explanation

    [92]

    Idiomatic Expressions

    • 更娶妻之从妹 - Remarried his wife's cousin, indicating a specific familial relationship in the marriage.
    • 构累垂就，辄无成 - Though repeatedly constructed, it always failed to be completed, suggesting an ongoing but unsuccessful attempt.
    • 体冷如冰 - Cold as ice, describing the deceased wife's state.
    • 以死生之隔语使去 - Using the words of separation by life and death to send her away, implying a respectful and formal farewell.
    • 天下男子独何限 - Are there no limits to the number of men in the world? A rhetorical question emphasizing the abundance of potential suitors.
    • 作冢不成，我使然也 - It is my fault that the tombs were not built, showing the wife's self-blame.
    • 夫妇俱殪 - Both husband and wife died, indicating a tragic end.
    • 朝发 - Left in the morning, indicating a specific time of departure.
    • 天上京兆，身是鬼 - I am from the celestial Jingzhao, and I am a ghost, introducing a supernatural element.
    • 见使来诣君耳 - I have been commanded to come see you, explaining the ghost's mission.
    • 悁悒 - Anxious or depressed, describing Wang Ju's emotional state.
    • 令召王矩为左司命主簿 - Commanded to appoint Wang Ju as a clerk in the Left Commandant's office, a supernatural message.
    • 大惧 - Extremely afraid, describing Wang Ju's reaction.
    • 折卷之，从矩求一小箱盛之，封付矩曰 - Folded it up, asked Wang Ju for a small box to put it in, sealed it, and handed it to Wang Ju saying, describing the ghost's instructions.
    • 比到广州 - When he arrives in Guangzhou, indicating a future event.
    • 因疾卒 - Died of illness, describing the cause of death.

    Cultural and Historical Allusions

    • 吕顺丧妇，更娶妻之从妹 - Refers to a common practice in ancient China where widowers might remarry close relatives, often to maintain family ties.
    • 衡阳太守王矩为广州 - Describes a political appointment, reflecting the administrative structure of the time.
    • 天上京兆，身是鬼 - Introduces a supernatural element, which is common in Chinese folklore and literature.
    • 左司命主簿 - A specific official position in the bureaucracy, indicating the hierarchical structure of government.

    Proper Nouns

    • 吕顺 - The name of the husband.
    • 衡阳太守王矩 - The name of the governor of Hengyang.
    • 广州 - The name of the province where Wang Ju was appointed.
    • 杜灵之 - The name of the ghost.
    • 左司命主簿 - The name of the official position.

    吕顺的妻子去世后，他再娶了妻子的堂妹。他们开始建造三个墓地，但每次建到一半就无法完成。一天，吕顺白天睡觉时，见到他的亡妻前来，两人共盖一条被子，她的身体冷得像冰一样。吕顺用生死之别的语言让她离开。后来，他的妻子又见到她的妹妹，愤怒地说：“天下的男人难道没有限制吗？你竟然与我共用一个丈夫！因为墓地建不成，都是我的原因。”不久之后，这对夫妇都去世了。

    衡阳太守王矩被任命为广州太守。王矩到达长沙时，看到一个身高一丈多的人，穿着白色单衣，站在岸边呼唤王矩的仆人：“过来！”王矩认出这个人是杜灵之，便上了船与他交谈，寒暄一番。王矩问他：“你是京兆人，什么时候来的？”杜灵之回答说：“早上来的。”王矩感到奇怪，便问他是谁。杜灵之说：“我是天上的京兆人，我是鬼，被命令来见你！”王矩非常害怕。于是，杜灵之要来纸笔，说：“你肯定看不懂天上的文书。”然后重新写了一封信，折叠好，向王矩要了一个小箱子装好，封上交给王矩说：“你现在不要打开，等你到达广州时再看。”王矩到了广州几个月后，心情忧郁，便打开了信。信上写着：“命令召王矩为左司命主簿。”王矩心中大为不悦，不久因病去世。 [92]

    马仲叔、王志都并辽东人也，相知至厚。叔先亡，后年，忽形见，谓曰：“吾不幸早亡，心恒相念。念卿无妇，当为卿得妇。期至十一月二十日送诣卿家，但扫除设床席待之。”至日，都密扫除施设。天忽大风，白日昼昏。向暮，风止。寝室中忽有红帐自施，发视其中，床上有一妇，花媚庄严，卧床上，才能气息。中表内外惊怖，无敢近者。唯都得往。须臾，便苏起坐，都问：“卿是谁？”妇曰：“我河南人，父为清河太守，临当见嫁，不知何由，忽然在此。”都具语其意。妇曰：“天应令我为君妻。”遂成夫妇。往诣其家，大喜，亦以为天相与也。遂与之生一男，后为南郡太守。
    　　会稽贺思令善弹琴，尝夜在月中坐，临风抚奏。忽有一人，形器甚伟，着械，有惨色。至其中庭称善，便与共语。自云是嵇中散，谓贺云：“卿下手极快，但于古法未合。”因授以《广陵散》。贺因得之，于今不绝。 [93]

    Relevant Explanation

    Explanation

    [93]

    Idiomatic Expressions

    • 相知至厚 - Describes a deep friendship.
    • 形见 - Refers to the appearance of a ghost or spirit.
    • 心恒相念 - Expresses constant remembrance.
    • 当为卿得妇 - Promises to find a wife for someone.
    • 期至十一月二十日送诣卿家 - Sets a specific date for delivering the wife.
    • 但扫除设床席待之 - Instructs to prepare the house and bed.
    • 天忽大风，白日昼昏 - Describes a sudden strong wind causing daylight to dim.
    • 向暮，风止 - Indicates the wind stopping as evening approaches.
    • 寝室中忽有红帐自施 - Describes a red canopy appearing in the bedroom.
    • 发视其中，床上有一妇，花媚庄严，卧床上，才能气息 - Describes a beautiful and dignified woman lying on the bed, barely alive.
    • 中表内外惊怖，无敢近者 - Describes the family's fear and reluctance to approach the woman.
    • 唯都得往 - Indicates that only Wang Zhi was able to approach.
    • 须臾，便苏起坐 - Describes the woman quickly waking up and sitting up.
    • 卿是谁 - Asks who the woman is.
    • 我河南人，父为清河太守，临当见嫁，不知何由，忽然在此 - Explains the woman's background and how she ended up there.
    • 天应令我为君妻 - Suggests that it is fate for the woman to become Wang Zhi's wife.
    • 遂成夫妇 - Indicates that they became husband and wife.
    • 往诣其家，大喜，亦以为天相与也 - Describes their joy and belief that it was a divine match.
    • 遂与之生一男，后为南郡太守 - States that they had a son who later became the governor of Nan Commandery.
    • 卿下手极快，但于古法未合 - Critiques He Siling's playing, saying it is fast but not in line with ancient methods.
    • 因授以《广陵散》 - Indicates that Ji Kang teaches He Siling the 'Guangling San' (a famous qin piece).
    • 贺因得之，于今不绝 - States that He Siling learned the piece and it has been passed down ever since.

    Cultural and Historical Allusions

    • 马仲叔、王志 - Names of historical figures, possibly from the Three Kingdoms period.
    • 辽东 - A region in modern-day Liaoning Province, historically significant.
    • 嵇中散 - Refers to Ji Kang, a famous scholar and musician of the Three Kingdoms period.
    • 广陵散 - A famous qin piece attributed to Ji Kang, known for its complexity and emotional depth.
    • 会稽 - An ancient name for Shaoxing, Zhejiang Province, known for its cultural significance.
    • 南郡 - An ancient commandery in Hubei Province, historically important.

    Proper Nouns

    • 马仲叔 - Ma Zhongshu, a historical figure.
    • 王志 - Wang Zhi, a historical figure.
    • 辽东 - A historical region.
    • 嵇中散 - Ji Kang, a famous scholar and musician.
    • 广陵散 - A famous qin piece.
    • 会稽 - An ancient name for Shaoxing.
    • 南郡 - An ancient commandery.
    • 贺思令 - He Siling, a skilled musician.

    Ma Zhongshu and Wang Zhi were both from Liaodong, and they were very close friends. Ma Zhongshu passed away first, and a year later, his spirit appeared to Wang Zhi and said, "I have unfortunately died young, and I have always been thinking of you. I remember that you do not have a wife, so I will find one for you. I will send her to your home on November 20th. Just clean the house and set up the bed to welcome her." On the day, Wang Zhi secretly cleaned the house and prepared everything. Suddenly, a strong wind blew, and daylight dimmed. As evening approached, the wind stopped. In the bedroom, a red canopy appeared by itself. When they looked inside, there was a woman on the bed, beautiful and dignified, lying there, barely alive. The family inside and out were terrified and dared not approach her. Only Wang Zhi could go near. Soon, the woman woke up and sat up. Wang Zhi asked, "Who are you?" The woman replied, "I am from Henan. My father was the governor of Qinghe, and I was about to be married, but I don't know why I am here now." Wang Zhi explained the situation to her. The woman said, "It seems fate has brought me to be your wife." They became husband and wife. When they went to Wang Zhi's family, everyone was very happy and believed it was a divine match. They had a son who later became the governor of Nan Commandery.

    He Siling, a skilled musician from Kuaiji, once played the qin under the moonlight at night. Suddenly, a man with a large and sad appearance, wearing shackles, appeared in the middle of the courtyard and praised him. He then started a conversation with He Siling. The man introduced himself as Ji Kang and told He Siling, "Your playing is very fast, but it does not conform to the ancient methods." He then taught He Siling the "Guangling San." He Siling learned the piece, and it has been passed down to this day. [93]

    巨鹿有庞阿者，美容仪。同郡石氏有女，曾内睹阿，心悦之。未几，阿见此女来诣阿，阿妻极妒，闻之，使婢缚之，送还石家，中路遂化为烟气而灭。婢乃直诣石家，说此事。石氏之父大惊，曰：“我女都不出门，岂可毁谤如此？”阿妇自是常加意伺察之。居一夜，方值女在斋中，乃自拘执以诣石氏。石氏父见之，愕眙曰：“我适从内来，见女与母共作，何得在此？”即令婢仆于内唤女出，向所缚者，奄然灭焉。父疑有异，故遣其母诘之。女曰：“昔年庞阿来厅中，曾窃视之。自尔彷佛即梦诣阿，及入户，即为妻所缚。”石曰：“天下遂有如此奇事！”夫棈神所感，灵神为之冥着，灭者，盖其魂神也。既而女誓心不嫁。经年，阿妻忽得邪病，医药无征，阿乃授币石氏女为妻。
    　　会稽国司理令朱宗之，常见亡人殡，去头三尺许，有一青物，状如覆瓮。人或当其处则灭，人去随复见，凡尸头无不有此青物者。又云，人殡时，鬼无不暂还临之。 [94]

    Relevant Explanation

    Explanation

    [94]

    Idiomatic Expressions

    • 中路遂化为烟气而灭 - Describes the supernatural transformation of turning into smoke and vanishing.
    • 夫棈神所感，灵神为之冥着 - Explains that spirits are moved by emotions, and divine spirits manifest themselves.

    Cultural and Historical Allusions

    • 巨鹿 - A prefecture in ancient China.
    • 庞阿 - A man's name, possibly a historical or fictional character.
    • 石氏 - The Shi family, a family name in ancient China.
    • 灵神 - Divine spirits, often associated with supernatural phenomena in Chinese folklore.
    • 魂神 - The soul or spirit, often used in the context of spiritual or supernatural events.

    Proper Nouns

    • 巨鹿 - A prefecture in ancient China.
    • 庞阿 - A man's name.
    • 石氏 - The Shi family, a family name in ancient China.
    • 灵神 - Divine spirits.
    • 魂神 - The soul or spirit.

    In Julu, there was a man named Pang A, who was handsome and well-mannered. In the same prefecture, there was a girl from the Shi family who once saw Pang A and fell in love with him. Soon after, Pang A saw this girl coming to visit him. Pang A's wife was extremely jealous, heard of this, and had a maid bind her and send her back to the Shi family. On the way, she turned into smoke and vanished. The maid then went directly to the Shi family and told them about the incident. The father of the Shi family was greatly surprised and said, 'My daughter never leaves the house, how can she be defamed like this?' Pang A's wife then became very suspicious and kept watch on her. One night, she happened to find the girl in her study and had her arrested and brought to the Shi family. The father of the Shi family saw her and was shocked, saying, 'I just came from inside and saw the girl working with her mother, how could she be here?' He immediately had his maids and servants call the girl out, and the one who was bound vanished suddenly. The father suspected something unusual and sent her mother to question her. The girl said, 'Last year, when Pang A came to the hall, I secretly looked at him. Since then, I have often dreamed of visiting him, and when I entered the house, I was bound by his wife.' The Shi family said, 'There are such strange things in the world!' It is said that the spirits are moved by emotions, and the divine spirits manifest themselves. The one who vanished is probably her soul. After that, the girl vowed not to marry. After a year, Pang A's wife suddenly fell ill with a strange disease, and no medicine could cure her. Pang A then gave gifts to the Shi family girl and married her.

    In the state of Kuaiji, the judicial officer Zhu Zong often saw that when a person was buried, about three feet above the head, there was a blue object shaped like an inverted pot. When someone stood in that place, the object would disappear, and when the person left, it would reappear. This blue object was always present above the heads of the deceased. It was also said that when a person was buried, their spirit would temporarily return to the burial site.

    This translation aims to maintain the original meaning and cultural context while ensuring the text reads naturally in English. [94]

    新野庾谨母病，兄弟三人，悉在侍疾。忽闻床前狗斗，声非常。举家共视，了不见狗，只见一死人头在地。犹有血，两眼尚动。其家怖惧，夜持出，于后园中埋之。明旦视之，出在土上，两眼犹尔。即又埋之，后旦已复出。乃以砖着头，令埋之，不复出。后数日，其母遂亡。
    　　东阳丁哗出郭，于方山亭宿。亭渚有刘散骑遭母丧，于京葬还。夜中，忽有一妇自通云：“刘郎患疮，闻参军能治，故来耳。”哗使前，姿形端媚，从婢数人。命仆具肴馔，酒酣，叹曰：“今夕之会，令人无复贞白之操。”丁云：“女郎盛德，岂顾老夫？”便令婢取瑟琶弹之，歌曰：“久闻所重名，今遇方山亭。肌体虽朽老，故是悦人情。”放瑟琶上膝，抱头又歌曰：“女形虽薄贱，愿得忻作婿。缱绻覌良觌，千载结同契。”声气婉媚，令人绝倒。便令灭火，共展好情。比晓，忽不见。吏云：“此亭旧有妖魅。” [95]

    Relevant Explanation

    Explanation

    [95]

    Idiomatic Expressions

    • 声非常 - Unusual sound, suggesting a supernatural event.
    • 了不见狗 - Clear absence, emphasizing the supernatural nature of the event.
    • 两眼尚动 - Eyes still moving, adding a sense of horror and the supernatural.
    • 夜持出 - Taking out at night, implying secrecy and the family's fear.
    • 明旦 - By the next day, emphasizing the persistence of the supernatural event.
    • 乃以砖着头 - Placing a brick on the head, suggesting a desperate measure.
    • 遂亡 - Finally died, indicating a finality, possibly linked to the supernatural event.
    • 出郭 - Leaving the city, setting the scene for the supernatural encounter.
    • 遭母丧 - Losing a mother, setting the emotional context for the encounter.
    • 患疮 - Having a wound, setting the context for the supernatural encounter.
    • 盛德 - Great virtue, emphasizing the woman's character.
    • 所重名 - Esteemed name, emphasizing the woman's reputation.
    • 薄贱 - Humble and lowly, emphasizing the woman's self-deprecation.
    • 绝倒 - Falling over, often used to describe being overcome by something.
    • 展好情 - Sharing intimate moments, emphasizing the intimate nature of the encounter.
    • 比晓 - By dawn, emphasizing the sudden disappearance of the woman.
    • 旧有 - Has had in the past, emphasizing the historical significance of the pavilion.

    Cultural and Historical Allusions

    • 新野 - A county in present-day Henan Province, historically significant.
    • 庾谨 - A person's name, likely a historical figure or a fictional character.
    • 东阳 - A place name, possibly referring to a city or region.
    • 丁哗 - A person's name, likely a historical figure or a fictional character.
    • 方山亭 - A pavilion, possibly a specific location with historical significance.
    • 刘散骑 - A title, possibly referring to a high-ranking official or a historical figure.
    • 瑟琶 - A musical instrument, similar to a lute.
    • 妖魅 - Ghosts and demons, often associated with supernatural phenomena in Chinese culture.

    Proper Nouns

    • 新野 - A place name.
    • 庾谨 - A person's name.
    • 东阳 - A place name.
    • 丁哗 - A person's name.
    • 方山亭 - A place name.
    • 刘散骑 - A title.
    • 瑟琶 - A musical instrument.
    • 妖魅 - Ghosts and demons.

    The mother of Yin Jin from Xinye was ill, and her three brothers were all tending to her illness. Suddenly, they heard a dog fighting in front of the bed, with an unusual sound. The whole family looked together, but saw no dog; only a dead human head on the ground. It still had blood, and the eyes were still moving. The family was terrified, so they took it out at night and buried it in the back garden. The next morning when they looked, it was out of the ground again, and the eyes were still moving. They then placed a brick on its head and buried it again, and it did not come out again. Several days later, their mother died.

    Ding Hua from Dongyang went out of the city and stayed at Fangshan Pavilion. At the pavilion, there was a man named Liu Sanqi who had lost his mother and returned from burying her in the capital. At night, suddenly a woman introduced herself, saying, "Liu Lang has a wound, and I've heard that you can treat it, so I came." Ding said, "Lady, your virtue is great, how could you concern yourself with an old man like me?" She then had her maidservants fetch a lute and play it, singing, "I've long heard of your esteemed name, now meeting you at Fangshan Pavilion. Though my body is old and decaying, it still delights human feelings." She placed the lute on her knee and sang again, holding her head, "Though my form is humble and lowly, I wish to be delighted as your husband. Intimate and close, we meet in good times, and for a thousand years, we will be bound by the same covenant." Her voice and demeanor were so charming that it made people fall over. She then had the lights extinguished and shared intimate moments. By dawn, she had suddenly disappeared. The officials said, "This pavilion has had ghosts and demons in the past." [95]

    京兆董奇，庭前有大树，阴映甚佳。后霖雨，奇独在家乡，有小吏言云：“承云府君来。”乃见承云，着通天冠，长八尺，自称为方伯，“某第三子有隽才，方当与君周旋。”明日，觉树下有异，每晡后无人，辄有一少年，就奇语戏，或命取饮食。如是半年，奇气强壮，一门无疾。奇后适下墅，其仆客三人送护，言：“树材可用，欲货之，郎常不听，今试共斩斫之。”奇遂许之。神亦自尔绝矣。 [96]

    Relevant Explanation

    Explanation

    [96]

    Idiomatic Expressions

    • 京兆 - Refers to the administrative region of Jingzhao, a major commandery during the Han Dynasty.
    • 庭前有大树，阴映甚佳 - Describes a large tree in the courtyard with excellent shade.
    • 霖雨 - Means heavy rain or continuous rain.
    • 奇独在家乡 - Indicates that Dong Qi was alone in his hometown.
    • 小吏言云 - Means 'a minor official said.'
    • 承云府君来 - 'Chengyun Fu Jun came,' where 'Chengyun' is a proper name and 'Fu Jun' is a title.
    • 着通天冠，长八尺 - Describes Chengyun wearing a ceremonial hat and being eight feet tall.
    • 方伯 - A high-ranking official or governor.
    • 某第三子有隽才 - Refers to the third son of Chengyun having exceptional talent.
    • 方当与君周旋 - Means 'will be with you in the future' or 'will interact with you.'
    • 明日，觉树下有异 - 'The next day, he noticed something unusual under the tree.'
    • 每晡后无人 - 'Every evening after sunset, when there was no one around.'
    • 辄有一少年，就奇语戏 - 'There would always be a young man who would come to talk to Dong Qi.'
    • 或命取饮食 - 'Sometimes he would order food and drink.'
    • 如是半年 - 'Like this for half a year.'
    • 奇气强壮 - 'Dong Qi became strong and healthy.'
    • 一门无疾 - 'No one in his family fell ill.'
    • 奇后适下墅 - 'Later, Dong Qi went to a suburban area.'
    • 其仆客三人送护 - 'Three of his servants and guests accompanied him.'
    • 言树材可用，欲货之 - 'They said the tree could be used, and they wanted to sell it.'
    • 郎常不听 - 'Dong Qi usually wouldn't allow it.'
    • 今试共斩斫之 - 'Now let's try to cut it down together.'
    • 神亦自尔绝矣 - 'The spirit also disappeared from then on.'

    Cultural and Historical Allusions

    • 京兆 - Refers to the Jingzhao commandery, a significant administrative region during the Han Dynasty.
    • 通天冠 - A ceremonial hat worn by high-ranking officials.
    • 方伯 - A high-ranking official or governor, often used in classical texts to denote a powerful local ruler.
    • 隽才 - Exceptional talent, often used to describe someone with remarkable abilities.
    • 承云府君 - A proper name and title, likely referring to a deity or a high-ranking official.
    • 下墅 - A suburban area, often used in classical texts to describe a place outside the city.

    Proper Nouns

    • 京兆 - The administrative region of Jingzhao.
    • 董奇 - The main character, Dong Qi.
    • 承云府君 - A proper name and title, likely referring to a deity or a high-ranking official.
    • 方伯 - A high-ranking official or governor.
    • 下墅 - A suburban area.

    Dong Qi of Jingzhao had a large tree in front of his courtyard, which provided excellent shade. After a period of continuous rain, Dong Qi was alone in his hometown when a minor official told him, "Chengyun Fu Jun has come." Dong Qi then saw Chengyun, who was wearing a ceremonial hat and was eight feet tall, and introduced himself as a high-ranking official, saying, "My third son has exceptional talent and will be with you in the future." The next day, Dong Qi noticed something unusual under the tree. Every evening after sunset, when there was no one around, there would always be a young man who would come to talk to Dong Qi, sometimes ordering food and drink. This continued for half a year, during which Dong Qi became strong and healthy, and no one in his family fell ill. Later, Dong Qi went to a suburban area, accompanied by three of his servants and guests. They said the tree could be used and they wanted to sell it, noting that Dong Qi usually wouldn't allow it. Now, they decided to try cutting it down together. The spirit also disappeared from then on. [96]

    清河郡太守至，前后辄死。新太守到，如厕，有人长三尺，冠帻皂服，云：“府君某日死。”太守不应，意甚不乐，催使吏为作主人，外颇怪。其日日中，如厕，复见前所见人，言：“府君今日中当死。”三言，亦不应。乃言：“府君当道而不道，鼠为死。”乃顿仆地，大如豚。郡内遂安。
    　　此虞魏虔祖婢，名皮纳，有色，徐密乐之。鼠乃托为其形而就密宿。密心疑之，以手摩其四体，便觉缩小，因化为鼠而走。
    　　晋陵民蔡兴忽得狂疾，歌吟不恒。常空中与数人言笑。或云：“当再取谁女？”复一人云：“家已多。”后夜，忽闻十余人将物入里人刘余之家。余之拔刀出后户，见一人黑色，大骂曰：“我湖长，来诣汝，而欲杀我？”即唤：“群伴何不助余邪？”余之即奋刀乱砍，得一大鼍及狸。 [97]

    Relevant Explanation

    Explanation

    [97]

    Idiomatic Expressions

    • 清河郡太守至，前后辄死 - Hyperbolic expression indicating that the previous governors of Qinghe Commandery died shortly after their arrival.
    • 如厕 - Literal meaning 'to go to the toilet,' but in this context, it likely refers to a specific place or ritual.
    • 冠帻皂服 - Describes a figure wearing a hat (冠), a headband (帻), and a black robe (皂服).
    • 府君某日死 - Direct and ominous statement predicting the death of the governor on a specific day.
    • 当道而不道 - Play on words, where '道' can mean both 'to follow the right path' and 'to speak' or 'to say.'
    • 鼠为死 - Pun on '鼠' (rat) and '死' (death), suggesting that the rat is a harbinger of death.
    • 如厕，复见前所见人 - Another instance of the figure appearing again, reinforcing the supernatural element.
    • 群伴何不助余邪 - Rhetorical question asking why the companions did not help, implying a sense of betrayal or confusion.
    • 大如豚 - Describes the size of the figure as being as large as a pig (豚).

    Cultural and Historical Allusions

    • 清河郡 - Commandery during the Han Dynasty, located in what is now Hebei Province.
    • 太守 - Highest-ranking official in a commandery, equivalent to a governor.
    • 府君 - Respectful title for a governor or high-ranking official.
    • 虞魏虔祖 - Historical figure, possibly a general or a notable person from the Han Dynasty.
    • 婢，名皮纳 - Female servant named Pinna.
    • 徐密 - Person, possibly a friend or associate of虞魏虔祖.
    • 鼍 - Large, crocodile-like reptile, often associated with water and power in Chinese mythology.
    • 狸 - Wild cat or lynx, often associated with cunning and stealth.

    Proper Nouns

    • 清河郡 - Qinghe Commandery.
    • 太守 - Governor.
    • 府君 - Lord or Governor.
    • 虞魏虔祖 - Yu Wei Qianzu.
    • 皮纳 - Pinna.
    • 徐密 - Xu Mi.
    • 鼍 - Toad or Crocodile.
    • 狸 - Lynx or Wild Cat.

    Translation:

    Whenever a new governor of Qinghe Commandery arrived, they would die shortly afterward. When the new governor arrived, he went to the toilet and saw a figure three feet tall, wearing a hat, a headband, and a black robe. The figure said, "The lord will die on a certain day." The governor did not respond, and he felt very unhappy. He ordered his attendants to make the figure a host, and the people outside found this strange. On that day at noon, when the governor went to the toilet again, he saw the same figure and said, "The lord will die today at noon." The figure repeated this three times, and the governor still did not respond. Then the figure said, "The lord should follow the right path but does not, and the rat will die." The figure then fell to the ground, as large as a pig. The commandery then became peaceful.

    This was a servant of Yu Wei Qianzu named Pinna, who was beautiful. Xu Mi was fond of her. The rat then took her form and joined Xu Mi in his bed. Xu Mi suspected something and touched her body with his hand, and he felt it shrink. She then transformed into a rat and ran away.

    A man from Jinling named Cai Xing suddenly developed a mad illness, singing and chanting irregularly. He often spoke and laughed with several people in the air. Someone said, "We will take another girl." Another person said, "We already have many." One night, he suddenly heard ten or more people bringing things into the home of a man named Liu Yu. Liu Yu drew his sword and went out the back door, and he saw a black figure. He shouted, "I am the chief of the lake, coming to see you, and you want to kill me?" He then called out, "Why do not my companions help me?" Liu Yu then swung his sword wildly and killed a large toad and a lynx. [97]

    江淮有妇人，为性多欲，存想不舍日夜。尝醉，旦起，见屋后二少童，甚鲜洁，如宫小吏者。妇因欲抱持，忽成扫帚，取而焚之。
    　　东魏徐，忘名，还作本郡，卒，墓在东安灵山。墓先为人所发，棺柩已毁。谢玄在彭城，将有齐郡司马隆，弟进，及安东王箱，等。共取坏棺，分以作车。少时，三人悉见患，更相注连，凶祸不已。箱母灵语子孙云：“箱昔与司马隆兄弟取徐府君墓中棺为车，隆等死亡丧破，皆由此也。”
    　　秦高平李羡家奴健，至石头冈，忽见一人云：“妇与人通情，遂为所杀，欲报仇，岂能见助？”奴用其言，果见人来。鬼便捉头，奴换与手，实时倒地，还半路，便死。鬼以千钱一匹青绞緵袍与奴，嘱云：“此袍是市西门丁与许，君可自着，勿卖也。” [98]

    Relevant Explanation

    Explanation

    [98]

    Idiomatic Expressions

    • 江淮有婦人 - Literal: 'There is a woman in the Jianghuai region.'
    • 為性多欲 - Literal: 'Her nature is full of desires.'
    • 存想不舍日夜 - Literal: 'She constantly thinks and cannot stop day and night.'
    • 嘗醉 - Literal: 'Once drunk.'
    • 旦起 - Literal: 'In the morning, she got up.'
    • 見屋後二少童 - Literal: 'She saw two young boys behind her house.'
    • 甚鮮潔 - Literal: 'Very clean and fresh.'
    • 如宫小吏者 - Literal: 'Like small officials in the palace.'
    • 因欲抱持 - Literal: 'She wanted to embrace and hold them.'
    • 忽成掃帚 - Literal: 'Suddenly turned into a broom.'
    • 取而焚之 - Literal: 'She took it and burned it.'
    • 東魏徐 - Literal: 'Xu of Dong Wei.'
    • 忘名 - Literal: 'Forgot his name.'
    • 還作本郡 - Literal: 'Returned to his native prefecture.'
    • 卒 - Literal: 'Died.'
    • 墓在東安靈山 - Literal: 'His tomb is in Ling Mountain, Dong'an.'
    • 墓先為人所發 - Literal: 'The tomb was previously looted by someone.'
    • 棺柩已毁 - Literal: 'The coffin and the body were already destroyed.'
    • 謝玄在彭城 - Literal: 'Xie Xuan was in Pengcheng.'
    • 將有齊郡司马隆 - Literal: 'He had Qi Prefecture's司马隆.'
    • 弟進 - Literal: 'His brother Jin.'
    • 及安东王箱 - Literal: 'And Dongan Wang Xiang.'
    • 共取坏棺 - Literal: 'Together they took the ruined coffin.'
    • 分以作车 - Literal: 'Divided it to make carriages.'
    • 少时 - Literal: 'After some time.'
    • 三人悉见患 - Literal: 'All three saw suffering.'
    • 更相注连 - Literal: 'Continuously affecting each other.'
    • 凶祸不已 - Literal: 'The misfortunes did not stop.'
    • 箱母灵语子孙云 - Literal: 'Xiang's mother's spirit spoke to her descendants.'
    • 箱昔与司马隆兄弟取徐府君墓中棺为车 - Literal: 'Xiang and his brothers,司马隆, took the coffin from Xu's tomb to make carriages.'
    • 隆等死亡丧破 - Literal: '司马隆 and others died, and their families were ruined.'
    • 皆由此也 - Literal: 'All because of this.'
    • 秦高平李羡家奴健 - Literal: 'Li Xian of Gao Ping in Qin had a slave named Jian.'
    • 至石头冈 - Literal: 'He went to Shitou Mountain.'
    • 忽见一人 - Literal: 'Suddenly, he saw a person.'
    • 云 - Literal: 'Said.'
    • 妇与人通情 - Literal: 'The woman had an affair with someone.'
    • 遂为所杀 - Literal: 'And was killed by him.'
    • 欲报仇 - Literal: 'Wanted to take revenge.'
    • 岂能见助 - Literal: 'Can you help me?'
    • 奴用其言 - Literal: 'The slave followed his words.'
    • 果见人来 - Literal: 'Indeed, someone came.'
    • 鬼便捉头 - Literal: 'The ghost grabbed his head.'
    • 奴换与手 - Literal: 'The slave switched hands.'
    • 实时倒地 - Literal: 'Immediately fell to the ground.'
    • 还半路 - Literal: 'Returned halfway.'
    • 便死 - Literal: 'Died.'
    • 鬼以千钱一匹青绞緵袍与奴 - Literal: 'The ghost gave the slave a thousand coins and a blue silk robe.'
    • 嘱云 - Literal: 'Told him.'
    • 此袍是市西门丁与许 - Literal: 'This robe was given by Ding of the western gate market.'
    • 君可自着 - Literal: 'You can wear it yourself.'
    • 勿卖也 - Literal: 'Do not sell it.'

    Cultural and Historical Allusions

    • 江淮 - Refers to the region between the Yangtze River and the Huai River, historically a significant area in China.
    • 東魏 - The Eastern Wei was a short-lived dynasty that existed from 534 to 550 AD.
    • 彭城 - Present-day Xuzhou, Jiangsu Province, an important city during the Six Dynasties period.
    • 齊郡 - A prefecture in ancient China, likely referring to a specific administrative division.
    • 東安 - A place name, possibly referring to a specific location in the region.
    • 靈山 - A mountain, likely a specific location in Dong'an.
    • 謝玄 - A prominent general during the Eastern Jin dynasty, known for his military campaigns against the Former Qin.
    • 司马隆 - A name, likely referring to a specific individual.
    • 安东王箱 - A title and name, likely referring to a specific noble or official.
    • 秦高平 - Refers to a region in the state of Qin, a powerful state during the Warring States period.
    • 李羡 - A name, likely referring to a specific individual.
    • 家奴健 - Refers to a slave named Jian.
    • 石头冈 - A mountain or hill, likely a specific location.
    • 市西门丁 - Refers to a person named Ding who lived in the western gate market.
    • 青绞緵袍 - A type of robe, likely a specific garment.

    Proper Nouns

    • 江淮 - Region between the Yangtze River and the Huai River.
    • 東魏 - Eastern Wei dynasty.
    • 彭城 - Present-day Xuzhou, Jiangsu Province.
    • 齊郡 - Ancient Chinese prefecture.
    • 東安 - Place name in the region.
    • 靈山 - Mountain in Dong'an.
    • 謝玄 - Prominent general during the Eastern Jin dynasty.
    • 司马隆 - Name of a specific individual.
    • 安东王箱 - Title and name of a specific noble or official.
    • 秦高平 - Region in the state of Qin.
    • 李羡 - Name of a specific individual.
    • 家奴健 - Slave named Jian.
    • 石头冈 - Mountain or hill.
    • 市西门丁 - Person named Ding who lived in the western gate market.
    • 青绞緵袍 - Type of robe.

    In the Jianghuai region, there was a woman whose nature was filled with desires, and she constantly thought about them day and night. Once, after getting drunk, she woke up in the morning and saw two young boys behind her house, who were very clean and fresh, like small officials in the palace. She wanted to embrace and hold them, but they suddenly turned into a broom, which she then took and burned.

    During the Eastern Wei period, there was a man named Xu, who forgot his name, returned to his native prefecture, and died. His tomb is located in Ling Mountain, Dong'an. The tomb was previously looted by someone, and the coffin and the body were already destroyed. Xie Xuan was in Pengcheng and had Qi Prefecture's司马隆, his brother Jin, and Dongan Wang Xiang. Together, they took the ruined coffin and divided it to make carriages. After some time, all three saw suffering, continuously affecting each other, and the misfortunes did not stop. Xiang's mother's spirit spoke to her descendants, explaining that Xiang and his brothers,司马隆, took the coffin from Xu's tomb to make carriages, and司马隆 and others died, and their families were ruined, all because of this.

    In Qin's Gao Ping, Li Xian had a slave named Jian. One day, Jian went to Shitou Mountain and suddenly saw a person who said that a woman had an affair with someone, was killed by him, and wanted to take revenge, asking if he could be helped. The slave followed his words, and indeed, someone came. The ghost grabbed the slave's head, the slave switched hands, immediately fell to the ground, returned halfway, and died. The ghost gave the slave a thousand coins and a blue silk robe, telling him that this robe was given by Ding of the western gate market, and he could wear it himself but not sell it. [98]

    宋初，义兴周超，为谢晦司马在江陵。妻许氏在家，遥见屋里月光一死人头在地，血流甚多，大惊，怪即便失去。后超被法。
