    • This sequence describes the diviner's advice and the family's actions, and must be read as a continuous narrative.

    This person said: "Yesterday, a ghost helped someone named A, and now it has come again to help me. Can carry grain to give it." Also gave the maid to winnow and sift. By evening, extremely tired, did not give the ghost food. The ghost returned in the evening, greatly angry, said: "I have been married to you, not comparable to others. How could you deceive me? Helped people for two days, did not receive even a bowl of food." The friendly ghost said: "You are just unlucky! These two families worship Buddha and practice Daoism, their emotions are naturally difficult to move. Now go and find ordinary families to cause trouble, then you can do whatever you want." The ghost went again, found one family. There was a bamboo pole at the door, entered through the door. Saw a group of women eating together in front of the window. To the courtyard, there was a white dog, picked up and flew in the air. The family saw it and was greatly surprised, said they had never seen such a strange occurrence. The diviner said: "There is a guest asking for food, can kill the dog and prepare sweet fruits, wine, and rice, worship it in the courtyard, can avoid further trouble." The family followed the diviner's advice, the ghost indeed ate well. Afterward, it always caused trouble, it was due to the friendly ghost's advice. [114]

    东昌县山有物，形如人，长四五尺，裸身被发，发长五六寸。常在高山岩石间住，喑哑作声，而不成语，能啸相呼。常隐于幽昧之间，不可恒见。有人伐木，宿于山中。至夜眠后，此物抱子从涧中发石取虾蟹，就人火边，烧炙以食儿。时人有未眠者，密相觉语，齐起共突击。便走，而遗其子，声如人啼也。此物使男女群共引石击人，趣得然后止。
    　　会稽施子然。……有一人，身着黄练单衣帢，直造席，捧手与子然语。子然问其姓名，即答曰：“仆姓卢，名钩，家在坛溪边临水。”复经半旬中，其作人掘田塍边沟蚁垤，忽见大坎，满中蝼蛄，将近斗许。而有数头极壮，一个弥大。子然至是始悟曰：“近日客称卢钩，反音则蝼蛄也。家在坛溪，即西坎也。”悉灌以沸汤，自是遂绝。 [115]

    Relevant Explanation

    Explanation

    [115]

    Idiomatic Expressions

    • 东昌县山有物 - Introduces the subject of the passage, which is a mysterious creature.
    • 形如人，长四五尺 - Describes the physical appearance of the creature.
    • 裸身被发，发长五六寸 - Further describes the creature's appearance.
    • 常在高山岩石间住 - Indicates the creature's habitat.
    • 喑哑作声，而不成语，能啸相呼 - Describes the creature's vocalizations.
    • 常隐于幽昧之间，不可恒见 - Suggests the creature's elusive nature.
    • 有人伐木，宿于山中 - Introduces the human observer.
    • 至夜眠后，此物抱子从涧中发石取虾蟹 - Describes the creature's nighttime activity.
    • 就人火边，烧炙以食儿 - Shows the creature's behavior towards its child.
    • 时人有未眠者，密相觉语，齐起共突击 - Describes the human reaction to the creature.
    • 便走，而遗其子，声如人啼也 - Shows the creature's response to the attack.
    • 此物使男女群共引石击人，趣得然后止 - Describes the creature's aggressive behavior.
    • 会稽施子然 - Introduces the second part of the passage, which involves a different individual.
    • 有一人，身着黄练单衣帢，直造席，捧手与子然语 - Describes the appearance and behavior of the mysterious person.
    • 子然问其姓名，即答曰：“仆姓卢，卢钩，家在坛溪边临水” - Is the mysterious person's introduction.
    • 复经半旬中，其作人掘田塍边沟蚁垤，忽见大坎，满中蝼蛄，将近斗许 - Describes the discovery of the pit.
    • 而有数头极壮，一个弥大 - Highlights the size and strength of some of the grasshoppers.
    • 子然至是始悟曰：“近日客称卢钩，反音则蝼蛄也。家在坛溪，即西坎也。” - Is Shi Ziran's realization about the mysterious person.
    • 悉灌以沸汤，自是遂绝 - Describes the final action taken against the grasshoppers.

    Cultural and Historical Allusions

    • 东昌县 - Dongchang County, a place in ancient China.
    • 会稽 - Kuaiji, a region in ancient China, now part of modern-day Zhejiang Province.
    • 黄练单衣帢 - A yellow silk single garment and a hat, which were likely part of the attire of the mysterious person.
    • 坛溪 - Tan Creek, a place mentioned in the text as the home of the mysterious person.
    • 蝼蛄 - Grasshoppers, which are mentioned in the text as being associated with the mysterious person.

    Syntactic Ambiguities

    • The phrase '裸身被发' could be interpreted as 'naked with hair covering the body' or 'naked with hair on the body,' but the first interpretation is more likely.
    • The phrase '而不成语' could be interpreted as 'but does not form words' or 'but does not speak in words,' but the first interpretation is more likely.
    • The phrase '不可恒见' could be interpreted as 'not always visible' or 'not constantly seen,' but the first interpretation is more likely.
    • The phrase '从涧中发石取虾蟹' could be interpreted as 'picked up crabs and shrimp from the stream by moving stones' or 'took crabs and shrimp from the stream by moving stones,' but the first interpretation is more likely.
    • The phrase '烧炙以食儿' could be interpreted as 'roasted them to feed its child' or 'cooked them to feed its child,' but the first interpretation is more likely.
    • The phrase '密相觉语' could be interpreted as 'secretly told others' or 'secretly informed others,' but the first interpretation is more likely.
    • The phrase '声如人啼也' could be interpreted as 'making a sound like a human crying' or 'making a sound like a human's cry,' but the first interpretation is more likely.
    • The phrase '趣得然后止' could be interpreted as 'stopping only when they succeeded' or 'stopping only after they succeeded,' but the first interpretation is more likely.
    • The phrase '直造席' could be interpreted as 'came straight to the mat' or 'came directly to the mat,' but the first interpretation is more likely.
    • The phrase '仆姓卢' could be interpreted as 'My name is Lu' or 'I am named Lu,' but the first interpretation is more likely.
    • The phrase '将近斗许' could be interpreted as 'nearly a dou in quantity' or 'almost a dou in quantity,' but the first interpretation is more likely.
    • The phrase '一个弥大' could be interpreted as 'one was particularly large' or 'one was especially large,' but the first interpretation is more likely.
    • The phrase '反音则蝼蛄也' could be interpreted as 'reverse pronunciation is grasshopper' or 'reverse pronunciation is 'lou gu,' which means grasshopper,' but the first interpretation is more likely.

    In Dongchang County, there is a creature in the mountains. Its shape is like a human, about four or five feet tall. It is naked, with hair covering its body, and its hair is about five or six inches long. It usually lives in high mountains and rocky areas. It makes a hoarse sound but does not form words, and it can howl to call each other. It often hides in dark and obscure places, and it is not always visible. There was a person cutting wood and staying in the mountains. After the person went to sleep at night, the creature took its child and picked up crabs and shrimp from the stream by moving stones. It brought the crabs and shrimp to the fire and roasted them to feed its child. At that time, someone who had not yet fallen asleep secretly told others, and they all got up and attacked together. The creature ran away and left its child, making a sound like a human crying. This creature made both men and women gather stones to attack people, stopping only when they succeeded.

    Shi Ziran from Kuaiji. … There was a person wearing a yellow silk single garment and a hat, who came straight to the mat and held out his hand to speak with Shi Ziran. Shi Ziran asked for his name, and he replied, "My name is Lu Gou, and my home is by the water at Tan Creek." After about ten days, the person working in the fields dug near the ditch and ant mounds, and suddenly saw a large pit filled with grasshoppers, nearly a dou in quantity. There were several very strong ones, and one was particularly large. Shi Ziran suddenly realized, "The guest recently called himself Lu Gou, but the reversed pronunciation is 'lou gu,' which means grasshopper. His home is at Tan Creek, which is the west pit." They all poured boiling water over them, and from then on, they were completely eliminated. [115]

    吴兴徐长夙与鲍南海神有神明之交，欲授以秘术，先谓徐“宜有纳誓”。徐誓以不仕，于是受箓。常见八大神在侧，能知来见往，才识日异。县乡翕然有美谈，欲用为县主簿。徐心悦之，八神一朝不见其七，余一人倨傲不如常。徐问其故，答云：“君违誓，不复相为。使身一人留卫箓耳！”徐仍还箓，遂退。
    　　彭虎子少壮有膂力，常谓无鬼神。母死，俗巫戒之云：“某日殃杀当还，重有所杀，宜出避之。”合家细弱，悉出逃隐，虎子独留不去。夜中，有人排门入，至东西屋觅人，不得，次入屋，向庐室中。虎子遑遽无计，床头先有一瓮，便入其中，以板盖头。觉母在板上，有人问：“板下无人邪？”母云：“无。”相率而去。 [116]

    Relevant Explanation

    Explanation

    [116]

    Idiomatic Expressions

    • 夙与鲍南海神有神明之交 - Long-standing, almost mystical relationship between Xu Chang and the God of South Sea.
    • 宜有纳誓 - Suggests that Xu should make a solemn promise.
    • 受箓 - Xu received a sacred text or talisman.
    • 八大神在侧 - Eight major deities were present.
    • 才识日异 - Xu's talent and knowledge grew daily.
    • 翕然有美谈 - People spoke highly of Xu.
    • 欲用为县主簿 - The people wanted to appoint Xu as a county clerk.
    • 心悦之 - Xu was pleased with the offer.
    • 八神一朝不见其七 - Suddenly, seven of the eight deities were no longer present.
    • 倨傲不如常 - The remaining deity was arrogant and not as usual.
    • 君违誓，不复相为 - The deity said Xu had broken his oath and would no longer serve him.
    • 使身一人留卫箓耳 - The deity left one person to guard the talisman.
    • 仍还箓 - Xu returned the talisman.
    • 遂退 - Xu withdrew or left.

    Cultural and Historical Allusions

    • 吴兴 - Refers to Wu County in Zhejiang Province, known for its cultural and historical significance.
    • 鲍南海神 - The God of South Sea, a deity associated with the southern seas in Chinese mythology.
    • 县乡翕然有美谈 - The local community spoke highly of Xu, reflecting the importance of reputation and social standing in ancient Chinese society.
    • 县主簿 - A local government official responsible for administrative duties.
    • 彭虎子 - A historical figure known for his strength and skepticism towards supernatural beliefs.
    • 俗巫 - A village witch or shaman, who often played a significant role in rural communities.
    • 殃杀 - A period of bad luck or misfortune, often associated with death or disaster.
    • 重有所杀 - To cause further death or bring more harm.
    • 出避之 - To go out and avoid it, indicating that the family fled to escape the predicted misfortune.
    • 夜中 - At night, setting the scene for the supernatural event.
    • 排门入 - To push the door open and enter, indicating a sudden and forceful intrusion.
    • 庐室 - A simple dwelling or hut.
    • 遑遽 - In a hurry or anxious, indicating Xu's state of mind.
    • 瓮 - A large jar or container, used here as a hiding place.
    • 板盖头 - To cover the head with a board, indicating Xu's attempt to hide.
    • 母在板上 - Mother on the board, indicating that Xu's mother was also hiding.
    • 相率而去 - To go away together, indicating that the intruders left.

    Proper Nouns

    • 吴兴 - A place name in Zhejiang Province.
    • 鲍南海神 - The God of South Sea.
    • 徐长 - The name of the protagonist in the first part.
    • 彭虎子 - The name of the protagonist in the second part.
    • 县主簿 - A local government official.
    • 俗巫 - A village witch or shaman.

    A Man from Wu County and a Man from Peng's Family

    Xu Chang from Wu County had a long-standing, almost mystical friendship with the God of South Sea. The deity wished to bestow upon Xu secret arts, first telling him, "You should take an oath." Xu swore that he would not seek officialdom, and thus received a talisman. He often saw eight major deities by his side, able to know the future and the past, and his talent and knowledge grew daily. The local community spoke highly of Xu, and they wanted to appoint him as a county clerk. Xu was pleased with the offer, but one morning, seven of the eight deities suddenly disappeared, leaving only one who was arrogant and not as usual. Xu asked why this had happened, and the deity replied, "You have broken your oath, and I will no longer serve you. I am leaving one person to guard the talisman!" Xu then returned the talisman and withdrew.

    Peng Huizi, a young and strong man, often claimed that there were no ghosts or spirits. After his mother's death, a village witch warned him, "On a certain day, misfortune will return, and further harm will come. You should go out and avoid it." The entire family, including the women and children, fled to escape the predicted misfortune, but Peng Huizi stayed behind. At night, someone pushed the door open and entered, searching the east and west rooms for people, but finding none, they entered the house and headed towards the simple dwelling. Peng Huizi, in a hurry and with no plan, hid inside a large jar near his bed, covering his head with a board. He felt his mother on the board above him, and someone asked, "Is there no one under the board?" His mother replied, "No." The intruders then left together. [116]

    晋升平元年，任怀仁年十三，为台书佐。乡里有王祖复为令史，恒宠之。怀仁已十五六矣，颇有异意。祖衔恨，至嘉兴，杀怀仁，以棺殡埋于徐祚后田头。祚夜宿息田上，忽见有冢，至朝中暮三时，食辄分以祭之，呼云：“田头鬼，来就我食。”至暝眠时，亦云：“来伴我宿。”如此积时，后夜忽见形云：“我家明当除服作祭，祭甚丰厚，君明随去。”祚云：“我是生人，不当相见。”鬼云：“我自隐君形。”祚便随鬼去，计行食顷，便到其家。家大有客，鬼将祚上灵座，大食灭。合家号泣，不能自胜，谓其儿还。见王祖来，便曰：“此是杀我人，犹畏之。”便走出，祚即形露。家中大惊，因问祚，因叙本末。遂随祚迎丧，既去，鬼便断绝。 [117]

    Relevant Explanation

    Explanation

    [117]

    Idiomatic Expressions

    • 颇有异意 - Suggests that Ren Huairen had some different or unusual ideas.
    • 除服作祭 - Refers to the traditional Chinese practice of removing mourning clothes and performing a memorial service.

    Cultural and Historical Allusions

    • 晋升平元年 - The first year of the Jincheng era during the Eastern Jin Dynasty.
    • 任怀仁 - A historical figure, though the exact details of his life are not widely known.
    • 王祖 - Another historical figure, likely a local official or influential person.
    • 嘉兴 - A city in Zhejiang Province, historically significant for its role in Chinese culture and politics.
    • 徐祚 - A historical figure.

    Proper Nouns

    • 晋升平元年 - The first year of the Jincheng era during the Eastern Jin Dynasty.
    • 任怀仁 - A historical figure.
    • 王祖 - A historical figure.
    • 嘉兴 - A city in Zhejiang Province.
    • 徐祚 - A historical figure.
    • 除服作祭 - A traditional Chinese practice.

    In the first year of the Jincheng era, Ren Huairen was thirteen years old and served as a clerk in the government office. In his hometown, there was Wang Zu, who also served as a clerk and often favored Ren Huairen. By the time Ren Huairen was fifteen or sixteen, he had some unusual ideas. Wang Zu harbored resentment towards Ren Huairen and went to Jiaxing, where he killed Ren Huairen and buried his body in a field behind Xu Zuo's house. One night, Xu Zuo stayed overnight on the fields and suddenly saw a grave. From morning until evening, he shared his meals with the ghost, who called out, "Ghost of the field, come eat with me." At night, the ghost also said, "Come stay with me." This continued for some time. One night, the ghost said, "My family will hold a memorial service tomorrow. The offerings will be very generous. Please come with me." Xu Zuo replied, "I am a living person and should not be seen by you." The ghost promised, "I will make you invisible." Xu Zuo then followed the ghost. After a short journey, they arrived at the ghost's home. The house was full of guests, and the ghost led Xu Zuo to a seat of honor, where they ate and drank together. The entire family wept uncontrollably and could not bear their grief, thinking that the ghost was their son. When they saw Wang Zu, they said, "This is the man who killed me, and I still fear him." The ghost then ran out, and Xu Zuo appeared. The family was greatly shocked and asked Xu Zuo to explain the events. They then followed Xu Zuo to welcome the funeral, and after the funeral, the ghost's appearances ceased. [117]

    临淮朱综遭母难，恒外处住。内有病，因前见，妇曰：“丧礼之重，不烦数还。”综曰：“自荼毒以来，何时至内？”妇曰：“君来多矣。”综知是魅，敕妇婢，候来便即闭户执之。及来，登床，往赴视。此物不得去，遽変老白雄鸡。推问是家鸡，杀之，遂绝。
    　　汉武凿昆明，极深，悉是灰墨，无复土。举朝不解，以问东方朔。朔曰：“臣愚，不足以知之。可试问西域胡僧。”帝以朔不知，难以核问。后汉帝时，外国道人来，入洛阳，时有忆方朔言者，乃试问之，胡人云：“经云：‘天地大劫将尽，则劫烧。’此烧之余。”乃知朔言有旨。 [118]

    Relevant Explanation

    Explanation

    [118]

    Idiomatic Expressions

    • 臨淮朱綜遭母難 - Refers to Zhu Zong from Linhuai encountering his mother's death.
    • 恒外处住 - Indicates that Zhu Zong always lived away from home.
    • 内有病 - Refers to his wife's illness.
    • 因前见 - Implies that Zhu Zong had previously seen his wife.
    • 丧礼之重 - Emphasizes the solemnity of the funeral rites.
    • 不烦数还 - Suggests that frequent visits are not necessary.
    • 荼毒以来 - Refers to the period after his mother's death.
    • 何时至内 - A direct question asking when someone came inside.
    • 君来多矣 - A response indicating frequent visits.
    • 综知是魅 - Zhu Zong recognized that his wife was a demon.
    • 敕妇婢 - Zhu Zong ordered his wife's maid.
    • 候来便即闭户执之 - A plan to wait for the demon and then close the door to seize it.
    • 登床，往赴视 - Describes the demon's actions of climbing onto the bed and going to see.
    • 此物不得去 - Indicates that the demon could not leave.
    • 遽変老白雄鸡 - The demon quickly transformed into an old white rooster.
    • 推问是家鸡 - Investigated and found that the demon was a chicken from the house.
    • 杀之，遂绝 - Killed the chicken, thus eliminating the demon.
    • 汉武凿昆明 - Emperor Wu of Han dug the Kunming Lake.
    • 悉是灰墨，无复土 - All was ash and ink, with no more soil.
    • 举朝不解 - The entire court did not understand.
    • 以问东方朔 - Asked Dongfang Shuo for an explanation.
    • 朔曰 - Dongfang Shuo's response.
    • 臣愚，不足以知之 - Dongfang Shuo's modest response indicating his lack of knowledge.
    • 可试问西域胡僧 - Dongfang Shuo's suggestion to ask a Buddhist monk from the Western Regions.
    • 帝以朔不知 - The emperor thought Dongfang Shuo did not know.
    • 后汉帝时 - During the reign of the Later Han emperor.
    • 外国道人来 - A foreign monk came from the Western Regions.
    • 时有忆方朔言者 - Someone remembered Dongfang Shuo's words.
    • 乃试问之 - They tried asking the foreign monk.
    • 胡人云 - The foreign monk's response.
    • 经云 - Refers to Buddhist scriptures.
    • 天地大劫将尽，则劫烧 - When the great calamity of the world is about to end, there will be a conflagration.
    • 此烧之余 - The remnants of this burning.
    • 乃知朔言有旨 - Thus it was known that Dongfang Shuo's words were profound.

    Cultural and Historical Allusions

    • 临淮 - A region in present-day Anhui Province, China.
    • 汉武 - Emperor Wu of Han, a notable emperor of the Han Dynasty.
    • 昆明 - Refers to the Kunming Lake, a large lake in present-day Beijing.
    • 东方朔 - Dongfang Shuo, a famous scholar and court jester of the Han Dynasty.
    • 西域胡僧 - A Buddhist monk from the Western Regions, referring to the areas west of China.
    • 后汉帝 - The emperor of the Later Han Dynasty, a later period in Chinese history.
    • 道人 - A term for a Buddhist monk or Taoist priest.
    • 经 - Refers to Buddhist scriptures, such as the *Tripitaka*.

    Proper Nouns

    • 临淮朱综 - Zhu Zong, a person from the Linhuai region.
    • 汉武 - Emperor Wu of Han.
    • 东方朔 - Dongfang Shuo.
    • 西域胡僧 - A Buddhist monk from the Western Regions.
    • 后汉帝 - The emperor of the Later Han Dynasty.
    • 道人 - A Buddhist monk or Taoist priest.
    • 经 - Buddhist scriptures.

    A man from Linhuai named Zhu Zong encountered the death of his mother and always lived outside. His wife was ill, and because he had previously seen her, she said, "The solemnity of the funeral rites does not require frequent returns." Zhu Zong replied, "Since the time of grief and suffering, when did you come inside?" His wife said, "You have come many times." Zhu Zong knew it was a demon and ordered his wife's maid to wait for him to come and then immediately close the door and seize him. When the demon arrived, it climbed onto the bed and went to see. This creature could not leave and quickly transformed into an old white rooster. It was investigated and found to be a chicken from the house, which was then killed, and thus it was eliminated.

    Emperor Wu of Han dug the Kunming Lake, and it was extremely deep, all filled with ash and ink, with no more soil. The entire court did not understand and asked Dongfang Shuo. Shuo said, "I am foolish, insufficient to know it. You may try asking a Buddhist monk from the Western Regions." The emperor thought Shuo did not know and found it difficult to verify. During the reign of the Later Han emperor, a foreign monk came to Luoyang. At that time, someone remembered Dongfang Shuo's words and tried asking him. The foreign monk said, "The scriptures say, 'When the great calamity of the world is about to end, there will be a conflagration.' This is the remnant of that burning." Thus it was known that Shuo's words were profound. [118]

    蒲城李通，死来云：见沙门法祖为阎罗王讲《首楞严经》。又见道士王浮身被锁械。求祖忏悔，祖不肯赴。孤负圣人，死方思悔。
    　　康阿得死三日，还苏，说：初死时，两人扶腋，有白马吏驱之。不知行几里，见北向黑暗门；南入，见东向黑门；西入，见南向黑门；北入，见有十余梁间瓦屋。有人皂服笼冠，边有三十余吏，皆言府君，西南复有四五十吏。阿得便前拜府君。府君问：“何所奉事？”得曰：“家起佛图塔寺，供养道人。”府君曰：“卿大福德。”问都录使者：“此人命尽耶？”见持一卷书伏地案之，其字甚细，曰：“余算三十五年。”府君大怒曰：“小吏何敢顿夺人命？”便缚白马吏着柱，处罚一百，血出流漫。问得：“欲归不？”得曰：“尔。”府君曰：“今当送卿归，欲便遣卿案行地狱。”即给马一匹，及一从人。 [119]

    Relevant Explanation

    Explanation

    [119]

    Idiomatic Expressions

    • 死来云 - A phrase meaning 'upon death, he said' or 'after death, it was reported that,' indicating a posthumous account.
    • 孤负圣人 - Meaning 'to betray the sage' or 'to disappoint the wise.'
    • 死方思悔 - Meaning 'only thinking of repentance at the moment of death.'
    • 欲归不 - Meaning 'do you want to return?'
    • 案行地狱 - Meaning 'to inspect hell.'
    • 欲便遣卿案行地狱 - Meaning 'I will send you to inspect hell.'

    Cultural and Historical Allusions

    • 沙门法祖 - Likely refers to a Buddhist monk named Fǎzǔ, possibly a historical or legendary figure.
    • 阎罗王 - Yama, the king of hell in Buddhist and Hindu traditions, often depicted as a judge of the dead.
    • 首楞严经 - A significant Mahayana Buddhist scripture, known for its profound philosophical content.
    • 道士王浮 - Wang Fu, a historical figure from the late Western Jin dynasty, known for his involvement in debates between Buddhism and Taoism.
    • 府君 - A title for a local magistrate or official, often used in classical Chinese literature.
    • 都录使者 - A high-ranking official responsible for recording and managing various administrative tasks.

    Proper Nouns

    • 康阿得 - A proper name, possibly of a historical figure or a fictional character in the context of the story.
    • 府君 - A title for a local magistrate.
    • 都录使者 - A high-ranking official.

    Pucheng's Li Tong, upon his death, reported: He saw the Buddhist monk Fǎzǔ teaching the Shǒuléngyán Jīng (Mahāprajñāpāramitā Sūtra) to Yánluó Wáng (Yama, the king of hell). He also saw the Taoist priest Wang Fu with shackles on him. Li Tong asked Fǎzǔ to accept his repentance, but Fǎzǔ refused. He had betrayed the sage, only thinking of repentance at the moment of death.

    Kang Adé died and came back to life after three days, recounting: At the time of his death, two people supported his armpits, and a white horse official led him. He did not know how far they traveled, but he saw a dark gate facing north; entering from the south, he saw a black gate facing east; entering from the west, he saw a black gate facing south; entering from the north, he saw a tile-roofed house with several beams. There was a man in a black robe with a conical hat, surrounded by more than thirty officials, all calling him "Fǔjūn" (magistrate). To the southwest, there were another forty or fifty officials. Kang Adé then approached and bowed to the magistrate. The magistrate asked, "What do you serve?" Kang Adé replied, "My family built a pagoda and temple, and I supported Taoist priests." The magistrate said, "You have great merit and virtue." He then asked the chief recorder, "Has this person's life ended?" He saw the recorder holding a scroll and placing it on the ground, the writing very fine, saying, "His remaining lifespan is thirty-five years." The magistrate became very angry, saying, "How dare a low-ranking official suddenly take away someone's life?" He then bound the white horse official to a pillar, punished him with one hundred strokes, and blood flowed out abundantly. He asked Kang Adé, "Do you want to return?" Kang Adé said, "Yes." The magistrate said, "I will now send you back, and I will have you inspect hell." He immediately gave him a horse and an attendant. [119]

    东北出，不知几里，见一城，方数十里，有满城土屋。因见未事佛时亡伯、伯母、亡叔、叔母，皆着杻械，衣裳破坏，身体脓血。复前行，见一城，其中有卧铁床上者，烧床正赤。凡见十狱，各有楚毒。狱名“赤沙”、“黄沙”、“白沙”，如此“七沙”。有刀山剑树，抱赤铜柱。于是便还。复见七八十梁间瓦屋，夹道种槐，云名“福舍”，诸佛弟子住中。福多者上生天，福少者住此舍。遥见大殿二十余梁，有二男子、二妇人从殿上来下，是得事佛后亡伯、伯母、亡叔、叔母。须臾，有一道人来，问得：“识我不？”得曰：“不识。”曰：“汝何以不识我？我共汝作佛图主。”于是遂而忆之，还至府君所，即遣前二人送归，忽便苏活也。 [120]

    Relevant Explanation

    Explanation

    [120]

    Idiomatic Expressions

    • 东北出 - Directional phrase meaning 'go northeast,' indicating the direction of travel.
    • 见一城，方数十里，有满城土屋 - Literally 'see a city, square several tens of miles, has a city full of earthen houses,' describing the size and type of dwellings in the city.
    • 因见未事佛时亡伯、伯母、亡叔、叔母，皆着杻械，衣裳破坏，身体脓血 - Complex sentence describing the vision of deceased relatives (uncle, aunt, uncle, aunt) who are suffering in hell, bound in chains, with torn clothes, and covered in pus and blood.
    • 复前行，见一城，其中有卧铁床上者，烧床正赤 - Complex sentence describing a subsequent vision of a city with people lying on iron beds that are burning red.
    • 凡见十狱，各有楚毒 - Literally 'in total, see ten hells, each with its own punishments,' indicating the variety of torments in the afterlife.
    • 狱名“赤沙”、“黄沙”、“白沙”，如此“七沙” - Describes the names of the hells, specifically mentioning 'Red Sand,' 'Yellow Sand,' 'White Sand,' and so on, up to 'Seven Sands.'
    • 有刀山剑树，抱赤铜柱 - Describes specific tortures, such as knife mountains and sword trees, and holding onto red copper pillars.
    • 于是便还 - Literally 'thus return,' indicating the end of the journey.
    • 复见七八十梁间瓦屋，夹道种槐，云名“福舍”，诸佛弟子住中 - Describes a vision of a place with many tiled houses, lined with willows, called 'Blessed Houses,' where Buddhist disciples live.
    • 福多者上生天，福少者住此舍 - Describes the fate of those with more or less merit, either ascending to heaven or staying in the Blessed Houses.
    • 遥见大殿二十余梁，有二男子、二妇人从殿上来下，是得事佛后亡伯、伯母、亡叔、叔母 - Describes the vision of a grand hall with two men and two women, identified as the deceased relatives who have practiced Buddhism.
    • 须臾，有一道人来，问得：“识我不？”得曰：“不识。”曰：“汝何以不识我？我共汝作佛图主。”于是遂而忆之，还至府君所，即遣前二人送归，忽便苏活也 - Describes the encounter with a monk who reveals his identity and sends the narrator back to life.

    Cultural and Historical Allusions

    • 东北 - Northeast, a direction.
    • 城 - City, a place.
    • 土屋 - Earthen house, a type of dwelling.
    • 未事佛时 - Not having practiced Buddhism, a state of religious practice.
    • 亡伯、伯母、亡叔、叔母 - Deceased relatives (uncle, aunt, uncle, aunt).
    • 杻械 - Chains or shackles, a form of punishment.
    • 衣裳 - Clothes, a type of clothing.
    • 身体脓血 - Body covered in pus and blood, a state of suffering.
    • 十狱 - Ten hells, a concept in Buddhist cosmology.
    • 赤沙、黄沙、白沙 - Red Sand, Yellow Sand, White Sand, names of hells.
    • 刀山剑树 - Knife mountains and sword trees, specific tortures.
    • 抱赤铜柱 - Holding onto red copper pillars, another form of torture.
    • 福舍 - Blessed Houses, a place of refuge.
    • 诸佛弟子 - Buddhist disciples, followers of Buddhism.
    • 大殿 - Grand hall, a place of importance.
    • 道人 - Monk, a religious figure.
    • 佛图主 - Temple master, a high-ranking Buddhist official.

    Proper Nouns

    • 东北 - Northeast, a direction.
    • 城 - City, a place.
    • 土屋 - Earthen house, a type of dwelling.
    • 未事佛时 - Not having practiced Buddhism, a state of religious practice.
    • 亡伯、伯母、亡叔、叔母 - Deceased relatives (uncle, aunt, uncle, aunt).
    • 杻械 - Chains or shackles, a form of punishment.
    • 衣裳 - Clothes, a type of clothing.
    • 身体脓血 - Body covered in pus and blood, a state of suffering.
    • 十狱 - Ten hells, a concept in Buddhist cosmology.
    • 赤沙、黄沙、白沙 - Red Sand, Yellow Sand, White Sand, names of hells.
    • 刀山剑树 - Knife mountains and sword trees, specific tortures.
    • 抱赤铜柱 - Holding onto red copper pillars, another form of torture.
    • 福舍 - Blessed Houses, a place of refuge.
    • 诸佛弟子 - Buddhist disciples, followers of Buddhism.
    • 大殿 - Grand hall, a place of importance.
    • 道人 - Monk, a religious figure.
    • 佛图主 - Temple master, a high-ranking Buddhist official.

    Northeast of the city, I traveled an unknown distance and came upon a vast city, spanning dozens of miles, filled with earthen houses. There, I saw my deceased uncle, aunt, uncle, and aunt who had not practiced Buddhism, all bound in chains, their clothes torn and their bodies covered in pus and blood. Continuing onward, I saw another city where people were lying on iron beds that were burning red. In total, I saw ten hells, each with its own form of punishment. The hells were named "Red Sand," "Yellow Sand," "White Sand," and so on, up to "Seven Sands." There were knife mountains and sword trees, and people holding onto red copper pillars. Thus, I returned. I then saw a place with many tiled houses, lined with willows, called "Blessed Houses," where Buddhist disciples lived. Those with more merit ascended to heaven, while those with less merit stayed in these houses. In the distance, I saw a grand hall with two men and two women coming and going, identified as my deceased relatives who had practiced Buddhism. Shortly after, a monk approached and asked me, "Do you recognize me?" I replied, "I do not." He said, "Why don't you recognize me? I was the temple master with you." At that moment, I remembered him and returned to the palace, where the official immediately sent the two men to escort me back, and I suddenly awoke to life. [120]

    石长和死，四日苏。说：初死时，东南行，见二人治道，恒去和五十步，长和疾行，亦尔。道两边棘刺皆如鹰爪。见人大小群走棘中，如被驱逐，身体破坏，地有凝血。棘中人见长和独行平道，叹息曰：“佛弟子独乐，得行大道中。”前行，见七八十梁瓦屋，中有阁十余，梁上有窗向。有人面辟方三尺，着皂袍，四纵掖，凭向坐，唯衣襟以上见。长和即向拜。人曰：“石贤者来也，一别二十余年。”和曰：“尔。”意中便若忆此时也。有冯翊牧孟承夫妻先死，阁上人曰：“贤者识承不？”长和曰：“识。”阁上人曰：“孟承生时不精进，今恒为我扫地。承妻精进，晏然与官家事。”举手指西南一房，曰：“孟承妻今在中。 [121]

    Relevant Explanation

    Explanation

    [121]

    Idiomatic Expressions

    • 石长和死，四日苏 - Literal phrase meaning 'Shi Changhe died, and four days later he revived.' Sets the stage for a supernatural narrative.
    • 东南行 - Means 'traveling southeast,' indicating the direction of the journey.
    • 治道 - Literally 'repairing the road,' but in this context, it likely refers to preparing or maintaining a path.
    • 棘刺皆如鹰爪 - Vivid description meaning 'thorns and brambles are all like eagle claws,' emphasizing the sharpness and danger of the environment.
    • 独乐 - Means 'enjoyment alone' or 'solitary pleasure,' highlighting the contrast between the individual's experience and the suffering of others.
    • 梁瓦屋 - Refers to 'houses with beams and tiles,' indicating a well-constructed or traditional dwelling.
    • 阁十余 - Means 'ten or more pavilions,' suggesting a complex or multi-story building.
    • 面辟方三尺 - Describes a face that is three feet wide, emphasizing the size and possibly the importance of the figure.
    • 着皂袍 - Means 'wearing a black robe,' indicating the attire of the figure.
    • 四纵掖 - Describes the sleeves of the robe, possibly meaning 'fourfold sleeves' or 'wide sleeves.'
    • 凭向坐 - Means 'leaning against the wall and sitting,' describing the posture of the figure.
    • 贤者识承不 - Rhetorical question meaning 'Do you recognize Cheng?' indicating familiarity or recognition.
    • 晏然与官家事 - Means 'calmly doing official duties,' suggesting a peaceful and orderly state.

    Cultural and Historical Allusions

    • 石长和 - The name of the protagonist, Shi Changhe, who is the subject of the narrative.
    • 佛弟子 - Refers to a follower of Buddhism, indicating the religious context of the story.
    • 冯翊牧孟承 - Refers to Meng Cheng, a historical or legendary figure mentioned in the text.
    • 孟承妻 - Refers to Meng Cheng's wife, who is also mentioned in the narrative.
    • 阁上人 - Refers to a figure on the pavilion, likely a spiritual or authoritative being given the context.

    Proper Nouns

    • 石长和 - The protagonist of the narrative.
    • 冯翊牧孟承 - A historical or legendary figure mentioned in the text.
    • 孟承妻 - The wife of Meng Cheng, also mentioned in the narrative.
    • 阁上人 - A figure on the pavilion, likely a spiritual or authoritative being.

    Shi Changhe died and revived after four days. He recounted: At the time of his death, he traveled southeast, seeing two people repairing the road, always fifty paces away from him. Even when he ran quickly, they maintained the same distance. The thorns and brambles on both sides of the road were sharp like eagle claws. He saw people, both big and small, running through the thorny bushes, as if being driven, with their bodies broken and the ground stained with blood. The people in the thorny bushes saw Shi Changhe walking alone on a smooth path and sighed, saying, "A follower of the Buddha enjoys solitude and can walk on the great path." Continuing forward, he saw houses with beams and tiles, with ten or more pavilions in between, and windows facing outward on the beams. There was a person with a face three feet wide, wearing a black robe with wide sleeves, leaning against the wall and sitting, only the upper part of the robe visible. Shi Changhe immediately bowed. The person said, "The wise man Shi has come, it has been more than twenty years since we last met." Shi Changhe replied, "Yes." In his heart, he seemed to remember this time. Meng Cheng, the magistrate of Fengyi, and his wife had died before, and the person on the pavilion asked, "Do you recognize Cheng?" Shi Changhe said, "Yes." The person on the pavilion said, "Meng Cheng was not diligent in life, now he always sweeps the ground for me. His wife was diligent and calmly performs official duties." Pointing to a room in the southwest, he said, "Meng Cheng's wife is now inside." [121]

    妻即开窗向，见长和，问：“石贤者何时来？”遍问其家中儿女大小名字平安不，“还时过此，当因一封书”。斯须，见承阁西头来，一手捉扫帚粪箕，一手捉把箉，亦问家消息。阁上人曰：“闻鱼龙超修精进，为信尔不？何所修行？”长和曰：“不食鱼肉，酒不经口，恒转尊经，救诸疾痛。”阁上人曰：“所传莫妄！”阁上问都录主者：“石贤者命尽耶？枉夺其命耶？”主者报：“按录余四十年。”阁上人敕主者：“■〈车卖〉车一乘，两辟车骑，两吏送石贤者。”须臾，东向便有车骑人从如所差之数。长和拜辞，上车而归。前所行道边，所在有亭传、吏民、床坐饮食之具。倏然归家，前见父母坐其尸边。见尸大如牛，闻尸臭。不欲入其中，绕尸三匝，长和叹息，当尸头前。见其亡姊于后推之，便踣尸面上，因即苏。 [122]

    Relevant Explanation

    Explanation

    [122]

    Idiomatic Expressions

    • 妻即开窗向 - Ambiguous phrase; could mean 'the wife immediately opened the window to look out' or 'to greet him'.
    • 遍问其家中儿女大小名字平安不 - Elliptical phrase; means 'she asked about the safety and well-being of all her family members, both young and old'.
    • 还时过此，当因一封书 - Ambiguous phrase; could mean 'when you return, pass by here and leave a letter' or 'stop by and bring a letter'.
    • 亦问家消息 - Means 'she also asked about family news'.
    • 阁上人曰 - Refers to a person in a high position or a deity, possibly a guardian or a spiritual figure.
    • 闻鱼龙超修精进，为信尔不？何所修行？ - Questions about the spiritual progress and practices of '鱼龙', a mythical creature or a person with extraordinary abilities.
    • 长和曰 - Name of the person being questioned in a spiritual or religious setting.
    • 不食鱼肉，酒不经口，恒转尊经，救诸疾痛 - Descriptions of the spiritual practices of '长和', including abstaining from meat and alcohol, constantly reciting sacred texts, and helping those in pain.
    • 阁上人曰 - Response from the person in a high position, suggesting a spiritual or religious context.
    • 所传莫妄 - Means 'what has been transmitted is not false', common in Chinese religious and philosophical texts.
    • 阁上问都录主者 - Refers to the person in a high position and the recorder or registrar, suggesting a bureaucratic or administrative context.
    • 石贤者命尽耶？枉夺其命耶？ - Questions about whether '石贤者' has reached the end of his life or if his life has been unjustly taken, suggesting a spiritual or religious context.
    • 主者报 - Refers to the recorder or registrar, suggesting a bureaucratic or administrative context.
    • 按录余四十年 - Means 'according to the record, he has forty more years left', suggesting a spiritual or religious context.
    • 阁上人敕主者 - Refers to the person in a high position instructing the recorder, suggesting a bureaucratic or administrative context.
    • ■〈车卖〉车一乘，两辟车骑，两吏送石贤者 - Suggests a grand procession, common in Chinese literature and mythology.
    • 须臾，东向便有车骑人从如所差之数 - Suggests a sudden appearance of carriages and escorts, common in Chinese literature and mythology.
    • 长和拜辞，上车而归 - Suggests a formal farewell, common in Chinese literature and mythology.
    • 前所行道边，所在有亭传、吏民、床坐饮食之具 - Suggests a well-prepared journey, common in Chinese literature and mythology.
    • 倏然归家 - Suggests a sudden return, common in Chinese literature and mythology.
    • 前见父母坐其尸边 - Suggests a scene of death and mourning, common in Chinese literature and mythology.
    • 见尸大如牛，闻尸臭 - Suggests a scene of decay, common in Chinese literature and mythology.
    • 不欲入其中，绕尸三匝，长和叹息，当尸头前 - Suggests a scene of grief and reflection, common in Chinese literature and mythology.
    • 见其亡姊于后推之，便踣尸面上，因即苏 - Suggests a supernatural event, common in Chinese literature and mythology.

    Cultural and Historical Allusions

    • 妻即开窗向 - Suggests a scene of greeting or looking out, common in classical Chinese literature.
    • 遍问其家中儿女大小名字平安不 - Reflects the importance of family and concern for family well-being in Chinese culture.
    • 还时过此，当因一封书 - Suggests a custom of leaving a letter or message when passing by a place, common in Chinese culture.
    • 亦问家消息 - Reflects the importance of family news and communication in Chinese culture.
    • 阁上人曰 - Refers to a person in a high position or a deity, possibly a guardian or a spiritual figure, common in Chinese mythology and literature.
    • 闻鱼龙超修精进，为信尔不？何所修行？ - Questions about the spiritual progress and practices of '鱼龙', a mythical creature or a person with extraordinary abilities, common in Chinese mythology.
    • 长和曰 - Name of the person being questioned in a spiritual or religious setting.
    • 不食鱼肉，酒不经口，恒转尊经，救诸疾痛 - Descriptions of the spiritual practices of '长和', including abstaining from meat and alcohol, constantly reciting sacred texts, and helping those in pain, common in Chinese religious practices.
    • 阁上人曰 - Response from the person in a high position, suggesting a spiritual or religious context.
    • 所传莫妄 - Means 'what has been transmitted is not false', common in Chinese religious and philosophical texts.
    • 阁上问都录主者 - Refers to the person in a high position and the recorder or registrar, suggesting a bureaucratic or administrative context.
    • 石贤者命尽耶？枉夺其命耶？ - Questions about whether '石贤者' has reached the end of his life or if his life has been unjustly taken, suggesting a spiritual or religious context.
    • 主者报 - Refers to the recorder or registrar, suggesting a bureaucratic or administrative context.
    • 按录余四十年 - Means 'according to the record, he has forty more years left', suggesting a spiritual or religious context.
    • 阁上人敕主者 - Refers to the person in a high position instructing the recorder, suggesting a bureaucratic or administrative context.
    • ■〈车卖〉车一乘，两辟车骑，两吏送石贤者 - Suggests a grand procession, common in Chinese literature and mythology.
    • 须臾，东向便有车骑人从如所差之数 - Suggests a sudden appearance of carriages and escorts, common in Chinese literature and mythology.
    • 长和拜辞，上车而归 - Suggests a formal farewell, common in Chinese literature and mythology.
    • 前所行道边，所在有亭传、吏民、床坐饮食之具 - Suggests a well-prepared journey, common in Chinese literature and mythology.
    • 倏然归家 - Suggests a sudden return, common in Chinese literature and mythology.
    • 前见父母坐其尸边 - Suggests a scene of death and mourning, common in Chinese literature and mythology.
    • 见尸大如牛，闻尸臭 - Suggests a scene of decay, common in Chinese literature and mythology.
    • 不欲入其中，绕尸三匝，长和叹息，当尸头前 - Suggests a scene of grief and reflection, common in Chinese literature and mythology.
    • 见其亡姊于后推之，便踣尸面上，因即苏 - Suggests a supernatural event, common in Chinese literature and mythology.

    The wife immediately opened the window and saw Changhe, asking, "When will Shi the Sage arrive?" She then inquired about the safety and well-being of all her family members, both young and old. "When you return, pass by here and leave a letter," she said. Soon after, she saw a person coming from the western side of the hall, holding a broom and a dustpan in one hand and a brush in the other, also asking about family news. The person in the upper hall said, "Have you heard that Yu Long has made great spiritual progress? Do you believe this? What practices is he following?" Changhe replied, "He does not eat fish or meat, does not drink alcohol, constantly recites sacred texts, and helps those in pain." The person in the upper hall said, "What has been transmitted is not false!" The person in the upper hall then asked the recorder, "Has Shi the Sage reached the end of his life? Has his life been unjustly taken?" The recorder replied, "According to the record, he has forty more years left." The person in the upper hall then instructed the recorder, "Send one carriage, two escorts, and two officials to accompany Shi the Sage." Soon, from the east, there appeared the number of carriages and escorts as instructed. Changhe bowed and took his leave, then got on the carriage and returned home. Along the road he had traveled, there were inns, officials, and facilities for rest and food. He suddenly returned home and saw his parents sitting beside his body. He saw the body as large as a cow and smelled the odor of the body. He did not want to enter, so he circled the body three times, sighed, and stood in front of the body's head. He saw his deceased sister push him from behind, and he fell face down on the body, then suddenly came to. [122]

    妻即开窗向，见长和，问：“石贤者何时来？”遍问其家中儿女大小名字平安不，“还时过此，当因一封书”。斯须，见承阁西头来，一手捉扫帚粪箕，一手捉把箉，亦问家消息。阁上人曰：“闻鱼龙超修精进，为信尔不？何所修行？”长和曰：“不食鱼肉，酒不经口，恒转尊经，救诸疾痛。”阁上人曰：“所传莫妄！”阁上问都录主者：“石贤者命尽耶？枉夺其命耶？”主者报：“按录余四十年。”阁上人敕主者：“■〈车卖〉车一乘，两辟车骑，两吏送石贤者。”须臾，东向便有车骑人从如所差之数。长和拜辞，上车而归。前所行道边，所在有亭传、吏民、床坐饮食之具。倏然归家，前见父母坐其尸边。见尸大如牛，闻尸臭。不欲入其中，绕尸三匝，长和叹息，当尸头前。见其亡姊于后推之，便踣尸面上，因即苏。

    The wife immediately opened the window and saw Changhe, asking, "When will Shi the Sage arrive?" She then inquired about the safety and well-being of all her family members, both young and old. "When you return, pass by here and leave a letter," she said. Soon after, she saw a person coming from the western side of the hall, holding a broom and a dustpan in one hand and a brush in the other, also asking about family news. The person in the upper hall said, "Have you heard that Yu Long has made great spiritual progress? Do you believe this? What practices is he following?" Changhe replied, "He does not eat fish or meat, does not drink alcohol, constantly recites sacred texts, and helps those in pain." The person in the upper hall said, "What has been transmitted is not false!" The person in the upper hall then asked the recorder, "Has Shi the Sage reached the end of his life? Has his life been unjustly taken?" The recorder replied, "According to the record, he has forty more years left." The person in the upper hall then instructed the recorder, "Send one carriage, two escorts, and two officials to accompany Shi the Sage." Soon, from the east, there appeared the number of carriages and escorts as instructed. Changhe bowed and took his leave, then got on the carriage and returned home. Along the road he had traveled, there were inns, officials, and facilities for rest and food. He suddenly returned home and saw his parents sitting beside his body. He saw the body as large as a cow and smelled the odor of the body. He did not want to enter, so he circled the body three times, sighed, and stood in front of the body's head. He saw his deceased sister push him from behind, and he fell face down on the body, then suddenly came to.
